paragraphs.text: In law, the word “property” embraces both tangible and intangible property and may refer to either Real Property and/or Personal Property. Tangible property is something one can touch, e.g. a building. Intangible property has no physical existence; e.g. goodwill, patents, copyrights, trademarks, etc. Real Property refers to land and things permanently attached to the land, thus the term “realty”; while Personal Property generally refers to everything else, the items which are moveable and not part of the land.

In the context of Property Insurance, the word “property” covers every material thing or physical object which may suffer an unexpected loss or damage by an insured peril. The term “property” includes:
▪ Moveable Property, such as household contents, cash, jewellery, silverware, and cargo being shipped or transported; and
▪ Immoveable Property, such as buildings, land, swimming pools, and tennis courts.
The basic Fire Insurance policy provides indemnity to the insured in the event of any damage to the property caused by certain perils, with obviously the primary peril being fire.

In Property Insurance, it is imperative to clearly identify and state the subject matter of the intended insurance. The subject matter of insurance is one of the key determinants to the risk assessment – whether to accept, decline and ultimately the determination of its terms, pricing and formation of the insurance contract.

For the purpose of identification, such description must be an adequate description of the subject matter. This is because insurers do not, in their policies, undertake to indemnify the insured against loss by fire, but only against loss arising from the destruction of the specific object insured. In order to recover under the policy, the insured must show that the property contemplated by the policy is the subject matter of insurance, and that it answers the description of the subject matter contained in the policy under which the claim is made.

These properties as mentioned above are subject to fire risk. Normally, a Fire Insurance policy is underwritten on the basis of separate sums insured for each of the above items. The main headings under which the property is insured are always reflected in the schedule of the policy, and a list of such items can run to many pages, depending on the size of the insured company.

A Fire Insurance policy insures property against loss or damage caused by certain perils. The policy covers loss of or damage by fire, lightning or explosion of gas used for lighting or domestic purposes (FLEX Cover).
Fire Insurance policies provide cover for any property loss or damage by the insured perils (i.e. FLEX), which occurs during the period of insurance (usually 12 months).

A couple of the common provisions and conditions which are specific to a Fire Insurance policy are described below: (a) Fall Or Displacement Under this condition, the insurance ceases to be effective immediately upon any fall or displacement to the building impairing its usefulness, or subjects it to increased risk of fire. However, if the fall or displacement was consequent upon the fire of any of the property insured, then, this condition would not apply; i.e. the insurance would remain effective. (b) Marine Exclusion This condition excludes liability altogether if the damaged property is also covered by a Marine Insurance policy. The main reason for this exclusion is the potential disputes and difficulties that may arise in event of claim, to determine the satisfactory contribution between Fire Insurance and Marine Insurance policies. The range, extent and conditions of cover may vary considerably. For example, there is the possibility that, before the fire, the goods may have sustained undiscovered and undefined damage by sea water, pilferage or any of the other marine perils. It would have been unfair for the fire insurers to bear such losses.

Note that detailed discussions on the policy provisions and conditions common to most general insurance policies are in the “Basic Insurance Concepts & Principles” Study Guide, published by the Singapore College of Insurance Limited.
Note that the underwriter may require additional information, and that details of the required information may differ from insurer to insurer.

The Fire Insurance policy has several exclusions (perils and properties that are not covered by the policy). The main reasons for having exclusions are to enable insurers to avoid paying for catastrophes (for which no cover is ever intended to be provided). Insurers also do not wish to cover property in which its value may be difficult to assess, as well as risks that call for special underwriting considerations, and/or should be more appropriately covered under other policies.

Other than the usual exclusions that relate to war, nuclear, strike, riot and civil commotion (SRCC) risks, the other common exclusions specific to this class of insurance are as follows:
▪ loss by theft during or after the occurrence of a fire;
▪ loss of or damage to property caused by its own fermentation, natural heating or spontaneous combustion, or by its undergoing any heating or drying process. Although the property undergoing fermentation in itself is not covered, any resulting fire damage to other insured property is covered, e.g. a sack of palm oil seeds catches fire on their own spontaneous heating – the damage to the oil seeds itself is not covered, but if the fire spreads and causes damage to nearby property, that property damaged by the fire spread is covered; as well as
▪ loss or damage caused by: - the burning of property by order of any public authority; and - subterranean fire.

If the damage occurs from the excluded perils that do not involve fire (e.g. a typhoon blows down a building), the policy also does not cover as such, since this is a Fire Insurance policy covering only fire and lightning damage. Any other perils not specifically excluded here, which results in fire damage, are deemed to be included.
Note that the exclusions for a Fire Insurance policy vary from insurer to insurer.

Special Perils (also known as Extraneous Perils or Extended Perils or Extra Perils) are, as the name suggests, additions to the standard cover and are available as an option. These are normally subject to extra premiums payable.

These special perils are commonly classified under the following headings: Classification Of Special Perils Examples 1. Perils of a Chemical Nature explosion, spontaneous fermentation, heating or combustion 2. Social Perils riots and strikes, locked-out workers, persons taking part in labour disturbances and malicious damage 3. Perils of Nature earthquake and volcanic eruption, subsidence, hurricane, cyclone, typhoon, windstorm and flood 4. Miscellaneous Perils sprinkler leakage, bursting or overflowing of pipes or water tanks, smoke damage, impact by road vehicles and aircraft
Other common clauses that provide coverage extensions following a covered fire may include Architects, Engineers and Surveyor’s Fees; Removal of Debris; and Public Authorities’ Requirements.

Most policies will contain some conditions specifying the claim procedures in the event of a loss. The purpose is to involve the insurer as soon as possible, so that the insurer can help to mitigate or verify the extent of the loss, and/or to seek the insured’s cooperation in matters such as providing the necessary claim evidence.
No claim shall be payable under this policy, unless the terms of this condition have been complied with.

The request for a declaration on oath (or in other legal form) of the truth relating to the claim or any information submitted is seldom called for, as it is only required if the insurer finds that the claim is highly suspicious.

A basic Theft (or Burglary) Insurance policy insures: “… loss or destruction or damage to property insured as a result of:
▪ theft or attempted theft involving forcible and violent entry to or exit from the premises, or
▪ theft following assault or violence or threat thereof to the insured or any director, partner or employee of the insured …”

Under Section 378 of the Singapore Penal Code 1871, the legal definition of the term “theft” states that: “Whoever, intending to take dishonestly any movable property out of the possession of any person without that person’s consent, moves that property in order to such taking, is said to commit theft.”

However, this is a very wide definition, as it does not state the need for an existence of force or violence in committing that act of theft. Simply, it indicates that shoplifting and pilferage are also considered as acts of thefts. However, note that these two acts are not insurable owing to the high risk of easy accomplishment and frequency of occurrences. For this reason, most basic Theft Insurance policies require the evidence of forcible and violent entry to or exit from the insured premises.
Theft Insurance policies have the same aim as that of standard Fire Insurance policies in that they intend to provide compensation to the insured in the event of loss of the property insured.

The common descriptions of the property insured are:
▪ stock-in-trade;
▪ goods held in trust or on commission; and
▪ plant, machinery, office equipment, furniture, fixtures and fittings.

Theft Insurance policy will usually include cover for damage to buildings arising from a theft or attempted theft; e.g. damage to any windows, doors, wall, roof, shutters, locks as a result of the entry into and/or exit from the buildings. This is provided that the insured is not responsible for causing the damage.

Note that theft from domestic premises is usually not insured under a commercial Theft Insurance policy but rather, as part of Householder’s Insurance1. 1 Detailed discussions on the Household Insurance are in the “Personal General Insurance” Study Guide, published by the Singapore College of Insurance Limited.

Basically, the insurer’s intention in issuing a Theft Insurance policy is to cover the loss of property resulting from a break-in. Hence, a typical Theft Insurance policy incorporates the following wording: “theft involving entry to or exit from the premises by forcible and violent means”.

The intention of the cover is that “theft” should be accompanied by either forcible and violent entry or exit from a building. “Forcible” on its own means “the mere use of any kind of force”. “Violent” has the notion of aggression and extra force with accompanying damage.
Theft Insurance policies provide cover for any property loss or damage by the insured perils (i.e. theft), which occurs during the period of insurance (usually 12 months).

Note that detailed discussions on the policy provisions and conditions common to most general insurance policies are in the “Basic Insurance Concepts & Principles” Study Guide, published by the Singapore College of Insurance Limited.
Shops dealing in wine and spirits, expensive dried foodstuff, such as bird’s nest, shark fins and abalone are of particular attraction to thieves.

A poor security system in the insured premises can increase the risk of theft as well. Many insurers are concerned about this physical aspect of risk, and very often, risk surveyors are engaged to investigate the effectiveness of the insured’s security system. If the insurer is not satisfied with the security precautions that are in place at the point of application, the insured may be requested to upgrade the security system before the risk can be accepted.
The rate of premium varies with the degree of theft hazard. Naturally, the higher the risk associated with the value of the goods, the higher will be the rate of premium.
Note that the underwriter may require additional information, and that details of required information may differ from insurer to insurer.
Note that the exclusions of a Theft Insurance policy vary from insurer to insurer.

Some common extensions specific to Theft Insurance are described below: (a) Robbery and/or Hold-up A robbery and/or hold-up can be defined as theft accompanied by assault or violence, or the threat of it to the insured or his employees, irrespective of whether there was forcible entry. (b) Full Theft Cover This extension covers loss of or damage to the property insured by theft without violent and forcible entry into the premises. Hence, if the loss occurred when entry/or exit is achieved via an open door and/or window, the loss will be covered.

However, the Full Theft extension does not cover any of the following:
▪ unexplained loss, mysterious disappearance or loss or shortage while taking inventory or stock;
▪ loss or damage from infidelity or dishonest acts of the insured or his employees or agents; or
▪ the first amount of loss shown in the schedule of the policy as the excess.

In Theft Insurance, an insured may insure on a First Loss basis. The insured may feel that any loss or damage is unlikely to affect all his property at once, and so he agrees to a limit on the insurer's liability on any single claim to an agreed amount. For example, if the insured’s stocks of laptops are valued at S$500,000, and the value is spread throughout a warehouse, it may be considered unlikely that any theft will cause a claim greater than S$100,000. Therefore, the insured arranges Theft Insurance on the basis of losses up to S$100,000. In other words, the S$100,000 sum insured is on a First Loss basis.

The above arrangement means that, in the event where all the stock is stolen, the insurer will pay only a maximum of S$100,000 for items worth S$500,000. In other words, the insurer’s liability for the theft loss is limited to the First Loss sum insured, notwithstanding that the actual loss amount may be much greater. In consideration, the insured achieves savings in premium by insuring on a First Loss basis. The condition of average (under-insurance) does not apply in this case.

Upon the happening of any event that may give rise to a claim under the policy, the insured must:
▪ give immediate notice to the police, and take all practical steps to discover the guilty person(s) and to recover the property lost or stolen; and
▪ report within seven days (or 14 days for some insurers) of the event supplying detailed particulars, proof and evidence in support of the claim.

In the event of a claim being made against the policy, the insured must:
▪ provide evidence to prove that the loss or damage claimed for has actually arisen from one of the causes insured against, and that the property in respect of which a claim is made is not merely mislaid or missing;
▪ give all reasonable assistance to the insurer who may at any time at its own expense use all legal means in the name of the insured for recovery of any of the property lost or stolen; and
▪ not abandon any property to the insurer, although the insurer can be entitled to any property for the loss of which a claim is paid under the policy.

An Electronic Equipment Insurance policy (also known as Computer Loss or Damage/Data Processing Equipment Insurance policy) provides an “All Risks” cover for damage to electronic equipment, such as computers and computer related peripherals, data processing equipment, auxiliary equipment, such as uninterrupted power supplies (UPS), air-conditioning system, in the computer or electronic room, data carrying media, telecommunication systems and printing equipment. It can also be extended to cover the cost of reconstructing data files and programs, as well as additional costs incurred when making use of alternative facilities to avoid business interruption after physical loss to insured items.

Such electronic equipment may comprise data processing systems used in research and development, commercial computer centres, administration and industry, medical equipment for diagnostic and therapeutic purposes, telecommunication systems, etc.

subsections.label: B
subsections.title: The Need For Electronic Equipment Insurance

paragraphs.text: Modern businesses have become more reliant on computers in some form or another. Computers and electronic equipment often entail huge capital investment and are critical to the operation. The equipment is susceptible to damage and a breakdown/disruption of any form can cause a severe impact on the business. For instance, even a partial failure to a computer installation may have far-reaching and damaging effects on the business, as a computer installation is usually the nerve centre of an organisation. Hence, it is of utmost importance that a business purchases adequate insurance cover for the electronic equipment.

Electronic Equipment Insurance is designed to provide an “All Risks” cover catering to the actual repair and/or replacement cost and other financial consequences, following the loss of or damage to the insured electronic equipment (hardware and software2), arising from any accidental cause. “All Risks” means all unforeseen and sudden damage/loss whatsoever, other than those specifically excluded, are included for coverage. 2 Hardware is any electronic, mechanical or electromechanical device, e.g. CPU, disk drive and printer. Software is a set of instructions that tells the computer what to do, e.g. word-processing software. The Electronic Equipment Insurance insures the hardware, as well as the software.

In other words, the basic Electronic Equipment Insurance policy insures against any sudden and unforeseen physical loss or damage to any part of the insured items, from any cause whatsoever other than those specifically excluded. The insurers will pay for all such loss or damage up to an amount not exceeding the sum insured.
Electronic Equipment Insurance policies provide cover for any insured property lost or damaged which occurs during the period of insurance (usually 12 months).

Some of the common provisions and conditions which are specific to an Electronic Equipment Insurance policy are described below: (a) Sum Insured It is a common provision that the sum insured must represent the cost of replacing the insured item with new items of the same kind and capacity, i.e. replacement costs, including freight, installation costs and any customs duties and dues. Otherwise, the claim payment shall be subject to average if the sum insured is less than the insured amount required. (b) Basis Of Indemnity Where damage to an insured item can be repaired (i.e. partial loss), the insurer will pay all expenses necessarily and reasonably incurred to repair the equipment, including other charges like dismantling, transport fees, etc. For loss to equipment that is beyond economic repair (i.e. total loss), or if the repair cost equals or exceeds the actual value of the insured item immediately before the occurrence of the damage, the settlement shall be made on the following basis:
▪ pay the actual value of the item immediately before the occurrence of the loss, i.e. replacement value less depreciation (replacement value will include costs for ordinary freight, installation and customs duties and dues, if any); or
▪ if the policy was extended to insure the full replacement value of the items, pay the replacement value without deduction for depreciation.

The customarily required underwriting information is described below: (a) General information – This comprises details like name of proposer, address and location of risk, nature of business and the required period of insurance. (b) Declaration of the interests to be insured under the three sections:
▪ Section 1: Description of electronic equipment to be insured, such as model and type of equipment, and the sums insured.
▪ Section 2: Description of data media to be insured, such as types of data media, and the sums insured. These may comprise detachable optical, magnetic and electronic storage media.
▪ Section 3: Extra Expenses, such as rental fees for replacement equipment. (c) Risk-specific information – This includes questions on claims and insurance history, any other insurance covering the same interest, any maintenance contract, any risk aggravating circumstances, etc.
Note that the underwriter may require additional information, and that details of the required information may differ from insurer to insurer.
Note that the exclusions of an Electronic Equipment Insurance policy vary from insurer to insurer.

Some extensions specific to the Electronic Equipment Insurance policy are described below: (a) External Data Media This extends the coverage to any data processing media that is insured, for any sudden and unforeseen loss of or damage to any part of the electronic data processing media as described in the schedule of the policy, from any cause whatsoever, other than those specifically excepted. The exceptions are:
▪ the deductible as stated in the schedule of the policy to be borne by the insured in any one occurrence;
▪ any cost arising from false programming, punching, labelling or inserting, unintentional cancelling of information or discarding of data media, and from loss of information caused by magnetic fields; and
▪ consequential loss of any kind. (b) Increased Cost Of Working This extension insures the additional expenditure incurred for the use of a substitute system to maintain normal business operations during the interruption, caused by any loss of or damage to the insured electronic equipment. However, the insurer shall not be liable for any additional expenses incurred by the insured as a result of:
▪ restrictions imposed by any government or public authority concerning the reconstruction of operation of the electronic data processing equipment; and
▪ the repairs or replacements of the damaged/destroyed equipment being delayed because of the insured not having the necessary funds to effect them.
In addition, excluded perils in the basic policy like earthquake, strike, riot, civil commotion, hurricane, typhoon, etc. may also be “brought back” or included with additional premiums payable.

Glass Insurance is commonly known as Plate Glass Insurance in the market, but this policy is not restricted only to coverage of only plate glass. Glass Insurance policy is issued to cover practically all kinds of fixed glass within the insured’s premises, as reflected in the general description as specified in the schedule of the policy, e.g. “All glass panels, including fittings and fronts contained in or fixed on the insured’s premises.”

Although there are many ways in which glass can be damaged without breakage occurring, Glass Insurance covers only the breakage (or destruction) risk. There is, however, an almost unlimited number of breakage causes. Therefore, this insurance is subject to certain exclusions.

Glass is an integral part of any building nowadays. It is also common to see buildings with “all-glass” structures. Within the building structure, glass is found in most offices, such as glass partitions and glass doors. Glass of many different types is subject to breakage by a variety of causes, which can result in hefty replacement costs.

The Glass Insurance policy insures the glass (including any lettering or logo on the glass) as described in the schedule of the policy and fixed in the insured’s premises, against the destruction or breakage. The insurer shall at its option: (a) replace the broken glass according to the description in the schedule of the policy; or (b) pay the sum equivalent to the value of the broken glass at the time of the destruction or breakage up to, but not exceeding, the value as specified against each item in the schedule of the policy.
The replacement value of the glass normally excludes the cost of removal, alteration or replacement of any fittings, fixtures, frames or framework necessary for the reinstatement of the broken glass.
However, it should be noted that “breakage” does not include scratching or chipping.
A Glass Insurance policy provides cover for the insured glass against breakage occurring during the period of insurance (usually 12 months).

Note that detailed discussions on the policy provisions and conditions common to most general insurance policies are in the “Basic Insurance Concepts & Principles” Study Guide, published by the Singapore College of Insurance Limited.

The customarily required underwriting information is described below: (a) Situation The location of the risk will indicate if there are any adverse features, such as risks located on busy streets and street corners, where there is a greater risk of damage by vehicles or stones thrown up by passing cars. On the other hand, the situation may also show that the premises have better than average protection in that they may be situated in a shopping centre which is closed to the public after business hours. (b) Occupation Obviously, there are some occupations which are more attractive to thieves than others, these raise the risk of smash-and-grab burglaries, such as jewellers and liquor stores. These risks can be reduced in some instances by the removal of goods from the window display after hours, and/or the installation of steel mesh behind the glass. (c) Additional Perils There are some risks that are more susceptible to additional perils, such as proximity to explosive manufacturers. (d) Loss History Questions like previous occurrence of breakages, causes of breakages, declination of risk and previous claims history will provide an indication of any moral hazards and/or adverse features, like disgruntled tenants or customers. (e) Type, Size And Thickness Of Glass All these factors indicate the probability of breakage of the glass.
Note that the underwriter may require additional information, and that details of the required information may differ from insurer to insurer.
Note that the exclusions of a Glass Insurance policy vary from insurer to insurer.

The insured must give immediate notice in writing to the insurer on the happening of any event that gives rise to a claim under the policy, stating the full circumstances of the case, and providing evidence as may be required. Unless the insurer receives such notice within a stipulated time, the insurer shall not be liable for the claim. The insured, at his own expense, must also take all feasible steps to ascertain the person who is liable for the breakage.

subsections.label: A
subsections.title: What Is Commercial Special Risks Insurance?

paragraphs.text: The policy is designed to cover items, such as machinery, equipment and other contents and at one time used to be commonly called an “All Risks” Insurance policy.

The term “All Risks” Insurance is, unfortunately, a misnomer, because it does not cover every conceivable risk of loss or damage as its name implies. Consequently, it is more common these days to refer to it as “Commercial Special Risks” Insurance. The intention of the insurer is to cover anything that is not expressly excluded under the policy, unlike in a “Specified” or “Named Perils” policy where no risk is insured, unless clearly specified.

subsections.label: B
subsections.title: The Need For Commercial Special Risks Insurance

paragraphs.text: Uncertainty of losses is not restricted to events brought about by fire or theft alone, nor is it limited to events happening on the insured’s premises. This has led to the development of this wider form of previously called “All Risks” cover. The Commercial Special Risks Insurance today represents the widest form of cover available for commercial property.

The Commercial Special Risks Insurance policy states that, if at any time during the period of insurance, the property insured while in the “situation” as described in the schedule of the policy be accidentally, physically lost, destroyed or damaged by any cause other than those excluded, and subject to any other terms and conditions, the insurer agrees to (at its option):
▪ pay the insured the value of the property at the time of its loss or destruction; or
▪ pay the amount of the damage; or
▪ reinstate, repair or replace such property or any part of it.
The “situation” is usually defined as in the insured’s premises.
Any payment by the insurer in respect of any claim will also reduce the sum insured by that amount, for the remaining period of insurance, unless it is reinstated with the agreement of the insurer.

Commercial Special Risk Insurance policies provide cover for the sudden and unforeseen loss or damage of property by any cause (other than those excluded) which occurs during the period of insurance (usually 12 months).

Some of the common provisions and conditions which are specific to a Commercial Special Risks Insurance policy are described below: (a) Alteration Of Risk Unless admitted by the insurer in writing, no claim shall be payable in respect of the insured property should there be, after the commencement of the policy, any alteration:
▪ by removal;
▪ whereby the risk is increased; or
▪ whereby the interest of the insured ceases, except by will or operation of law. (b) Excess The policy does not cover the amount of the excess as stated in the schedule of the policy for each and every loss, as ascertained after the application of all other policy terms and conditions, including the condition of average (under-insurance).

Note that detailed discussions on the policy provisions and conditions common to most general insurance policies are in the “Basic Insurance Concepts & Principles” Study Guide, published by the Singapore College of Insurance Limited.

In addition, “management’s attitude” may also be an important key rating factor. For example, some underwriters believe that if a client is not willing to undertake risk improvements, particularly those relating to housekeeping, it is better to decline the proposal, rather than load the rates or apply an excess. Carelessness and inefficiency in management will be considered a moral hazard.
Note that the underwriter may require additional information, and that details of the required information may differ from insurer to insurer.
Being a “Special Risks” policy, the list of exclusions is of particular importance. The excluded perils are mostly uninsurable, difficult to insure, or commonly insured under other forms.

Other than the usual exclusions that relate to war, nuclear, strike, riot and civil commotion risks, the common exclusions specific to this class of insurance are as follows: (a) loss of or damage to property owing to non-accidental means, such as:
▪ shortage owing to clerical or accounting error and misfiling, or shortage in supply or delivery to or from the insured,
▪ mere disappearance or loss discovered during stock-take, unless proven that such loss being due to theft,
▪ unexplained or mysterious circumstances; (b) “unavoidable” or causes of gradual operating loss, such as scratching, wear and tear, depreciation, moth, mildew, inherent vice, corrosion, rust, dampness, fungus, change in colour, atmospheric conditions, weather (except lightning), etc.; (c) electrical or mechanical breakdown of machinery and equipment; (d) breakage of fragile items, such as glass and china (unless caused by fire or theft); (e) faulty defective design, materials or workmanship; (f) interruption of:
▪ utilities, such as water, gas and electricity supply,
▪ fuel systems; (g) loss or damage arising directly from the property undergoing any process, alteration, repair, restoration, testing, renovating, servicing, installation, removal, dismantling, re-erection; (h) damage or loss caused by or arising from:
▪ pollution or contamination,
▪ wilful act or wilful negligence by the insured or his representative,
▪ cessation of work, delay, lack of performance, loss of market or any other consequential or indirect loss; (i) loss of or damage to property:
▪ in transit,
▪ not contained within an enclosed building,
▪ more specifically insured; and (j) requisition or wilful destruction by order of the government or any public authority.
Note that the exclusions of a Commercial Special Risks Insurance policy may vary from insurer to insurer.

The Industrial “Special Risks” policy provides coverage for any physical accidental loss to property insured at the business premises subject to exclusions. It is a single policy comprising two sections i.e. Section I is the Material Damage section covering accidental and unforeseen damage to the insured’s property at the business premises subject to exclusions while Section II covers loss of income resulting from the disruption of the insured’s business in consequence of an indemnifiable material damage loss under Section I. Section II is also known as the Business Interruption section. The Industrial “Special Risks” policy is commonly used for insuring industrial properties.
Much of the coverage, exclusions, etc. for the Material Damage section is similar to those already described in Commercial Special Risks Insurance.
We shall study Business Interruption Insurance in greater detail in the next chapter of this Study Guide.

There has been an increased awareness of the threat of violent terrorist attacks in the region. The events of 11 September 2001 led to the withdrawal of terrorism cover in many lines of insurance business. While companies in Singapore have not been subject to such attacks, it is prudent for businesses to realise that their operations may be adversely affected by either a direct attack or suffer collateral damage from such an event. In addition, their overseas operations are obviously at risk.

As most property insurance policies would exclude damage caused by terrorism, it is vital for businesses to take out appropriate insurance to provide cover their properties against such risks. A severe terrorist attack could cause irreparable damage to the organisation.

Property Terrorism insurance covers the insured’s property against damage caused by Acts of Terrorism. In particular, it covers the following risks:
▪ Sabotage i.e. a deliberate subversion to cause harm or destruction of property and
▪ Terrorism i.e. the unlawful use of violence against persons or property.

More specifically, Property Terrorism Insurance insures buildings and contents against physical loss or physical damage by an Act or series of Acts of Terrorism and/or Sabotage, occurring during the period of insurance.

Insurers usually define an “Act of Terrorism” as an act, including the use of force or violence, of any person or group(s) of persons, whether acting alone or on behalf of or in connection with any organisation(s), committed for political, religious or ideological purposes including the intention to influence any government and/or to put the public in fear for such purposes.

As for an “Act of Sabotage”, insurers define it as an act or series of acts of deliberate subversion by any person or group(s) of persons, whether acting alone or on behalf of or in connection with any organisation(s), committed for political, religious or ideological purposes including the intention to influence any government and/or to put the public in fear for such purposes. Subversion is the act of trying to destroy or damage an established system or government3.
The reader will note that the intention to influence any government and/or to put the public in fear for such purposes is a common element in both definitions above. 3 Source: dictionary.cambrige.org

A Property Terrorism Insurance policy contains numerous exclusions. Some examples include: (i) All losses or damage caused by nuclear detonation, reaction, nuclear radiation or radioactive contamination. (ii) Loss or damage occasioned directly or indirectly by war, invasion or warlike operations (whether war be declared or not), hostile acts of sovereign or government entities, civil war, rebellion, revolution, insurrection, civil commotion assuming the proportions of or amounting to an uprising, military or usurped power or martial law or confiscation by order of any Government or public authority. (iii) Loss by seizure or illegal occupation. (iv) Loss or damage caused by confiscation, requisition, detention, legal or illegal occupation, embargo, quarantine, or any result of any order of public or government authority which deprives the Assured of the use or value of its property, nor for loss or damage arising from acts of contraband or illegal transportation or illegal trade. (v) Loss or damage directly or indirectly arising from or in consequence of the discharge of pollutants or contaminants, which pollutants and contaminants shall include but not be limited to any solid, liquid, gaseous or thermal irritant, contaminant of toxic or hazardous substance or any substance the presence, existence or release of which endanger the health, safety or welfare of persons or the environment.

As for “occurring during the period of insurance”, insurers define “Occurrence” as any one loss and/or series of losses arising out of and directly occasioned by one Act or series of Acts of Terrorism for the same purpose or cause.

The duration and extent of any one “Occurrence” shall be limited to all losses sustained by the insured at the property insured during any period of 72 consecutive hours arising out of the same purpose or cause. However, insurer also state that no such period of 72 consecutive hours may extend beyond the expiration of the policy unless the insured shall first sustain direct physical damage by an Act of Terrorism prior to the expiration of the policy and within said period of 72 consecutive hours.
Insurers also make it clear that any period of 72 consecutive hours shall not commence prior to the attachment or inception of the policy.

(a) Notification Insurers stipulate that the insured, upon knowledge of any occurrence likely to give rise to a claim, shall give immediate written advice thereof to the insurers. (b) Proof of Loss Insurers require the insured to render a signed and sworn proof of loss within sixty (60) days after the occurrence of a loss (stating the time, place and cause of loss, the interest of the insured and all others in the property, the sound value thereof and the amount of loss or damage thereto. (c) Subrogation If the insurer becomes liable for any payment under the Policy in respect of loss or damage, the Underwriters shall be subrogated, to the extent of such payment, to all the rights and remedies of the insured against any party in respect of such loss or damage and shall be entitled at their own expense to sue in the name of the insured. (d) Salvage and Recoveries If any salvages, recoveries and payments are recovered or received after a loss settlement, they shall be treated as if they have been received before the loss settlement.
Most of the policy conditions are similar to those found in other Property Insurance policies.
Just like most general insurance policies, the insured is required to act as if uninsured.
Insurers require that any protection provided for the safety of the insured property shall be maintained in good order throughout the period of the policy and shall be in use at all relevant times.

In the event of any under-insurance at the time of the loss and where there is a partial loss, the insurer will penalise the insured and pay less than indemnity by applying the average clause in the policy.
This condition states that the insurer shall be permitted but not obligated to inspect the insured’s property at any time.

Insurers will usually require the following information before they can quote for Terrorism Insurance cover:
▪ Details of Insured/Location
▪ Details of Control of Access
▪ Details of Building Precautions
▪ Details of Management/Staff Preparation
▪ Details of Locality − Insurers will be interested to know if any facilities is within say, 500 metres of the building(s) to be insured. Examples of such facilities include Diplomatic Facility or Military Facility.
▪ Details of Threats or Losses − Details of any threats or losses within the last five years.

paragraphs.text:
When an insured peril under a property insurance policy occurs, this can destroy or damage physical properties, such as buildings and contents of the business. In addition to these, there will be the potential loss of intangible assets and other associated financial exposures, such as:
▪ loss of profits, arising from inability to generate any business pending restoration of the damaged property;
▪ increased expenses, arising from the business owner having to contract out to his competitors, in order to meet his contractual obligations to his customers; and
▪ loss of goodwill, arising from loss of customers to his competitors owing to the business owner's inability to meet his contractual obligations.
It is essentially this loss of intangible assets and other associated financial exposures that leads to the need for Business Interruption Insurance.

Business Interruption Insurance is complementary to material damage insurance (e.g. Fire Insurance). Material damage insurance provides the funds to reinstate, replace or repair assets damaged by certain perils, while Business Interruption Insurance provides protection against losses of the immediate and future earning capacity of the business.

Business Interruption Insurance is also known as “Loss of Profits” or “Consequential Loss” Insurance. Income Loss Business Shutdown STORE Direct Loss STORE Directly causes (after loss) also results in INCOME (before loss)

sections.title: The Need For Business Interruption Insurance

paragraphs.text: It will be prudent for all businesses to always consider Business Interruption Insurance; regardless whether they be a large corporation or small enterprise, manufacturing or non-manufacturing concern, retail, or financial institution.

Suppose a business sustains a serious fire damage to its property; the site must first be cleared of its damaged debris, an architect must be employed, plans must be drawn, planning permission must be sought, and contractors must be employed to repair or reconstruct the building. Similarly, some machinery and equipment cannot always be bought off the shelf. It often needs to be designed, ordered, manufactured, delivered, installed and tested. While this is happening, the business concerned will lose orders and income from customers going to competitors. Unfortunately, even if income decreases, expenditure continues, such as rental, insurance, accountants’ fees, maintenance fees, directors’ fees and employees’ salaries. Extra costs, such as advertising, staff overtime and rental of temporary premises, may also need to be incurred.

This is why there is a need for Business Interruption Insurance. It meets the demand for protection in the event of some incident happening which disrupts the operation of the business causing loss of income.

A Business Interruption Insurance policy cannot be effected alone without a material damage insurance policy, such as a Fire Insurance policy. The material damage warranty in a Business Interruption Insurance policy is explained later in this chapter.

There are different types of Business Interruption Insurance covers, such as:
▪ Loss of Profits following Fire and Additional Perils Insurance;
▪ Loss of Profits following Machinery Breakdown Insurance; and
▪ Industrial Special Risks (a combination of both material damage and Business Interruption Insurance).

The standard Business Interruption Insurance policy covers: (a) Loss Of “Gross Profit” This is to compensate the insured for not being able to generate revenue while his factory is damaged. Although the term “gross profit” is an accounting term, insurers define it in a different manner as commonly used by accountants. Insurers consider “gross profit” as net profit and specified standing charges (these are expenses that do not cease even during business interruption, e.g. bank mortgage charges, salaries of retained full-time staff members, etc.). (b) Increased Cost Of Working (ICOW) This cover relates to additional expenses reasonably incurred with a view to restoring trading or manufacturing activities i.e. minimising the period of disruption, such as hiring alternative premises, contracting out to competitors in order to meet the contractual obligations to the customers, etc. (c) Payroll It refers to salaries and/or wages paid to retained staff members during the indemnity period. These may be insured as a standing charge under the “gross profit” or as a separate sum insured.

The perils included in Business Interruption Insurance generally reflect the coverage of material damage insurance, i.e. those perils that can be included under material damage policies are also generally insurable under Business Interruption Insurance policies.

In fact, in Business Interruption Insurance policies, there is usually a warranty known as the ‘material damage warranty’. This warranty makes it necessary for the policy owner to also have a material damage policy admitting liability for the material damage before the insurer is liable to pay the claim under the Business Interruption Insurance policy. It follows that the perils under a material damage policy and the Business Interruption Insurance policy must be very similar, if not identical. The reason for this warranty is to protect the Business Interruption insurer, by ensuring that sufficient funds are available to rebuild or repair the premises after an insured event has occurred. The standard Business Interruption Insurance policy covers the perils of:
▪ fire;
▪ lightning; and
▪ explosion of gas used for illuminating or domestic purposes.

A typical Business Interruption Insurance policy is usually arranged for a fixed period of 12 months although insurers usually offer policyholders longer periods of time for an additional period if this is required.

One should not confuse the period of cover with the indemnity period. The period of cover is the period of insurance during which the policy is in force, whereas the indemnity period is the length of time during which the business is affected as a consequence of the damage, and whereby protection is granted under the Business Interruption Insurance policy. The indemnity period always commences from the date of loss within the period of insurance cover, it can be lesser, equal or longer than the period of insurance cover.

There are two indemnity periods. First, the one fixed by the proposer when arranging the insurance is called the maximum indemnity period. Then, there is the actual indemnity period, which will be evident only after an interruption, and while it can be longer than the maximum indemnity period, insurers are only liable to indemnify the insured for loss of profits up to the maximum indemnity period.

Many policies have a maximum indemnity period of 12 months, but except for the very simplest of businesses, this can prove to be totally inadequate, and so businesses should consider longer periods, such as 18, 24 or 36 months.

The policy conditions of a Business Interruption Insurance policy are similar to a Fire Insurance policy. A couple of the provisions and conditions which are specific to a Business Interruption Insurance policy are: (a) Alterations This condition states that the cover shall cease if:
▪ the business is wound up or permanently discontinued;
▪ the interest of the insured ceases other than by death; or
▪ any alteration is made either in the business, or to the premises or property, such that the risk of damage is increased, at any time after the commencement date of the cover, unless such alteration is endorsed by the insurer, subject to any additional premium payable by the insured. (b) Property Damage Rate Of Premium This condition requires the insured to notify the insurer if the rate of premium payable for the material damage insurance covering the property is increased. As such, if need be, the insured has to pay an additional premium for the Business Interruption Insurance policy.

Note that a detailed discussion on the policy provisions and conditions common to most general insurance policies is in the “Basic Insurance Concepts & Principles” Study Guide, published by the Singapore College of Insurance Limited.
As mentioned before, the insurer’s definition of gross profit under a business interruption insurance policy is different from the accounting term. See below.
We will now look at each area in detail.

Gross profit as an accounting term refers to the difference between the sales price of goods and the cost of such goods sold. In a business, this gross profit is then reduced by the costs involved in running the business and whatever is left over is called net profit (or net loss, if it is a negative figure). The net profit belongs to the owners (shareholders) of the business.

There are two ways of arranging Business Interruption cover to insure gross profit, namely, the “addition basis” and the “difference basis”. The equations presented below depict the insurer’s definition of gross profit.
Under the addition basis, gross profit is represented by the following equation:
Net profit also will disappear if income disappears. The owners do not want to lose it, so that needs to be insured as well.

For many years, the addition basis has been used for arranging Business Interruption Insurance. The insured, or the accountant of the insured provides the insurer with a list of the standing charges to be insured, and they are listed in the policy as being insured. The total of the standing charges will then be added to the net profit. Of course, there is a flaw in this – one or more charges may be omitted accidentally. Thus, in the event of a claim, the insured will not be fully indemnified. Gross Profit = Net Profit + Standing Charges

The difference basis avoids the problem as mentioned above, by defining gross profit in another way. Today, the difference basis is the predominant method of arranging Business Interruption cover. In contrast to the addition basis, the insured has to decide on the charges to exclude instead, and if they are not excluded, they are insured. This is in fact a much simpler and safer way of doing things.
The difference basis defines gross profit as:

The uninsured working expenses are variable expenses, i.e. they increase or decrease, depending on the corresponding increase or decrease in the turnover. These represent the normal list of expenses which business owners are of the opinion that these do not need to be insured. Examples are freight, carriage and packing material charges, etc.

The question of how the wages and salaries of employees are to be insured is always present. These are usually the largest expenses that an organisation will have, and the premium expended on their inclusion in the Business Interruption Insurance will be of interest to the business owner.

Many methods have been devised over the years, mainly aimed at keeping the premium within reasonable bounds. Current practice is to have what is called 100% payroll cover. That is to say that the whole of the payroll wages and salaries is included within the insurance of gross profit. There is no need to mention the payroll. It is automatically insured because it is not listed as an uninsured working expense.

This 100% inclusion ensures that the insurance will meet all legislative requirements relating to the employees’ payments, as well as meeting employers’ wishes to retain employees with special skills, and to offer them job security.

However, the fact remains that the payroll is a large figure. Insurers recognise that a total loss under a 100% payroll cover is most unlikely. This is because, in the event of a prolonged business interruption, some employees are likely to be dismissed, while some others will likely to leave on their own accord. In view of the large amount involved, the payroll element of the gross profit attracts a discounted rate of premium. Therefore, when cover is arranged on a 100% payroll basis, it is necessary to obtain not only the gross profit figure, but also the payroll figure.

Virtually, every Business Interruption Insurance policy will cover professional and accountant fees. The claim condition makes it obligatory on the part of the insured to produce various documents at his own expense as proof of any claim. It is usual for much of the information required to be supplied by the insured’s professional accountant and auditor. As there is a potential expense, there is a need to insure this item. For many years, the practice was to have a separate item covering these fees with a specific sum insured. These days, it is usual to include these fees under the gross profit item.
It should be noted that the fees are payable only for the production of the proof required. It does not include fees of assessors or other parties engaged to formulate and negotiate a claim.
The rent that the insured pays is automatically included within the gross profit item (as long as it is not listed in the uninsured working expenses).
There are occasions when the gross profit and payroll arrangements are not suitable to the client’s business. As such, alternatives are possible.

Some businesses do not rely on the purchase and sale of materials and goods, but instead sell their services, e.g. professional firms, such as solicitors, accountants, auditors, doctors and dentists. As they have few or no variable cost, they do not need the gross profit cover. Instead, a policy just insuring loss of fees (or revenue) and additional cost of working will suffice. In fact, most firms of this nature probably feel that they can continue their business with little loss of fees, but they do need the Additional Cost Of Working Insurance to cover the extra expenses, such as physical removal costs to alternative premises, advertising, staff overtime, etc.
The premium rate of a Business Interruption Insurance policy is derived from the annual rate charged under a material damage insurance policy. This is the minimum rate or the basis rate.

A loading is then applied to the minimum or basis rate. The rate of the loading depends on the length of the indemnity period selected. The loadings are generally shown in the table below: Indemnity Period (IP) Loading 3 months 50% 6 months 75% 9 months 85% 12 months 100% 18 months 88% 24 months 76% 30 months 70% 36 months 75%
Note further that, where the insured Indemnity Period is for less than 12 months, its minimum Gross Profit Sum Insured must represent its computed annual Gross Profit.

Example 2.1 gives a simple illustration on the calculation of premium for the gross profit item. Example 2.1: Calculation Of Premium For The Gross Profit Fire Rate = 0.20% Indemnity Period (IP) = 18 months Annual Gross Profit = S$1 million Gross Profit Sum Insured = S$1 million  = S$1.5 million Premium Computation = Rate  IP (loading)  Sum insured = 0.20%  88%  S$1.5 million = S$2,640
The material damage rate will apply here, irrespective of the indemnity period.

sections.title: Calculating The Amount To Be Insured

paragraphs.text: It is vital that the amount to be insured is calculated correctly. This is because an inadequate sum insured will not be able to provide full protection in the event of a loss.

To provide a satisfactory sum insured, there are three points to bear in mind when assessing information in the financial statements:
▪ those figures being used are historical, while a Business Interruption Insurance policy insures potential future losses (note also that businesses have trends and plans);
▪ the length of the maximum indemnity period; and
▪ the interruption may start at the very end of the insurance period – even on the last day.

To get the figure produced for gross profit and/or payroll, there must be an adjustment made for any known or predictable events that may affect the results of the business. For example, there may be plans to increase production because of a new contract, or new and more efficient machinery may be in the process of being installed, which will produce more output. The effects of inflation on the figures also need to be included.

Where the indemnity period (disruption period to the business) is likely to exceed 12 months, the resultant annual figures will have to be proportionately increased. Thus, for an 18-month indemnity period, at least 1.5 times the annual figure is required; for a 24-month indemnity period, at least twice the annual figure should be provided, and so on.

Further, care should be taken when assessing any business, where income is not earned steadily throughout the year. For a business earning the majority of its income in a concentrated period, say, a toy shop with high sales before Christmas, or a hotel with a high occupancy rate during the sales season, it is quite possible to earn two annual gross profits within 18 months or three within two years. In such cases, 1.5 or 2 times the annual gross profit may be inadequate.

Finally, bear in mind that an interruption may happen anytime. A fire or a flood or whatever peril may occur on the very last day of the insurance period. That is when the indemnity period will start. The implication of this is that, for a 12- month indemnity period, the insured or their broker should apply a trend for two years ahead; for a 24-month indemnity period, the insured or their broker should look at three years ahead, and so on. See Figure 2.1. Figure 2.1: Anticipated Gross Profit For A 24-Month Indemnity Period

Example 2.2 illustrates the three points describe earlier. Maximum Indemnity Period $ Anticipated Sales & Gross Profit Period Of Cover (365 days) Year 1 Year 2 Damage Occurring On The Last Day You should look at three years ahead when you anticipate the gross profit, as damage can occur even on the last day of the policy year. Example 2.2: Computation Of Gross Profit Sum Insured In S$ (24-Month Indemnity Period) A business about to fix its Business Interruption Insurance looks into the previous year’s accounts. It reviews the following figures: Sales S$ 300,000 Purchases S$ 100,000 Stock at 1 January 2021 S$ 45,000 Stock at 31 December 2021 S$ 50,000 Uninsured Working Expenses (other than Purchases) S$ 40,000
▪ The owners think that the business will expand at 5% each year for the next few years. Inflation is assumed to be running at 2% per year. They want to arrange a Business Interruption Insurance policy insuring gross profit on the “difference basis” with an indemnity period of 24 months. This means that the owners should look at three years ahead, when determining the amount of gross profit to be insured.
▪ The first thing that they must do is ascertain their gross profit according to the policy definition: Computation Of Gross Profit Sum Insured (In S$) Sales $300,000 Closing Stock (Stock at 31 Dec 2021) + $ 50,000 Total (1) $350,000 LESS Opening Stock (Stock at 1 Jan 2021) $ 45,000 Uninsured Working Expenses + $ 40,000 Purchases + $100,000 Total (2) $185,000 Annual Gross Profit for 2021 (1) – (2) $165,000 Business Trend and Inflation (7% p.a.)* $ 11,550 Estimated Annual Gross Profit for 2022 (3) $176,550 Business Trend and Inflation (7% p.a.)* + $ 12,359 Estimated Annual Gross Profit for 2023 (4) $188,909 Business Trend and Inflation (7% p.a.)* + $ 13,224 Estimated Annual Gross Profit for 2024 (5) $202,133 * To build in the business trend or inflation into the calculation: At the end of 2021, 5% growth in business trend and 2% inflation = 0.07 x S$165,000 = S$11,550 To fix a sum insured with a 24-month indemnity period, we must anticipate an interruption starting at the end of the year of insurance, and two more years of profits are to be insured. At the end of 2022, 5% growth in business trend and 2% inflation = 0.07 x S$176,550 = S$12,359 At the end of 2023, 5% growth in business trend and 2% inflation = 0.07 x S$188,909 = S$13,224 As mentioned earlier, a loss can occur even on the last day of the policy year. Therefore, the amount of gross profit to be insured should be at least: = (4) + (5) = S$188,909 + S$202,133 = S$391,042 However, it is advisable to have a higher sum insured than S$391,042, just in case, as trends or inflation may change. A figure between S$400,000 and, say, S$450,000 can confidently be expected.

If payroll is insured as a separate amount from the gross profit, it should be the total amount of wages or salaries paid or payable by the insured. Business trends and inflation must also be taken into consideration, when determining the payroll sum insured.
When selecting the sum insured for this item, one should estimate the fees that would be incurred to produce information for a claim. The basis should be on a “worst case scenario”.
Note that the underwriter may require additional information, and the details of required information may differ from insurer to insurer.

The perils excluded are virtually the same as those of a material damage policy, e.g. Fire Insurance policy. This is to be expected since the covers for both policies should fit each other exactly. As mentioned above, if a material damage insurance claim is not valid, the Business Interruption Insurance policy will not respond to the business interruption claim arising from the material damage.
Note that the exclusions of a Business Interruption Insurance policy vary from insurer to insurer.

Besides the above-mentioned excluded perils, there is also an excess clause that relieves the insurer from the liability to pay a specified portion of the amount of loss. The purpose for the insurer to impose an excess is to make the insured his own insurer for the specified amount of the excess, so that the insured is encouraged to take greater care or prudence on his part, to prevent the loss event from occurring.

In Business Interruption Insurance, there are two forms of excess in use, namely:
▪ a monetary excess; or
▪ a time excess.

A monetary excess is straightforward. The insured voluntarily accepts to pay the first part of any claim amount. A time excess, on the other hand, excludes losses arising in the first “x” hours or days after the occurrence of the damage.

In Singapore, monetary or time excesses were at one time not commonly used. This is with the exception of Engineering Consequential Loss (Business Interruption) Insurance policies. These days, excesses are more commonly applied and customarily noted in Contingent Business Interruption extensions, such as Infectious Disease, Suppliers, Utilities clause, and the like.

There are a number of extensions available to the basic insurance cover. In its standard form, the policy covers only such losses that are resulting from the insured damage at the insured’s own premises which are specified in the schedule of the policy. The business may be in part dependent upon the continued existence not only of its own premises, but also of the premises of suppliers, customers or other persons or bodies from whom goods or services are received. Therefore, a policy can be extended to cover loss resulting from damage by the insured perils at such other premises as well.
There are two types of extensions. Some require additional premiums, while some do not.

subsections.label: A
subsections.title: Extensions That Do Not Require Additional Premiums

paragraphs.text: Some common extensions that do not require payment of additional premiums are described below: (a) Payment On Account In the event of a loss, the insurers will make an interim payment for such a loss to the insured. (b) Material Damage Proviso Waiver As described earlier, a Business Interruption Insurance policy has a material damage proviso which states that liability in the material damage policy must be apparent, before the Business Interruption Insurance policy responds. However, there may be cases, whereby the amount of material damage loss has not exceeded the deductible. As such, the material damage policy need not respond. In such a situation, this extension overcomes the problem by waiving the material damage proviso, so that the insured will still be able to claim under his Business Interruption Insurance policy. (c) Interdependency Suppose the insured has two or more premises, and one of them has suffered damage and affected the business of the other undamaged premises. As such, this extension allows Business Interruption resulting from the other undamaged premises to be included as part of the claim. (d) Accumulation Of Stock When incorporated in the policy, this clause allows adjustment in claims payable if the insured has an accumulation of stocks of finished goods stored in warehouses and/or depots. As a result of usage of the stocks accumulated in the warehouses/depots, the effect of shortage in turnover is lessened. Thus, this will be taken into account when the insurer assesses the claim. (e) New Business The standard formula for loss of profits settlement cannot be applied to a business that has been going for less than 12 months. This clause specifies a basis of loss settlement for such new business.

Some common extensions that require payment of additional premiums are described below: (a) Specified Suppliers Businesses sometimes depend on other people, i.e. suppliers, to obtain such things as raw materials and semi-finished goods, in order to manufacture the finished products. This clause covers against loss of profits suffered by the insured owing to loss of or damage to the suppliers’ property at their premises by an insured peril. As the name suggests, the clause requires the suppliers to be named here. (b) Unspecified Suppliers The insured may have a large number of small suppliers but wishes to extend the policy to include them. The unspecified suppliers’ extension includes premises not in the occupation of the insured, where the property of the insured is stored. This extension is not normally available, without a specified supplier being named first. (c) Specified Customers This extension indemnifies the insured for loss of turnover due to damage to the named customers’ premises caused by an insured peril under the policy. When this extension is given, each customer has to be specified, and the percentage of the annual turnover derived from each customer must be stated against its name. (d) Accountant Clause This extension covers professional and accountant fees incurred for producing information required in connection with a claim. (e) Prevention Of Access Access to an insured’s premises may be blocked off because of damage at nearby premises, although the insured’s own premises are not damaged. This can cause interruption to the insured’s business and affect his trading. An extension to provide for the shortfall in the insured’s earnings arising from the situation can be granted, and it is the practice to charge a flat additional rate for a 12-month indemnity period. (f) Public Utilities This covers interruption or interference with the insured’s business in consequence of damage to property at any electricity station or substation, gas works or water works of the public supply undertaking from which the insured obtains electricity, gas or water. Cover provided under this extension is limited to damage at the premises of these public utilities. Should interruption be caused by the destruction of the distribution network (e.g. in the event of a severe storm), this cover is not triggered. (g) Infectious Or Contagious Disease, Murder, Suicide, Pest, Food Or Drink Poisoning, Or Defective Sanitary Arrangements If any of the above causes the restriction of the use of the premises by order of the police or public authority, the insured may suffer a loss. This is covered under this extension. (h) Leeway Clause This clause provides automatic cover for a pre-agreed additional increase to the sum insured amount. (i) Reinstatement Of Loss If this clause is attached, the insurance shall be maintained in force for the full sum insured even after a loss, and the insured needs to pay an additional premium only for the amount of the loss for the remaining period of insurance. (j) Transit The property of the insured may be covered while in transit by road, rail or inland waterway. This extension may be required where turnover can be affected following damage to goods in transit to the customer’s premises, or where machinery / equipment is being sent from one location of the insured’s premises to another. “Road Risks” are usually excluded from the cover under the extension. (k) Contract Sites This extension covers situations where the insured is carrying out a contract.

subsections.label: C
subsections.title: The Impact of Outbreak of Disease On Business Interruption Insurance

paragraphs.text: In view of the recent outbreak of COVID-19 (2019 Novel Coronavirus/2019-nCoV) which is grabbing the headline all over the world, we will briefly discuss its impact on Business Interruption Insurance in view of the extensions that we have studied above.

We have discussed above that under Prevention Of Access extension, the cover operates when access to an insured’s premises is blocked off because of damage at nearby premises, although the insured’s own premises are not damaged. As such, the outbreak of a disease would not be considered “damage at”. Therefore, this extension is unlikely to pay for interruption losses arising from outbreak of a disease.

In practice, Business Interruption cover is more likely to be triggered as a result of COVID-19 where the policy contains a relevant non-damage Business Interruption extension for infectious diseases. The extension may provide cover for closure of part or whole of the insured’s premises by an order of a competent/public authority as a result of an outbreak of a ‘notifiable’ human infectious disease. In the UK re the Financial Conduct Authority’s (FCA)’s Business Interruption insurance test case, the Supreme Court ruled in January 2021 that “cover may be available for partial closure of premises (as well as full closure) and for mandatory closure orders that were not legally binding and that valid claims should not be reduced because the loss would have resulted in any event from the pandemic.”1

Another possible situation is whereby the insured may suffer a Business Interruption loss arising from the closure of a supplier’s premises overseas e.g. Thailand (or elsewhere), by order or action of a relevant authority as result of a “specified” or “notifiable” disease. Depending on the policy wording applicable, it is possible that a non-damage Business Interruption trigger (e.g. non-damage Business Interruption extension for infectious diseases) being combined with contingent Business Interruption cover (e.g. Suppliers extension) to the effect that cover is provided for Business Interruption loss arising from the closure of a supplier’s premises overseas in such circumstances.

In view of the above, several insurers in Singapore and other markets have introduced communicable disease exclusions in property, business interruption and other policies. 1 Source: https://www.fca.org.uk/news/press-releases/supreme-court-judgment-business-interruptioninsurance- test-case

sections.title: Other Types Of Covers Related To Business Interruption

paragraphs.text: The two special covers related to Business Interruption Insurance are described below:

If there is an interruption, it is likely that the event will destroy records of the amounts owed to the firm by its customers. These are past debts. As such, they are not insured by the Business Interruption Insurance policy that deals with potential losses in the future. Hence, Loss of Book Debts cover is usually issued as a separate cover from the Business Interruption Insurance policy. It deals only with the costs of tracing and establishing the debts owed by the customers. It does not cover the failure to recover such debts from the customers.

The usual Business Interruption cover deals with the protection of future earnings of an existing enterprise, whereas an Advance Loss of Profits cover deals with protection of future earnings of a future business.

Advance Loss of Profits cover is available where a new business is contemplated, or a major extension to an existing business is to be made, by either new building work or the introduction of new machinery. Where either of these events is to happen, there is a time when financial loss may occur through delay by:
▪ damage to the works involved;
▪ damage to the premises of a supplier of key units of plant or equipment; or
▪ damage to such units in transit.
As such, the Advance Loss of Profits cover (also commonly known as DSU or Delay in Start Up Insurance) will protect against losses consequent upon interruption in such cases.

The indemnity period will start on the date when, but for the damage, the new business should have commenced. In addition to fire and additional perils, the coverage may include accidental damage to plants in transit, installation or testing.

In the event of a claim arising under a Business Interruption Insurance policy, the insured should be advised to take actions as described below: (a) Notification Of Loss Most insurers require the insured to notify them as soon as possible upon the occurrence of an insured peril, which may lead to a claim under the Business Interruption Insurance policy. Some insurers may even request that the insured notifies them within the same day of the incident occurring. The insurers are then committed to the investigation of the possible loss, and the provision of assistance in the safeguarding and recovery of the insured’s business. (b) Minimisation Of Loss It is also a requirement under the claim condition that the insured must act as though he is uninsured and take whatever action that is necessary to minimise the loss. (c) Co-operation With Loss Adjuster As for a material damage claim, it is a normal practice for the insurer to appoint a loss adjuster to assess the incurred loss, as well as to advise the insured of the best method of working, after the damage has occurred. Hence, it is important for the insured to co-operate with the loss adjuster, so that the time taken for the settlement of the claim is much shorter. Loss adjusters are also appointed for Business Interruption claims. It is equally important for the insured to co-operate with them as well. (d) Submission Of Claim Form & Relevant Documents The use of claim forms is not common in business interruption. A completed claim form will have already been completed for the property damage loss assuming both property damage and business interruption are with the same insurer so basic investigations, such as compliance with warranties on the property damage policy, will already have been carried out. The insured must submit the relevant supporting documents to the insurer, within 30 days after the expiry of the indemnity period. Examples of relevant supporting documents include accounting and financial reports, such as the Profit and Loss statements, payroll records, inventory reports, and a statement of claims. This supporting documentary evidence must be produced and delivered to the insurer at the expense of the insured.

In all claims, the client must be reminded:
▪ to act with reasonable speed in dealing with the loss or damage;
▪ to take all reasonable measures to avert or minimise the loss or damage; and
▪ not to jeopardise the insurer’s rights of action of recovery against any third party who may be responsible for the loss or damage.

chapters.title: LIABILITY AND CONTINGENCY INSURANCE PART I
learningOutcomes: identify the various types of liabilities under Common Law and statute
know what Work Injury Compensation Insurance is all about
know how an employer’s legal liability towards his employees may arise
understand what an employee must prove in order to sue his employer under Common Law
identify who is considered and who is not considered an “employee” under the Work Injury Compensation Act 2019
realise what constitutes the “earnings” of an employee under the Act
know the nature of accidents arising out of and in the course of employment
know the benefits which an employer must provide under the Act
understand how the compensatory amounts payable for temporary and permanent incapacity are arrived at
know the definition of “relative” of the employee under the Act
understand how the death compensation is calculated under the Act

understand the scope of cover, policy provisions and conditions, underwriting information required, policy and other documents, exclusions, additional benefits and claim procedure of Work Injury Compensation Insurance
know what Public Liability Insurance is all about
understand the scope of cover, policy provisions and conditions, underwriting information required, exclusions, extensions, and claim procedure of Public Liability Insurance
know what Products Liability Insurance is all about
understand the scope of cover, policy provisions and conditions, underwriting information required, exclusions, extensions and claims of Products Liability Insurance

paragraphs.text: Both individuals and organisations face the risks of liability losses. A liability loss is the money that the individual or organisation has to pay as a result of a specific legal claim or suit against them. For a claim or suit to succeed, the claimant must establish that the wrongdoer (i.e. the individual or organisation) is legally liable. The legal liabilities of an individual or organisation may arise from statute1, Common Law2 or contract3.

Employers have a legal liability towards their employees for injuries sustained, diseases contracted, as well as death at the workplace by virtue of the Work Injury Compensation Act 2019. This is an example of a legal liability arising from statute4.

On the other hand, liability under Common Law arises when a person commits a tort. A tort is a civil wrong that violates the rights of another. Examples of torts include negligence, intentional torts, strict liability and vicarious liability. Negligence is an unintentional tort involving the failure of a person to exercise the degree of care that a reasonably prudent person would have exercised under similar circumstances, to avoid harming another person or a legally recognised entity. For example, a company’s employee injures a passer-by while he is loading and unloading goods from a van in a public road. The company will be liable to the injured passer-by. An example of intentional tort is defamation which includes both libel and slander.

Strict liability also known as absolute liability arises from situations involving inherently dangerous activities. For example, the owner of the pet lion is liable for any injury that the lion may inflict on others regardless of the precautions taken by the owner. Vicarious liability occurs when an individual who is liable for some occurrence is under the control of some other person or organisation, and who, therefore, transfers liability to the party in control. For instance, the liability of an employee may be transferred to his employer i.e. the employer may be vicariously liable for the actions of his employees.

As for liability arising from contract, a manufacturer who supplies defective goods to his purchaser causing the purchaser to suffer losses is said to have breached the legal liability that he owes to the purchaser under the contract. Therefore, he is liable for the losses incurred by the purchaser. 1 Statutes are written laws passed by Parliament. 2 Common Law is unwritten law established by the courts on a case-by-case basis over time. 3 A contract is a legally enforceable promise or set of promises between two or more parties. 4 Another example of a legal liability arising from statute is the requirement for all motorists to insure their thirdparty liabilities resulting from motor car usage. This aspect of liability will be discussed in Chapter 4 of this Study Guide.
