To protect against liability losses, individuals and organisations can purchase Liability Insurance (also known as “Third-party Insurance”) policies. An individual may purchase a Personal Liability Insurance policy,5 while the organisation can purchase Commercial Liability Insurance policies to cover their risk exposure.

There are many types of Commercial Liability Insurance policies to cater to the different liability needs of the organisations. In Part I of this chapter, we will discuss Work Injury Compensation Insurance, Public Liability Insurance and Products Liability Insurance. In Part II of this chapter, we shall cover other types of Liability Insurance.
The Work Injury Compensation Act (Cap. 354) [“the repealed Act”] was repealed on 1 September 2020.

The Work Injury Compensation Act 2019 [“the Act”] or referred to as WICA 2019 in this study text took effect from 1 September 2020. It offers the following benefits for both employees and employers: (i) Influence companies to prevent injuries from happening in the first place; (ii) Faster and simpler claims processes for everyone; (iii) Enhanced protection for employees; and (iv) More certainty for employers.

Related subsidiary legislation amendments took effect from January 2020. Enacted in September 2019, the Act WICA 2019 requires all insurers intending to sell Work Injury Compensation (WIC) insurance policies to be designated by the Ministry of Manpower (MOM), to be able to sell approved insurance policies and process claims. A list of the designated insurers can be found in the MOM website. This new regulatory regime took effect from 1 September 2020.

The Act holds an employer liable to provide compensation to any employee or his dependants for certain specified occupational diseases, death or personal injury by accident arising out of and in the course of his employment (whether in Singapore or overseas). Unless exempted, the Act also makes it an offence for any employer who does not provide the employee with Work Injury Compensation benefits. The compensable occupational diseases are listed under the Second Schedule of the Act. These include, among others, contracted diseases and chronic medical condition resulting from work-related prolonged exposure to physical agents, such as radiation, noise, biological or chemical agents and exposure to excessive heat. 5 This is discussed in the “Personal General Insurance” Study Guide, published by the Singapore College of Insurance Limited.

Under the Act, “employee” means an individual who has entered into or works under a contract of service with an employer and — a) includes every employee of the Government in a class or description declared by the President to be employees for the purposes of this Act; but b) does not include any class of individuals specified in the Third Schedule.

The Act covers all employees (whether local or foreign employees) who are engaged under a contract of service or apprenticeship, regardless of their level of earnings and whether involving manual or non-manual work, unless exempted. Classes of persons not covered are specified in the Third Schedule of the Act i.e. (i) Any member of the Singapore Armed Forces. (ii) Any officer of the Singapore Police Force, the Singapore Civil Defence Force, the Central Narcotics Bureau or the Singapore Prisons Service. (iii) A domestic worker, being an individual employed in or in connection with the domestic services of any private premises.

Every employer is required under the Act to maintain adequate Work Injury Compensation Insurance (WICI) for:
▪ all employees doing manual work, regardless of salary level; and
▪ all employees doing non-manual work and earning S$2,600 or less a month each; unless the Minister, by notification in the Gazette, waives the requirement of such insurance in relation to any employer.
Failure to do so is an offence punishable by a maximum fine of S$10,000 and/or imprisonment of up to 12 months. This is specified under Section 25(1) of the Act.

Previously, employers who bought WICI policies may find some work scenarios that were excluded from coverage. To safeguard employers, a core set of standard terms for Act (WICA 2019)-compliant policies (i.e. an approved Work Injury Compensation Act 2019 insurance policy) have been prescribed to ensure adequate coverage. Insurers can offer additional coverage, but they cannot reduce the benefits under the standard policy. From 1 Jan 2022 onwards, all WICI policies had to be Act compliant.

WIC Insurance covers the insured employer against any legal liability as required under the Act and provides compensation in accordance with the provisions of the Act. In the approved Work Injury Compensation Act 2019 insurance policy, there is NO cover of common law liability that was found in WIC policies issued under the repealed Act. However, the Work Injury Compensation (Insurance) Regulations 2020, S2(4) states: “Subject to section 26(1)(b) and (3) of the Act, an approved policy may provide additional insurance cover for an employer’s liability to pay compensation for work injury of the employer’s employee, including such liability under common law or any other written law.”

Basically, insurers can add benefits (e.g. common law liability) to the policy. Most importantly, these must not deviate from the compulsory terms specified in the WIC Regulations 2020. The insuring or operative clause under the approved Work Injury Compensation Act 2019 insurance policy (see Appendix 3A) states as follows: 4. “NOW if any employee described in the Schedule in the Insured's employment has a Relevant Injury the Company will, subject to Terms of this Policy, indemnify the Insured against all sums that the Insured shall be liable to pay under the Legislation in respect of that employee and will in addition pay all costs and expenses incurred by the Insured with the written consent of the Company. 5. In the event of the death of the Insured the Company will indemnify the Insured's legal personal representatives in accordance with the Terms of this Policy in respect of liability incurred by the Insured provided that such personal representatives shall observe, comply, fulfil and be subject to the Terms of this Policy as though they were the Insured insofar as the Terms of the Policy can apply.”

For issuance or renewal of the Work Permit or S Pass documents for the employment of approved foreign workers in Singapore, the Ministry of Manpower (MOM) requires the employer to provide information on the name of the insurer, policy number, policy commencement date and policy expiry date.
Before proceeding to discuss WICI, we will first examine how an employer’s liability towards his employees arises.

subsections.label: A
subsections.title: Employer’s Legal Liability Towards His Employees

paragraphs.text: An employer’s legal liability towards his employees arises under Common Law, and also in accordance with Section 7(1) of the Act, which states: “Where personal injury is caused to an employee by an accident arising out of and in the course of the employee’s employment with an employer, that employer is liable to pay compensation under this Act.”

An employer is liable to be sued under Common Law by his employee for negligence leading to an accident which results in the employee being injured. However, for an employee to succeed under Common Law, he has to prove that the employer owes him a duty of care and has been negligent and in breach of that duty which has resulted in him suffering injuries. These have to be substantiated by a court of law, usually through a civil suit, where lawyers are involved. Common Law claims must be filed with the court within 3 years from the date of the accident.

As mentioned earlier, in the approved Work Injury Compensation Act 2019 insurance policy, there is NO cover of common law liability that was found in WIC policies issued under the repealed Act. However, as we have already discussed, it is possible for such covers to be granted as additional benefits by insurers.

The Act mandates that the employer must provide compensation to his employees for personal injuries, death and certain occupational diseases arising out of and in the course of their employment. It also specifies the types of benefits that the employer must provide for his employees. The amount of compensation is computed based on a fixed formula and is subject to limits. The Act is a piece of no fault, social legislation for the benefit and protection of the employees. It provides the injured employees with a low-cost and expeditious alternative to Common Law to settle compensation claims. For any liability under the Act, negligence does not have to be proved.
A claim for compensation under the Act must be made within 1 year from the date of accident.
An injured employee can claim either compensation under the Act or Common law damages, but not both.

subsections.label: B
subsections.title: Benefits Which An Employer Must Provide Under The Act

paragraphs.text: An employer is required to provide medical expenses benefit for medical treatment to an employee who is injured in a work-related accident. Also, an employer is liable to pay medical expenses incurred within a period of one year after the happening of the accident causing the injury to the employee, up to a limit of S$45,000, whichever is reached earlier. This limit applies to any accident that happens on or after 1 January 2020. However, if a medical practitioner is unable to make an assessment of the degree of incapacity until after one-year, medical expenses will be payable until the injury has stabilised sufficiently for the medical assessment to be made. The Commissioner at MOM can order extra medical expenses to be paid if there is reasonable basis to show that the employee needs longer-term medical treatment beyond the one-year period.

Within these limits, employers will be responsible for all the medical expenses incurred for medical treatment considered necessary by any Singaporeregistered medical practitioner or in any approved hospital. Employers are liable to pay such cost of medical treatment directly to the hospital.
Employers are liable to pay cost of medical treatment received overseas, if the accident happens while the employee is working overseas, and where immediate medical treatment is needed.

Under Section 5(2) of the First Schedule of the Act: “To avoid doubt, the cost of medical treatment includes, but is not limited to — (a) the charges in connection with an emergency medical transport for the conveyance of an injured employee to receive medical treatment; (b) the fees for medical reports required for the purposes of this Act; (c) the charges for physiotherapy and occupational and speech therapy; (d) the charges for case management, psychotherapy for the treatment of post‑traumatic stress disorder, functional capacity evaluation and worksite assessment, required for the purposes of rehabilitating and enabling an injured employee to return to work; (e) the cost of medicines, artificial limbs and surgical appliances; and (f) the charges and fees for medical examination and consultation related to medical treatment.”
In general, expenses for Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) are not eligible.

subsections.label: B2
subsections.title: Medical Leave Wages (Compensation For Temporary Incapacity)

paragraphs.text: An employer is required to provide an employee, who suffers temporary incapacity paid medical leave (Average Monthly Earnings i.e.”AME”) up to a year. For the first 14 days of medical leave (60 days if he is hospitalised), the employer is required to pay AME to the employee, irrespective whether or not he is staying in the hospital. In addition, he has to pay up to 14 days shortfall between actual and AME for light duties.

Beyond the above-mentioned periods, two-thirds of the employee’s AME is payable up to a period of one year from the date of accident. Public holidays6, rest days and non-working days are excluded from the number of days of medical leave granted to the employee.
Medical leave and medical expenses are to be paid promptly by the seventh day of the following month, after they are incurred or due for payment.

Note: This is in line with the requirements as specified in the Employment Act 1968. 6 For public holidays, while these are not granted as paid medical leave under the Work Injury Compensation Act, the employee may be paid for the public holidays as his entitlement under the Employment Act 1968.

subsections.label: B3
subsections.title: Lump Sum Compensation For Permanent Incapacity

paragraphs.text: The Act also mandates that an employer must compensate his employee for permanent incapacity. Permanent total incapacity refers to the injuries that incapacitate an employee from doing all work which he was capable of doing at the time of the accident, and thereby reducing permanently the earning capacity of the employee in every job.

Examples are the loss of both hands or feet, total loss of sight, total paralysis, or injuries resulting in being permanently bedridden as specified in the Fourth Schedule of the Act. The lump sum payable for permanent incapacity is subject to the following formula in accordance with the Act: Employee’s monthly earnings x multiplying factor (see A below) x percentage of loss of earning capacity (see B below) A: Depending on the employee’s age next birthday as specified in Section 2(a) of the First Schedule of the Act. B: Depending on the employee’s injury as specified in the Fourth Schedule of the Act.

The amount of compensation payable is subject to maximum and minimum limits updated with effect from 1 January 2020 as follows: Maximum limit = S$289,000 x (% loss of earning capacity) Minimum limit = S$97,000 x (% loss of earning capacity)

If the medical practitioner certifies that the injured employee is suffering from permanent total incapacity (i.e. 100% loss of earning capacity), the employer has to pay an additional compensation of one quarter (i.e. 25%) of the amount awarded for permanent total incapacity to the employee, to offset the cost of care for the injured employee.
Note that the percentage of loss of earning capacity (as specified in the Fourth Schedule of the Act) will be determined by the attending medical practitioner.

When an employee dies in a work-related accident or lacks capacity in relation to the employee’s property and affairs, the employer has to pay compensation to the deceased employee’s relative. A “relative” according to Section 2 of the Act includes: (a) a spouse of the claimant; (b) a child (including any adopted child, illegitimate child or stepchild) of the claimant; (c) a parent (including any adoptive parent or step-parent) of the claimant; (d) the claimant’s sibling (including any stepsibling or half sibling); and (e) the claimant’s grandparent or grandchild.

As seen above, the definition of “relative” is very wide under the Act in that the Commissioner is obliged to consider every eligible dependant, and there is no requirement that the dependant must indeed be dependent upon the deceased employee, in order for such a dependant to be entitled to the death compensation.
The formula for computing the death compensation is as follows: Employee’s monthly earnings x multiplying factor [see Section 1(1) of the First Schedule of the Act]

Just like permanent incapacity, the death compensation is paid in a lump sum and is subject to a maximum and minimum limit, similarly updated with effect from 1 January 2020 to S$225,000 and S$76,000 respectively.
Appendix 3B gives an illustration on how to compute the amount of compensation due to an employee for permanent total incapacity.

The amount that an employer is liable towards their employees can potentially be substantial. This is especially important as an accident may involve a number of employees sustaining serious or fatal injuries. This can have a severe impact on the company’s finances and may even affect its cash flow. Some employers may not even be able to pay the compensation. For this reason, the Act mandates that employers insure certain groups of their eligible employees under the WICI policy.
For details of the Act and its Schedules mentioned above, as well as the Work Injury Compensation Guide, refer to the MOM Website.

Compensation Limits From 1 January 2020 (a) On 1 January 2020, the maximum and minimum compensation limits for death and total permanent incapacity under WICA were increased. The cap on medical expenses was raised from S$36,000 to S$45,000. The amounts are indicated in the table below. Limits For any accident that happened before 1 January 2020 For any accident that happened on or after 1 January 2020 Death Minimum S$69,000 S$76,000 Maximum S$204,000 S$225,000 Total Permanent Incapacity* Minimum S$88,000 S$97,000 Maximum S$262,000 S$289,000 Medical Expenses Up to S$36,000 or one year from date of accident, whichever is reached first Up to S$45,000 or one year from date of accident, whichever is reached first * This excludes the additional 25% compensation that is paid to the injured employee with total permanent incapacity to offset the cost of care for him. (b) Treatments that facilitate return to work are also claimable as part of WICA medical expenses. These will cover the charges for case management, functional capacity evaluation and worksite assessment for purposes of rehabilitating an injured employee back to work.

As far as the provisions of the Act is concerned, the employer must insure employees who fall under the mandatory insurance category (i.e. employees who are involved in manual work, regardless of their level of earnings, and employees who are involved in non-manual work and each having monthly earnings of S$2,600 or less7). In addition to these, the employer may also want to insure their other employees; i.e. the non-mandatory personnel namely those in non-manual work and each earning above S$2,600 per month.

The above-mentioned covers are insured under an “annual policy” basis. Prior to 1 September 2020, WICI could also be issued under a “project policy” basis. A project WIC policy refers to the portions of project insurance policies that provide coverage for work injury sustained at a project site, or while doing work for a specific project. 7 As mentioned earlier, from 1 April 2021, the salary threshold for non-manual employees requiring work injury compensation insurance was increased to S$2,600. The increase happened in 2 phases i.e. from 1 April 2020, the salary threshold was S$2,100 and from 1 April 2021, it is S$2,600. Employers must still purchase insurance for all manual employees, regardless of salary.

MOM has stipulated that from 1 September 2020, employers should not get new project WIC policies. There are WIC claims that are covered under both project WIC policy and employer’s WIC policy. Such duplicate coverage can lead to delays and disputes over who should compensate the injured employee or to reimburse the employer. Companies can potentially reduce insurance premiums by avoiding duplicate coverage.

In respect of the above, employers must get WIC insurance for their employees, even if they are covered by project WIC policies. If employees are only covered under a project WIC policy, employers may be charged for non-insurance. This is because project WIC policies do not cover some instances of work injury, such as injury that occurred in company transport or while working outside the project site.

Notwithstanding the above, insurers can keep their existing project WIC policies. They will be considered double coverage because employers, i.e. subcontractors, must get their own insurance. If there is a claim, it will be made against the employer’s policy.

Insurers ceased selling new project WIC policies from 1 September 2020. Employers had to buy their own WIC insurance policy. MOM recommended that insurers work with developers to terminate the WIC portion of their existing project policies.

For main contractor and developers, they are allowed to keep their existing project WIC policies. They will be considered double coverage as employers, i.e. sub-contractors, must get their own insurance. As mentioned earlier, if there is a claim, it will be made against the employer’s policy. In this respect, main contractor and developers should not renew or buy new WIC policies. Instead, they should ensure that each of their sub-contractors buy insurance to cover their own employees.

Main contractor and developers can also buy insurance to cover common law liabilities. Such insurance is not governed under the Act. MOM recommends that they work with their insurers to terminate the WIC portion of their project policies.

In this section, we will examine the provisions and conditions of the approved Work Injury Compensation Act 2019 insurance policy, a specimen copy of which can be found at Appendix 3A of this study guide. From 1 Jan 2022 onwards, all WICI policies had to be “Act” or WICA 2019 compliant.

The specimen policy wording in Appendix 3A of this study guide is actually derived from the First Schedule of the Work Injury Compensation (Insurance) Regulations 2020. Please refer to Appendix 3A1” First Schedule Of The Work Injury Compensation (Insurance) Regulations 2020” of this study guide for details of these terms.
Some of the provisions and conditions of the approved Work Injury Compensation Act 2019 insurance policy are described below:

The policy is governed by the laws of the Republic of Singapore. As such, judgements which are delivered by or obtained from a court or tribunal of a jurisdiction outside Singapore will result in the insurer not having to pay a claim.

The Act requires the insurer to pay WICI claims in certain circumstances notwithstanding that there could be a non-disclosure of a material fact, fraudulent claim or breach of a policy condition. However, the Recovery From Insured Clause provides that the insurer is entitled to recover such amounts paid from the insured (the employer).

subsections.label: D3
subsections.title: Insured’s Duty To Ensure Safety At The Workplace

paragraphs.text: This condition of the policy requires the insured (employer) to take all reasonable precautions to prevent accidents and diseases to his employees. It also requires the insured to comply with all statutory obligations and requirements, including those relating to safety, at the workplace.

subsections.label: D4
subsections.title: Insured To Make No Admission Or Promise

paragraphs.text: This is an important condition. It requires that the insured must not make directly, or through others, any offer, promise or payment, without the consent of the insurer.
This condition helps to prevent the situation, whereby the insurer may be prejudiced by any impulsive or uncontrolled action of the insured.

subsections.label: D5
subsections.title: Change In Nature Of Business, Job Category or Category of Employee

paragraphs.text: This condition requires the insured to notify the insurer immediately if the Nature of the Business or Job Category or Category of Employee as described in the Schedule has changed in such a way as to increase the risk of accident or disease to any employee in the insured’s employment and at the latest within 14 days from the date of the change. The insurer can refuse to pay any claim made under such circumstances if the insurer is not notified of the changes as mentioned above.
The insurer also reserves the right to adjust the premium to an amount reasonably payable for the insured’s risk profile upon the insured’s notification to the insurer of such change.

How does the insurer compute the premium payable when issuing a Work Injury Compensation Insurance policy? It is based on the total amount of earnings paid by the insured (as well as other employers and known to the insured) to every employee in the insured’s employment during the Period of Insurance.

However, at the inception or commencement date of the policy, the insured can only declare to the insurer an estimate of the total earnings to be paid by the insured for the policy period. A provisional premium is charged by the insurer based on this declaration. The insured would not know the actual earnings to be paid until the expiry of the policy.

The insured is required within a month after the end date or termination of the policy, to furnish the insurer an account of all earnings paid by the insured (as well as by other employers and known to the insured) to every employee in the Insured’s employment during the policy period. This would enable the insurer to determine the actual premium payable for the policy period.

Please note the following: (a) An additional premium will be charged, or a refund of premium will be made depending on the difference between the provisional premium and actual premium payable as discussed above. (b) The insured shall keep and maintain a proper record of the name and full personal particulars of every employee in the Insured’s employment together with the amount of earnings paid by the Insured (as well as other employers and known to the Insured) during the policy period. (c) The insurer is entitled to inspect the above-mentioned record and the insured needs to allow the insurer to inspect such records when required. (d) For claims settlement, the estimated annual earnings declared is compared to the past annual earnings [the total of the monthly earnings paid by the Insured (as well as by other employers and known to the Insured) during the 12 months immediately before the commencement date of the policy] to determine the extent of under-insurance (if any). If there is underinsurance, the insurer will not indemnify the insured in full and the insured is required to bear a proportion of the claim commensurate with the extent of under-insurance. This is the application of Average by insurers, and it is to discourage insureds from under-declaring the earnings of their employees.

As discussed above, there is an Average provision contained within the Premium Adjustment and Declaration of Earnings Clause of the policy. A separate Average Condition also exists in the insurance policy. The reader would recall that when we discussed the Recovery From Insured clause, the Act requires the insurer to pay certain claims in full notwithstanding that there could be breach of the policy conditions.

However, under the Recovery From Insured clause, the insurer is entitled to recover such amounts paid from the Insured (the employer). Likewise, the Average condition entitles the insurer to recover the rateable proportion of the liability from the Insured (the employer). For example, if there was 30% underinsurance, the insurer would have to pay the claim in full under the Act but is entitled to recover 30% of the claim from the Insured.

This clause makes it clear that no terms of the policy or memorandum or endorsement affects the compulsory terms of the approved Work Injury Compensation Act 2019 insurance policy as provided under section 26 of the Act. In addition, even the policy schedule or any information in the proposal for insurance is not to affect the compulsory terms as stated in the Act.

As discussed previously, to safeguard employers, a core set of standard terms for Act (WICA 2019)-compliant policies have been prescribed to ensure adequate coverage. These policies are known as approved policies.

As explained earlier in this chapter, insurers can offer additional coverage, but they cannot reduce the benefits under the approved policy. Even if any term or condition or endorsement in the policy appears to reduce such benefits, this clause makes it clear that they shall be of no effect in this regard.

Note that a detailed discussion on the policy provisions and conditions common to most general insurance policies are found in the “Basic Insurance Concepts & Principles” Study Guide, published by the Singapore College of Insurance Limited.
Note that the underwriter may require additional information, and the details of the required information may differ from insurer to insurer.

Designated WIC insurers are required to share policy and claims data with MOM. The data will be made available to all designated insurers approved by MOM. This is to facilitate information sharing between insurers of their clients’ past claims record.

Once an insurer has underwritten and accepted the risk, it will insure the employer under an approved policy. The insurer is required under Section 4 of the Work Injury Compensation (Insurance) Regulations 2020 (“the regulations”) to issue a certificate of insurance within 7 days after the date on which the approved policy commences or is renewed. See Appendix 3C for a specimen copy of such a certificate of insurance. The insured is required under the regulations to display it prominently in his workplace, if more than one workplace, a copy in each workplace.
In this section, we will briefly detail the exclusions of the approved Work Injury Compensation Act 2019 insurance policy.

The exclusions are:
▪ any sum which the insured would have been entitled to recover from any party but for an agreement between the insured and such party;
▪ any claims based upon or arising out of asbestosis and mesothelioma.
▪ the insured’s liability directly or indirectly caused by fundamental risks such as war, invasion, civil commotion, terrorism etc. and any action taken in controlling, preventing, suppressing such risks
▪ liability arising from nuclear weapons material, ionising radiations or contamination by radioactivity from any nuclear fuel or from any nuclear waste from the combustion of nuclear fuel*
▪ liability claims relating to United Nations sanction and/or trade plus economic sanctions of various countries including Singapore.

While the policy excludes liability arising from nuclear weapons material, ionising radiations or contamination by radioactivity from any nuclear fuel or from any nuclear waste from the combustion of nuclear fuel, it does not exclude any liability relating to radioactive isotopes other than the exception as outlined above.
The above extensions are customarily subject to extra premium consideration.

Under Section 35 of the Act: (a) A claim for compensation under this Act is deemed to be made by an employee when the employer first has notice of an accident giving rise to the employee’s work injury (Section 35(1)), namely when the employee informs the employer or the foreman or person supervising him at the time of the accident or any person designated for that purpose by the employer (Section 35(2)(a)) or the employer has knowledge of the accident by any other means (Section 35(2)(b)); (b) The employer must within 10 days (as stipulated under the Work Injury Compensation Regulations 2020) after he first has notice of the accident giving rise to the work injury to the employee, give notice of the accident to the Commissioner in the prescribed form and to the employer’s insurer in writing (Section 35(3)); (c) The employee must, as soon as possible after sustaining a work injury in an accident, notify the employer or person mentioned in Section 35(2)(a), otherwise his claim is deemed withdrawn if the employer does not have notice of the accident within 1 year of the date of the accident (Section 35 (4) & (5)).

Section 39 of the Act stipulates that: (a) Any employee who has made a deemed claim for a work injury under section 35 and who wishes to claim compensation for permanent incapacity or current incapacity arising from that work injury must make the claim in the prescribed form and manner required by the Commissioner, not later than one year after the date of the accident, for permanent incapacity or current incapacity. (b) However, the above does not apply where the employer’s insurer or the Commissioner notifies the employee that the employee is being assessed for permanent incapacity or current incapacity.

The accident reporting requirement under the Act is in line with those under the Workplace Safety and Health Act 2006. WICA is intended as a piece of social legislation to provide low cost and expeditious resolution of work-related injury claims. To make a claim, an injured employee does not need to prove fault. He must prove that he was injured in a workplace accident or suffered an occupational disease relating to his work.

In the event of the occurrence of any accident or any disease mentioned in the Act that may give rise to a claim under the policy, the insured shall give notice of the occurrence to the insurer with full particulars within the time required by the Act and regulations.
Employers must report all work-related medical leave or light duties to MOM.
The employer can submit the Incident Report online to MOM using the iReport system on the MOM Website.

In addition, the insured has to give notice or forward to the insurer as soon as possible any letter of claim, writ, summons and process upon receipt. The insured must also notify the insurer immediately of any impending prosecution, inquest or fatal enquiry in connection with the occurrence.

For WIC policies, the amount of compensation is determined by the MOM (see also Appendix 3B for the illustration of how the computation is done). A Notice of Assessment of compensation must be forwarded to the insurer, employer and employee. The employer/insurer is given 14 days from the date of receipt of the Notice of Assessment of Compensation (NAC) in which to object to the quantum of compensation awarded by the Commissioner in MOM. In such a case, the insurer may send the employee for reassessment by its appointed medical practitioner and submit the medical reports to the Commissioner for review. Once the amount of compensation is finalised, the insurer will make the payment to the employee or his personal legal representative, either directly or through MOM.

Delay in settling Permanent Incapacity (PI) claims means some injured employees can only receive their compensation after several months, while employers incur higher upkeep and maintenance expenses to look after the injured workers. This causes uncertainty and inconvenience for all parties involved. Compensation for PI is therefore based on the prevailing state of incapacity (termed “current incapacity” or CI) at the earliest opportunity six months from the date of accident. The more complex injuries can still be assessed for permanent incapacity at a later date.

For cases of fatal or serious injuries, employees or their representatives do not need to file claim applications. Claim processing for these cases will commence once MOM or the insurer is notified of the accidents. Employees who do not wish to make a claim under the Act can opt out.

The roles played by designated insurers are as follows: (i) Designated WIC insurers process all insured claims – for temporary incapacity, fatal and permanent incapacity. A licensing framework is in place to regulate insurers, to ensure checks and balances are in place for insurers to process claims fairly and expediently. (ii) The insurers must comply with MOM’s processing guidelines and cannot frivolously reject claims. Insurers do not determine the compensation amount as the basis of computing the compensation sum under WICA is prescribed in the WICA Schedule. (iii) To ensure that injured employees continue to receive their compensation promptly, insurers have to meet the processing timelines set by MOM. MOM will also conduct audits on insurers for compliance with MOM’s requirements. (iv) Designated insurers who fail to comply with MOM’s licensing conditions may face administrative financial penalties (AFP) of up to $30,000 for each such failure, or in more serious cases, have their licenses suspended or revoked.

Does Work Injury Compensation Act (WICA) cover diseases due to COVID-19? Would it apply if employees fall sick on a work trip? WICA covers employees who contract diseases from biological agents, including COVID-19, arising from and in the course of work. However, WICA will not cover diseases due to COVID-19 if it was contracted through non-work activities or exposure.

Are employees covered under the Work Injury Compensation Act (WICA) while working from home? Yes. Employees are covered under WICA wherever they work, including from home. If an employee was injured in the course of working from home, the employer is liable to compensate. Nonetheless, the onus is on the employee to prove that the injury indeed arose while doing work at home, and not while performing non-work activities at home.

The Public Liability Insurance or also commonly known as the General Liability Insurance is designed to indemnify an insured (usually a business) in respect of claims from members of the public or companies who may suffer owing to his negligence or that of his employees. Cover is in respect of damages, as well as costs and expenses of litigation incurred in the event of a claim against the insured for injury or for damage to property not to mention claimant’s costs awarded against the insured.

Public liability covers the insured’s liability for injury caused to third party individuals or damage to third party properties. It does not cover claims made by the employees, servants or agents of the insured.
The period of insurance for a Public Liability Insurance policy is usually 12 months. 8 Source: https://www.mom.gov.sg/covid-19/frequently-asked-questions#eligible-claims-and-medical-benefits

Example 3.1 illustrates the usefulness of a Public Liability Insurance policy. Example 3.1: When Can A Public Liability Insurance Policy Help? A deliveryman came to Atlantic Bakery Company (ABC) to deliver some goods ordered by ABC. The employee who attended to him negligently dropped a heavy object onto the deliveryman’s left foot which was crushed by the impact. The deliveryman subsequently sued ABC for injuries and damages. In such a case, ABC could rely on its Public Liability Insurance policy to cover the liabilities arising from the accident. Hence, ABC would not have to worry about how the compensation would affect its business cash flow or whether it would have to declare bankrupt.

The policy (see sample in Appendix 3D) indemnifies the insured against all sums which the insured may become legally liable to pay compensation in respect of:
▪ bodily injury to or illness of any person; and/or
▪ loss of or damage to property.

The insurer will only indemnify the insured on the condition that the accident occurred:
▪ during the period of insurance;
▪ within the places covered under the policy; and
▪ in the course of work or of the performance of any duties carried out by or on behalf of the insured in connection with the business or operations as stated in the policy schedule.

The policy also covers all costs and expenses of litigation:
▪ recovered by any claimant against the insured; and
▪ incurred with the written consent of the insurer in respect of a claim against the insured for compensation.

There are usually two limits of indemnity. Firstly, the limit of indemnity for any one period is usually unlimited in amount, although a limit may be placed on certain hazardous risks such as fire, explosion or pollution. Secondly, the limit of indemnity for any one accident or occurrence is the amount as specified in the policy schedule. Note that some policies may also have excess amounts imposed on them.
Some of the common provisions and conditions which can be found in a Public Liability Insurance policy are described below.

This clause states the maximum liability of the insurer in respect of any claim or any series of claims for personal injury and/or property damage caused by or arising out of one occurrence to the amount of indemnity as stated in the policy schedule.
It also specifies that all personal injury and/or property damage arising out of continuous or repeated exposure to the same general conditions substantially will be considered as one occurrence.

An excess is the amount which the insured has to bear for each and every claim that the insured makes under the Public Liability Insurance policy and the insurer is only liable for any claims in excess of the policy excess. A deductible is similar in that the insurer may deduct the amount of the deductible from any claim settlement.

The insured is required to take all reasonable precautions to prevent accidents and must comply with all statutory obligations. The insured has to ensure that the premises and plant are kept in a proper state of maintenance and repair.

This condition requires the insured to keep accurate records containing all particulars relating to the variables (e.g. payroll, sales, turnover) that are used to calculate the premium. The insured has to furnish the actual amount of the variables within one month from the expiry of the period of insurance and has to allow the insurer access to the information relating to the variables at all times.

This condition requires the insured to allow the insurer to go to the premises for inspection. It also specifies that, if there is any defect or danger being detected, the insurer will suspend the cover until the insured rectifies the matter to the satisfaction of the insurer.

subsections.label: B6
subsections.title: Insured Not To Commit Without The Insurer’s Consent

paragraphs.text: This condition requires that the insured shall not repudiate liability, negotiate or make any admission, offer, promise or payment in connection with any accident, occurrence or claim, without the written consent of the insurer. This condition also provides for the transfer of subrogation rights to the insurer.

This condition requires the insured to keep the insurer informed of any change to its nature of business, as this may increase the risk covered under his policy. The insurer can refuse to pay any claim made under such circumstances if the insurer is not notified of the change in risk.

Note that a detailed discussion on the policy provisions and conditions common to most general insurance policies are in the “Basic Insurance Concepts & Principles” Study Guide, published by the Singapore College of Insurance Limited.

Note that the underwriter may require additional information, and that the details of the required information may differ from insurer to insurer. For example, for complex risks or risks with high indemnity amounts, the insurer may send a risk surveyor to conduct a survey, before they can decide whether to accept the risk and, if so, on what terms and conditions.
Note that the exclusions of a Public Liability Insurance policy may vary from insurer to insurer.

Most Public Liability insurers allow extensions, such as those mentioned below, to be included so that policy coverage can be enhanced. These extensions may be subject to payment of additional premiums.
▪ car park
▪ defective sanitary installation
▪ employee’s effect
▪ first aid facilities
▪ food and drink
▪ swimming pool
▪ worldwide extension for executive on travel
▪ indemnity to directors and executives
▪ lifts, hoists and cranes
▪ loading and unloading of vehicles
▪ non-owned vehicles
▪ private works for directors and executives
▪ cross liability
▪ untenantability
▪ advertising and neon signs
▪ tenant’s and occupiers’ liability
▪ plant and machinery clause
A detailed description of the above-mentioned extensions can also be found in Appendix 3E.

In the event of a claim, the insured needs to submit a claim form. In addition, he has to keep the insurer informed of any development in the claim or proceedings instituted against him in connection with the claim.
One important point is that, if the insured has more than one policy covering the same risk, the insurer’s liability may be reduced owing to the application of contribution.

Claims of Liability Insurance are typically long tail claims. They are so called as they may be settled years after the original incident. For example, the degree of severity of a personal injury may be such that the final impact on the life of the person who suffers such injuries may not be known for many years. As such, the final level of damages to be awarded cannot be easily quantified until then.

Another important point is that the Public Liability Insurance policy is on an “Occurrence Basis”. Occurrence based claims refer to those, where the occurrence or event for which indemnity is sought under the policy, must occur within the policy period of insurance, i.e. between the dates of cover during which the policy is operative. This means that the coverage trigger of an occurrence form is bodily injury or property damage that occurs during the policy period of insurance.

For example, if the damage was caused by the insured on 15 December 2021, but the claim was lodged to the insurer only on 16 January 2022, the claim should be made on the policy covering the period during which the damage was actually done. If the insured’s current policy is for the period of insurance from 1 January 2022 to 31 December 2022, it would mean that the insured should claim under his previous policy which covered him for the period from 1 January 2021 to 31 December 2021. Figure 3.1 illustrates how an “Occurrence Basis” works under such a policy. Figure 3.1: Illustration Of “Occurrence Basis” Under A Public Liability Insurance Policy O ⚫ O ⚫ ⚫ O ⚫ ⚫ ⚫ O = Occurrence C=Claim

subsections.label: G
subsections.title: Personal Mobility Insurance (Extended For Commercial Use)

paragraphs.text: With effect from 1 December 2020, licensed device-sharing operators providing motorised devices for hire, and all businesses who employ or contract riders using active mobility devices on public paths must ensure that they are covered by third-party liability insurance against injury and death during the course of their work. In addition, the rule applies to licensed device-sharing operators that provide motorised devices for hire.

As commercial personal mobility device (PMD) users are exposed to a greater risk compared to personal PMD users due to their higher frequency and time spent on roads, it is mandatory for commercial PMD users to be insured.
These changes were announced in February 2020, as part of amendments to the Active Mobility Act to provide victims in any accident greater recourse to compensation.
Active mobility devices include bicycles, personal mobility devices (PMDs), power-assisted bicycles (PABs), motorised wheelchairs or mobility scooters.

A personal mobility device (PMD) is defined as a vehicle that is designed for use by one person on footpaths only and travelling within the speed limit of not more than 25 km/h; has one or more wheels that operate on single axis; is propelled by an electric motor attached to the vehicle or by human power; does not resemble a motor car or motorcycle. Bicycle includes pedal bicycle, pedal tricycle, and a power-assisted bicycle. Policy Effective Date Policy Expiry Date Policy Term (Period Of Insurance) Covered Covered Not Covered

An LTA approved policy is any third-party liability insurance policy extended to cover commercial use of PMD and meets all the following requirements: (a) A sum insured of at least $200,000 per claim; (b) An excess amount that does not exceed $500 per claim; and (c) Does not contain exclusions preventing the rider from (i) carrying loads or (ii) using a device that is not registered in the name of the rider (applicable only to electric scooters and power-assisted bicycles).

In September 2019, a food delivery company in Singapore launched a joint initiative with an insurer to provide third-party liability insurance for active mobility riders, which covers damages incurred which are over $500 and up to $1,000,000. Anything in excess will be subject to discussion. All active PAB, PMD and bicycle riders are covered under the third-party liability insurance.
However, this third-party liability insurance does not cover scenarios such as: (a) Accidentally falling and getting injured while on your bicycle; or (b) PAB exploding while charging it at home.

So, what happens if the business fails to ensure that its employees/outworkers are adequately covered by third-party liability insurance? Under Section 58A of the Active Mobility Act 2017, it is an offence for a business to fail to take all reasonable and practicable measures to ensure third-party insurance coverage under an approved policy. This offence carries a fine not exceeding $10,000, or imprisonment of up to 12 months or to both. Where the offender is not an individual, the penalty will be a fine not exceeding $20,000.

Products Liability Insurance is designed to cover legal liability for bodily injury to and/or damage to property of any third-party resulting from the products9 manufactured, retailed, sold, supplied or distributed by an insured. It can be issued as an extension to the Public Liability Insurance policy or as a standalone policy. The former is suitable for shops, restaurants or hotels. The latter is more suitable for manufacturers, distributors or wholesalers.
The liability of the insured can arise from tort or from contract. Examples include wrong labelling and defective containers.

Under Section 5(1) of the Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977 [UCTA] where goods are of a type ordinarily supplied for consumer use, no contractual term may exclude liability for loss arising out of the goods proving defective or from the negligence of the manufacturer or distributor. 9 Product means any tangible goods or property after it has left the custody and control of the manufacturer.

Section 5(1) of UCTA states that: “In the case of goods of a type ordinarily supplied for private use or consumption, where loss or damage – (a) arises from the goods proving defective while in consumer use; and (b) results from the negligence of a person concerned in the manufacture or distribution of the goods, liability for the loss or damage cannot be excluded or restricted by reference to any contract term or notice contained in or operating by reference to a guarantee of the goods.”

The policy indemnifies the insured against all sums which the insured may become legally liable to pay compensation in respect of:
▪ accidental bodily injury (including death or disease) to any person; and/or
▪ accidental loss of or damage to material property caused by anything harmful or defective in any products sold, supplied, repaired or treated by the insured in connection with his business.

The insurer will only indemnify the insured on the condition that the accident occurred:
▪ within the territorial limits as specified in the policy and
▪ before or during the period of insurance; or
▪ on or after the retroactive date as stated in the schedule of the policy, but the claim for the accident is being made only during the policy period of insurance.

The policy also covers all costs and expenses of litigation:
▪ recovered by any claimant against the insured; and
▪ incurred with the written consent of the insurer in respect of a claim against the insured for compensation.

Note that the liability of the insurer under a Products Liability Insurance policy is on an aggregate limit basis. This is to say that each claim paid will reduce the limit of liability during the period of insurance. The insurer will be discharged from its liability if the total claims paid during the same period of insurance add up to the limit of liability as specified in the schedule attached to the policy.

The policy provisions and conditions of a Products Liability Insurance policy are generally similar to those of a Public Liability Insurance policy as discussed in the earlier section of this chapter.
Note that the underwriter may require additional information and the details of the required information may differ from insurer to insurer.
As with the other types of insurance, the exclusions under a Products Liability Insurance policy may also vary from insurer to insurer.
The policy can be extended to include errors in design or formula, subject to stringent underwriting requirements.

In addition, the policy can be extended to cover risks owing to deficient advice or instructions given on the use of or storage of the product. Some insurers also offer product recall as an extension or as a standalone cover.

The Products Liability Insurance policy is usually on a “Claims Made Basis”. Claims Made claims refer to those where the claim must be made against the insured within the policy period of insurance, regardless of whether or not the occurrence or event also happened within that period. This is to say that the coverage trigger of a claims-made form is the making of a claim against the insured during the policy period of insurance. This means that the policy applies to claims made against the insured during the period of insurance rather than claims occurring during the policy term.

For example, a damage or injury occurred on 1 December 2021, but a claim was made only on 1 February 2022. In this case, the insurer that issued a policy covering the period of insurance from 1 January 2022 to 31 December 2022 would be responsible for paying the claim. Figure 3.2 gives an illustration of how a policy issued on a “Claims Made Basis” works. Figure 3.2: Illustration Of A “Claims Made Basis” Under A Products Liability Insurance Policy O ⚫ O ⚫ O ⚫ ⚫ O ⚫ ⚫ ⚫ O = Occurrence C=Claim Policy Effective Date Policy Expiry Date Policy Term (Period Of Insurance) Covered Covered Retroactive Date Not Covered Not Covered APPENDIX 3A WICI 2019 POLICY APPROVED WORK INJURY COMPENSATION ACT 2019 INSURANCE POLICY Work Injury Compensation Act 2019 This simple guide will bring you through the key things you need to know about Work Injury Compensation Act 2019 (WICA 2019) and the recommended steps that you should take in the event of a work-related accident. Key things you need to know: A Approved Work Injury Compensation Act 2019 Insurance Policy (commenced on/after 1 Jan 2021) 1. What is an approved Work Injury Compensation Act 2019 insurance policy and how is this different from other policies and previous practices? An approved Work Injury Compensation Act 2019 insurance policy, also known as the “WICI 2019” policy, is an annual policy issued by an MOM-designated insurer. This “WICI 2019” policy covers employer’s liabilities for compensation under WICA 2019. It is fully compliant with MOM’s compulsory terms, and does not contain any terms or extensions that derogate from these compulsory terms. MOM-designated Insurers will process all work injury claims (including death and permanent incapacity claims) under “WICI 2019” policies, in accordance with MOM’s prescribed guidelines and requirements. Similarly, employers are to provide all reasonable assistance to enable insurers to conduct such proceedings. 2. Mandatory sharing of data with MOM Under this new regime, your insurance policy data and claims data will be shared with MOM. Insurers will need to collect your company’s UEN, total payroll and total head count to submit to MOM. In addition, past claims data will be made available to MOM as well as MOM-designated insurers, for greater information transparency. Employers with good safety records would be able to enjoy lower premiums, while those with poor safety records would face significantly higher premiums. 3. How do I make a claim under the “WICI 2019” policy? Please report any work-related incidents within ten (10) days to MOM through MOM’s WSH Incident Reporting eService. When filing the Incident Report with MOM, you will be asked whether you are claiming under a WICA 2019 insurance policy that commenced on/after 1 Jan 2021. Please indicate so. Please concurrently report the matter to your Work Injury Compensation Insurer as well as your insurance intermediary if you had purchased your insurance policy from an agent, financial advisor or a broker. Your Insurer will contact you for further action and process the claim in accordance with MOM’s guidelines. All claims will be processed by default, unless withdrawn by the injured employee. 4. What happens if I cannot follow above steps? Employers must report any work-related incidents even if the ten (10) days reporting deadline has passed. You will be required to provide the reasons for late reporting in your incident report. Non-reporting is an offence. MOM-designated insurers are mandated to report any instances of non-reporting to MOM. Failure to report may also prejudice your claim under the insurance policy. B Changes in limits and coverage for work injury compensation 5. Changes in compensation and medical expenses limits The compensation limits under WICA 2019 have been updated as follows SPECIMEN ABC Insurance Company Limited 123 Happy Street S123455 Tel: 6123 4567 Fax:6765 4321 Email: chat@abc.com Website: www.abcinsurance.com Type Limits Before 1 Jan 2020 From 1 Jan 2020 Death Maximum S$204,000 S$225,000 Minimum S$ 69,000 S$ 76,000 Total Permanent Incapacity (PI) Maximum S$262,000 S$289,000 Minimum S$ 88,000 S$ 97,000 Medical Expenses S$ 36,000 or within 1 year from date of accident, whichever is reached first S$ 45,000 or within 1 year from date of accident, whichever is reached first 6. Expanded mandatory insurance coverage to non-manual employees From 1 Apr 2021, the mandatory insurance salary threshold for non-manual employees will be increased. Before 1 Apr 2020 From 1 Apr 2020 From 1 Apr 2021 Mandatory Insurance Salary threshold for non-manual employees S$1,600 S$2,100 S$2,600 Unless exempted, any employer who fails to insure himself in accordance with the Work Injury Compensation Act 2019 shall be guilty of an offence and shall be liable on conviction to a fine up to S$10,000 and/or to imprisonment for a term up to one year. The maximum fine for second or subsequent offences will be increased to S$20,000 w.e.f. 1 Sep 2020. 7. Scope of compensation to include light duties For accidents from 1 Sep 2020, employees on light duties will be compensated for any loss in earnings if the actual wages received during the light duty period are lower than their Average Monthly Earnings (or lower than 2/3 of the Average Monthly Earnings if medical leave and light duties have exceeded 14 days). For submission of light duty claims to insurers, please include itemised payslips for the corresponding months where light duty was awarded to the employees. A Changes in reporting of incidents 8. Compulsory reporting for any instances of medical leave or light duties issued for work accident From 1 Sep 2020, employers must report all incidents of work-related accidents with medical leave or light duties to MOM. B Others 9. What do I do if I have a complaint against my insurers for “WICI 2019” policy? MOM-designated insurers are required to comply with MOM’s guidelines for the processing of your claim under the Work Injury Compensation Act 2019. We will recommend that you enquire with your insurer for details. If the issue remains unresolved, you may wish to contact MOM or the Financial Industry Disputes Resolution Centre (FIDREC) for assistance. 10. What will happen to existing Work Injury policies? Existing WIC insurance policies that comply with WICA (Chapter 354) continue to be valid until the following dates: a. For policies that commenced before 1 Sep 2020: policy end date, or latest 31 Aug 2021 b. For policies that commenced between 1 Sep 2020 and 31 Dec 2020: policy end date, or latest 31 Dec 2021 Employers must obtain a “WICI 2019” policy thereafter. SPECIMEN APPROVED WORK INJURY COMPENSATION ACT 2019 INSURANCE POLICY 1. This policy (herein after called the ‘Policy’) is issued as an approved policy under the Work Injury Compensation Act 2019. INTERPRETATION 2. (1) References to “Act” in this Policy mean the Work Injury Compensation Act 2019, as may be amended from time to time. (2) References to “Legislation” in this Policy mean the Work Injury Compensation Act 2019 and any regulations made thereunder, as may be amended from time to time. (3) Words used in the Policy have the meanings given by the Legislation. (4) References to “Terms of this Policy” mean any terms, exceptions, conditions and warranties, and any memorandum if applicable, contained in or endorsed on this Policy, which are consistent with the compulsory terms prescribed under the Act. (5) The Insured refers to each insured specified in the Schedule, including the Policyholder, that is participating in the insurance plan under this Policy. (6) The Policyholder refers to the party executing the contract for itself and on behalf of all other Insured specified in the Schedule. (7) The Insured’s risk profile is the risk of accident or disease to any employee in the Insured’s employment, taking account of the Insured’s workforce, payroll numbers and other material information required to be stated in the Schedule. (8) References to “Relevant Injury” in this Policy mean death or personal injury - (a) sustained by an employee that is caused by an accident that - (i) arises out of and in the course of the employee’s employment with the Insured and (ii) occurs during the Period of Insurance; or (b) that results from a disease contracted in the circumstances mentioned in section 10(1) of the Act in respect of the employee’s employment with the Insured during the Period of Insurance. (9) References to “the employee’s employment with the Insured” in this Policy include work done by the employee for another person while the employee’s services are temporarily lent or let on hire by the Insured to that other person (as mentioned in section 3(2) of the Act). (10) References to “earnings” have the meaning given by the Act. (11) References to “Estimated Annual Earnings” in this Policy mean an amount, not less than the Past Annual Earnings of the Insured, declared by the Insured to be an estimate of the total earnings to be paid by the Insured (as well as by other employers and known to the Insured) during the 12 months starting on the Commencement Date of the Policy. (12) References to “Past Annual Earnings” of the Insured in this Policy mean the total of the monthly earnings paid by the Insured (as well as by other employers and known to the Insured) during the 12 months immediately before the Commencement Date of the Policy. (13) A word or expression to which a specific meaning has been attached in any part of this Policy or the Schedule shall bear such specific meaning wherever it may appear. 3. WHEREAS the Insured is carrying on the Business described in the Schedule, and has (a) submitted a Proposal to the ABC INSURANCE COMPANY LTD (hereinafter called the “Company”) for the insurance under this Policy and (b) paid or agreed to pay the premium stated in the Schedule as consideration for such insurance, this Policy incorporates the Schedule and the Proposal, which shall be read together as one contract. SPECIMEN 4. NOW if any employee described in the Schedule in the Insured's employment has a Relevant Injury the Company will, subject to Terms of this Policy, indemnify the Insured against all sums that the Insured shall be liable to pay under the Legislation in respect of that employee and will in addition pay all costs and expenses incurred by the Insured with the written consent of the Company. 5. In the event of the death of the Insured the Company will indemnify the Insured's legal personal representatives in accordance with the Terms of this Policy in respect of liability incurred by the Insured provided that such personal representatives shall observe, comply, fulfil and be subject to the Terms of this Policy as though they were the Insured insofar as the Terms of the Policy can apply. 6. PROVIDED ALWAYS that – (a) In the event of any change in the Legislation the Company reserves the right to cancel this Policy in accordance with clause 10(10) of this Policy or allow the Policy to remain in force and charge reasonable additional premium therefor; (b) The contents of the Proposal are deemed to be representations, not warranties, but where there is fraudulent nondisclosure or misrepresentation of the Nature of the Business or Job Category or Category of Employee in the Proposal, the Company may avoid the contract and refuse all claims. JURISDICTION 7. (1) This Policy is governed by the laws of the Republic of Singapore. (2) The indemnity under this Policy does not apply in respect of judgments delivered by or obtained from a court or tribunal of a jurisdiction outside Singapore. RECOVERY FROM INSURED 8. (1) Where the Company pays any amount under this Policy that an Insured is liable to pay under the Legislation, the Company shall have the right to recover from the Insured - (a) where there is a non-disclosure of any material fact which an Insured could reasonably be expected to have disclosed, or a deliberate or negligent misstatement of any material fact, the amount paid by the Company which is attributable to any Relevant Injury arising in relation to those non-disclosed or misstated material facts; (b) where the Insured causes a fraudulent claim to be brought, the amount paid by the Company on behalf of the Insured in respect of the fraudulent claim; (c) where the Insured breaches any obligation under clause 10 of this Policy, the amount paid by the Company on behalf of the Insured which is attributable to that breach. (2) For the avoidance of doubt — (a) material facts under clause 8(1)(a) of this Policy include but are not limited to the Nature of the Business or Job Category or Category of Employee required to be stated in the Schedule. (b) clause 8(1)(a) of this Policy does not confer any right of recovery where the amount paid is in relation to the employee’s activities that are incidental to the Job Category or Category of Employee stated in the Schedule or reasonably foreseeable to be carried out by an employee in the Job Category or Category of Employee stated in this Schedule. SPECIMEN EXCEPTIONS 9. (1) The Company shall not be liable in respect of (a) any sum which the Insured would have been entitled to recover from any party but for an agreement between the Insured and such party; (b) any death, disability, loss, damage, destruction, any legal liabilities, cost or expense including consequential loss of whatsoever nature, directly or indirectly caused by, resulting from or in connection with any of the following regardless of any other cause or event contributing concurrently or in any other sequence to the loss: (i) war, invasion, acts of foreign enemies, hostilities or warlike operations (whether war be declared or not), civil war, rebellion, revolution, insurrection, civil commotion assuming the proportions of or amounting to an uprising military or usurped power; or (ii) any act of terrorism including but not limited to - (A) the use of threat of force or violence; and/or (B) harm or damage to life or to property (or threat of such harm or damage) including, but not limited to, nuclear radiation and/or contamination by chemical and/or biological agents, by any person(s) or group(s) of persons, committed for political, religious, ideological or similar purposes, express or otherwise, and/or to put the public or any section of the public in fear; (c) any action taken in controlling, preventing, suppressing or in any way relating to clause 9(1)(b)(i) or (ii) of this Policy. (d) subject to clause 9(2) of this Policy, any liability of whatsoever nature directly or indirectly caused by or contributed to by or arising from — (i) nuclear weapons material; or (ii) ionising radiations or contamination by radioactivity from any nuclear fuel or from any nuclear waste from the combustion of nuclear fuel and for the purposes of this exception combustion shall include any selfsustaining process of nuclear fission; (e) any liability directly or indirectly caused by, arising out of or in any way connected with any claim against the Insured to the extent that the provision of any cover, or the payment of any claim or benefit hereunder would expose the Company, or its parent to any sanction, prohibition or restriction implemented pursuant to resolutions of the United Nations or the trade and economic sanctions, laws or regulations of Singapore, the European Union, United Kingdom, or United States of America; (f) any claims based upon or arising out of asbestosis and mesothelioma. (2) Clause 9(1)(d) of this Policy does not exclude any liability caused by or contributed to by or arising from radioactive isotopes, other than nuclear fuel, when such isotopes are being prepared, carried, stored, or used for commercial, agricultural, medical, scientific or other similar peaceful purposes. CONDITIONS 10. (1) Insofar as it is not prohibited by the Legislation, the Insured shall at all times observe, comply with and fulfil the Terms of this Policy. (2) The Policyholder warrants and shall be deemed to have the authority to enter into this Policy either as principal or where applicable as agent of all other Insured where applicable. The Policyholder also warrants and is deemed to have been authorised by all other Insured under this Policy to make such declarations or disclosures as the Company requires on their behalf. On receipt of this Policy, the Policyholder must provide a copy of the Policy and the Schedule to all other Insured to be insured by the Company under this Policy, and all Insured that are insured by the Company under this Policy will be deemed to have consented to the Terms of this Policy. SPECIMEN (3) Every notice or communication to be given or made under this Policy shall be delivered in writing to the Company. (4) The Insured shall take all reasonable precautions to prevent accidents and disease to the Insured's employees and shall comply with all statutory obligations and requirements. (5) In the event of the occurrence of any accident or any disease mentioned in section 10(1) of the Act that may give rise to a claim under this Policy, the Insured shall give notice of the occurrence to the Company with full particulars within the time required by the Legislation. (6) Every letter, claim, writ, summons and process relating to any accident or any disease mentioned in section 10(1) of the Act that may give rise to a claim under this Policy shall be notified or forwarded to the Company as soon as possible after receipt. Notice shall also be given to the Company as soon as possible after the Insured knows of any impending prosecution inquest or fatal inquiry in connection with any such accident or disease. (7) No admission, offer, promise, or payment shall be made by or on behalf of the Insured without the written consent of the Company. (8) The Company shall be entitled if it so desires to take over and conduct in the Insured's name the defence or settlement of any claim or to prosecute in the Insured's name for its own benefit any claim for indemnity or damages or otherwise and shall have full discretion in the conduct of any proceedings and in the settlement of any claim and the Insured must give such information and assistance as the Company may require. This does not affect any right of the Insured to participate in the resolution of disputes by the Commissioner in accordance with the Legislation. (9) The Insured must notify the Company immediately if the Nature of the Business or Job Category or Category of Employee as described in the Schedule has changed in such a way as to increase the risk of accident or disease to any employee in the Insured’s employment and at the latest within 14 days from the date of the change. The Insured must, in addition, specify in the notice the changes in the Nature of the Business or Job Category or Category of Employee and the date of the change. Where the Insured corrects an inaccuracy in the description of the Nature of the Business or Job Category or Category of Employee in the Schedule by notifying the Company of the change, the Company may adjust the premium to an amount reasonably payable for the Insured’s risk profile applicable to its proper description of the Nature of the Business or Job Category or Category of Employee. (10) The Company may cancel this Policy by giving 14 days' notice by registered letter to the Insured at his last known address; and provided no claim has arisen during the period during which the Policy had been in force the Company will return to the Insured the premium paid less the actual premium payable for the period during which the Policy had been in force subject to a minimum premium payment of $50.00 by the Insured. The Insured may cancel this Policy by giving 7 days’ written notice to the Company and provided no claim has arisen during the period during which the Policy had been in force the Insured shall be entitled to a return of premium paid less the actual premium payable for the period during which the Policy had been in force subject to any adjustment of premium required by the Terms of this Policy and subject to a minimum premium payment of $25.00 by the Insured. (11) Any dispute arising out of or in connection with this Policy, including any question regarding its existence, validity or termination, shall be referred to arbitration in accordance with Singapore arbitration laws. The seat of the arbitration shall be Singapore. The Tribunal shall consist of 1 arbitrator to be appointed by parties jointly, or, failing parties’ agreement on the arbitrator, appointed by the Singapore International Arbitration Centre. The language of the arbitration shall be English. The making of an Award by the Tribunal as herein before specified shall be a condition precedent to any right of action against the Company. (12) A person that is not a party to this Policy shall have no right under the Contracts (Rights of Third Party) Act 2001 to enforce any of its terms. SPECIMEN DATA GOVERNANCE 11. (1) The Insured agrees and gives consent for the Company to verify the following information about the Insured with governmental or regulatory authorities, for the purposes of processing, underwriting, administering and managing the Policy with the Company: (a) workforce size and aggregated payroll for all, or any class of employees; (b) number of compensation cases and amount of work injury compensation paid or payable for all, or any class of employees. (2) The Insured also consents to the collection, use, disclosure and dissemination of all information (including but not limited to information provided by the Insured related to the Policy to the Insured’s insurance intermediaries and the Company’s authorised agents and service providers) for purposes relating to or incidental to the Insured’s claims under the Policy or in accordance with the Legislation. PREMIUM ADJUSTMENT AND DECLARATION OF EARNINGS 12. (1) The premium payable by the Insured shall be based on the total amount of earnings paid by the Insured (as well as other employers and known to the Insured) to every employee in the Insured’s employment during the Period of Insurance. (2) If the total amount of earnings paid by the Insured (as well as other employers and known to the Insured) during the Period of Insurance differs from the total amount on which the premium was calculated at the commencement of this Policy, the difference in the premium shall be met by an additional payment or by a refund as the case may be, subject to a minimum premium payment of $50.00 by the Insured. (3) For the purpose of the premium adjustment, the Insured shall keep and maintain a proper record of the name and full personal particulars of every employee in the Insured’s employment together with the amount of earnings paid by the Insured (as well as other employers and known to the Insured) during the Period of Insurance and the Insured shall at all times allow the Company to inspect such records. (4) The Insured shall without demand and within a month after the end date or termination of this Policy, furnish the Company an account of all earnings paid by the Insured (as well as by other employers and known to the Insured) to every employee in the Insured’s employment during the Period of Insurance.If the Estimated Annual Earnings declared by the Insured are less than the Past Annual Earnings, the Insured may not be indemnified for the full extent of the Insured’s liability, as the Insured will be deemed to be his own insurer to the extent of the shortfall in the Estimated Annual Earnings declared and the Insured shall bear a rateable proportion of the liability accordingly. UNDERINSURANCE AND AVERAGE CONDITION 13. (1) If the Estimated Annual Earnings declared by the Insured are less than the Past Annual Earnings, the Insured may not be indemnified for the full extent of the Insured’s liability, as the Insured will be deemed to be his own insurer to the extent of the shortfall in the Estimated Annual Earnings declared and the Insured shall bear a rateable proportion of the liability accordingly. (2) In the event the Company is required to make any payment to the claimant by virtue of its obligations under the Legislation, the Company shall pay the claimant the compensation in full but reserves the right of recovery of the rateable proportion of the liability mentioned in clause 13(1). PREMIUM PAYMENT WARRANTY 14. (1) Despite anything in this Policy but subject to clause 14(2) of this Policy, it is hereby agreed and declared that if the period of insurance is 60 days or more, any premium due must be paid and actually received in full by the Company (or the intermediary through whom this Policy was effected) within 60 days of the Commencement Date of the Policy, Renewal Certificate or Cover Note. (2) In the event that any premium due is not paid and actually received in full by the Company (or the intermediary through whom this Policy was effected) within the 60-day period referred to above, then: (a) the cover under the Policy, Renewal Certificate or Cover Note is automatically terminated immediately after the expiry of the said 60-day period; (b) the automatic termination of the cover shall be without prejudice to any liability incurred within the said 60-day period; and SPECIMEN (c) the Company shall be entitled to a pro-rata time on risk premium subject to a minimum of $25.00. (3) If the period of insurance is less than 60 days, any premium due must be paid and actually received in full by the Company (or the intermediary through whom this Policy was effected) within the period of insurance. POLICY OWNERS’ PROTECTION SCHEME 15. This Policy is protected under the Policy Owners’ Protection Scheme, which is administered by the Singapore Deposit Insurance Corporation (SDIC). Coverage for this Policy under the Policy Owners’ Protection Scheme is automatic and requires no further action from the Insured. For more information on the types of benefits that are covered under the scheme as well as the limits of coverage, where applicable, please contact the Company or visit SDIC website (https://www.sdic.org.sg/). NO AVOIDANCE OF COMPULSORY TERMS 16. Nothing in this Policy (including the Schedule and the Proposal) or any memorandum or endorsement affects the compulsory terms under section 26 of the Act. ADDITIONAL BENEFITS The following Additional Benefits shall apply under this Policy and shall be subjected to the Terms, Exceptions and Conditions. 01 COMMON LAW ENDORSEMENT It is noted and agreed that this Policy is extended to indemnify the Insured against legal liability at Common Law, for Relevant Injury sustained by employees described in the Schedule Provided that (1) The Company's liability shall be limited to $10,000,000.00 for any one claim or series of claims arising out of one event.
