(2) The Company shall not be liable for any liability of the Insured to pay compensation to an employee or to the legal representatives or dependents of an employee by virtue of the Legislation. In addition, the following exclusion will apply: Communicable Disease Exclusion LMA5396 (1) Notwithstanding any provision to the contrary within this policy, this policy does not cover all actual or alleged loss, liability, damage, compensation, injury, sickness, disease, death, medical payment, defence cost, cost, expense or any other amount, directly or indirectly and regardless of any other cause contributing concurrently or in any sequence, originating from, caused by, arising out of, contributed to by, resulting from, or otherwise in connection with a Communicable Disease or the fear or threat (whether actual or perceived) of a Communicable Disease. (2) For the purposes of this endorsement, loss, liability, damage, compensation, injury, sickness, disease, death, medical payment, defence cost, cost, expense or any other amount, includes, but is not limited to, any cost to clean-up, detoxify, remove, monitor or test for a Communicable Disease. (3) As used herein, a Communicable Disease means any disease which can be transmitted by means of any substance or agent from any organism to another organism where: (3.1) the substance or agent includes, but is not limited to, a virus, bacterium, parasite or other organism or any variation thereof, whether deemed living or not, and (3.2) the method of transmission, whether direct or indirect, includes but is not limited to, airborne transmission, bodily fluid transmission, transmission from or to any surface or object, solid, liquid or gas or between organisms, and (3.3) the disease, substance or agent can cause or threaten bodily injury, illness, emotional distress, damage to human health, human welfare or property damage. SPECIMEN 02 COMPENSATION FOR MEDICAL TREATMENT ENDORSEMENT It is noted and agreed that the Company’s liability under the First Schedule paragraph 5(1)(b) of the Legislation is increased to $50,000.00 per accident per employee. 03 TO AND FROM WORK MEAL BREAKS EXTENSION (APPLICABLE TO NON-COMPANY PROVIDED TRANSPORT) It is noted that this Policy shall cover any injuries sustained by the employee in pursuance of or arising out of and in the course of his employment by the Insured whilst the employee on any working day: (i) is travelling between his place of residence in the Republic of Singapore to his place of employment or any other place for the purpose of his employment in the Republic of Singapore and vice versa; or (ii) is having a meal break within the Republic of Singapore other than his place of employment Provided always that such event giving rise to a claim under this Policy does not occur during or after any substantial interruption or deviation from the journey made for reasons or purposes unconnected with his employment which would ordinarily have materially added to the risk of injury. The Company's liability under this Policy shall be limited to - (a) $5,000.00 per employee in relation to bicycling or Portable Mobility Devices (PMD) (b) $10,000.00 per employee in relation to motorcycling (c) $25,000.00 per employee other than (a) and (b) (d) $250,000.00 in the aggregate during any one period of insurance 04 TRADITIONAL CHINESE MEDICAL TREATMENT FEES This Policy is extended to cover the cost of Traditional Chinese Medicine’s (TCM) medical treatment received by an employee in relation to his Relevant Injury arising out of and in the course of employment subject to: (a) The TCM practitioners must be registered with TCM Practitioners Board and render his medical services as prescribed in the TCM Practitioners Board (b) a sub-limit of $250.00 per accident and $20,000.00 in aggregate This extension limit forms part of the compensation for medical treatment under the Legislation. EXCEPTIONS TO ALL ADDITIONAL BENEFITS For the purposes of all Clauses/Endorsements providing additional coverage beyond the terms of this Policy, the Company shall not be liable in respect of: (1) any liability of the Insured which attaches by virtue of an agreement but which would not have attached in the absence of such agreement; (2) the insured's liability to employees of contractors engaged by the Insured; (3) any injury to any employee of the Insured resulting from an accident if it is proved that the injury to the employee is directly attributable to the employee having been at the time thereof under the influence of alcohol or a drug not prescribed by a medical practitioner; (4) any incapacity or death resulting from a deliberate self injury or the deliberate aggravation of an accidental injury. In addition, the following clause will apply: Non-Contribution Clause (applicable to all Additional Benefits) If at the time claim arises from any of these Additional Benefits, there be any other insurance covering the same liability, the Company shall not indemnify the Insured or contribute to any payment/compensation. IMPORTANT - The Insured is requested to read this Policy. If any error or misdescription be found, the Policy should be returned to the issuing office for correction. This policy (hereinafter called the “Policy”) is issued as an approved policy under the Work Injury Compensation Act 2019. Interpretation 2. (1 ) References to “Act” in this Policy mean the Work Injury Compensation Act 2019, as may be amended from time to time. (2) References to “the Legislation” in this Policy mean the Work Injury Compensation Act 2019 and any regulations made thereunder, as may be amended from time to time. (3) Words used in this Policy have the meanings given by the Legislation. (4) References to “Terms of this Policy” mean any terms, exceptions, conditions and warranties, and any memorandum if applicable, contained in or endorsed on this Policy, which are consistent with the compulsory terms prescribed under the Act. (6) The Policyholder refers to the party executing the contract for itself and on behalf of all other Insured specified in the Schedule. (8) References to “Relevant Injury” in this Policy mean death or personal injury — (a) sustained by an employee that is caused by an accident that — (i) arises out of and in the course of the employee’s employment with the Insured; and (ii) occurs during the Period of Insurance; or (b) that results from a disease contracted in the circumstances mentioned in section 10(1) of the Act in respect of the employee’s employment with the Insured during the Period of Insurance. (9) References to “the employee’s employment with the Insured” in this Policy include work done by the employee for another person while the employee’s services are temporarily lent or let on hire by the Insured to that other person (as mentioned in section 3(2) of the Act). (10) References to “earnings” have the meaning given by the Act. (11) References to “Estimated Annual Earnings” in this Policy mean an amount, not less than the Past Annual Earnings of the Insured, declared by the Insured to be an estimate of the total earnings to be paid by the Insured (as well as other employers and known to the Insured) during the 12 months starting on the Commencement Date of the Policy. (12) References to “Past Annual Earnings” of the Insured in this Policy mean the total of the monthly earnings paid by the Insured (as well as by other employers and known to the Insured) during the 12 months immediately before the Commencement Date of the Policy. In the event of the death of the Insured the Company will indemnify the Insured’s legal personal representatives in accordance with the Terms of this Policy in respect of liability incurred by the Insured provided that such personal representatives shall observe, comply with, fulfil and be subject to the Terms of this Policy as though they were the Insured insofar as the Terms of the Policy can apply. 6. (b) the automatic termination of the cover shall be without prejudice to any liability incurred within the said 60-day period; and (c) the Company shall be entitled to a pro-rata time on risk premium subject to a minimum of $25*. * The Insurer may substitute a lower amount. (3) If the period of insurance is less than 60 days, any premium due must be paid and actually received in full by the Company (or the intermediary through whom this Policy was effected) within the period of insurance+. + The Insurer may substitute a shorter period of not less than 14 days. Policy Owners’ Protection Scheme 15. This Policy is protected under the Policy Owners’ Protection Scheme, which is administered by the Singapore Deposit Insurance Corporation (SDIC). Coverage for this Policy under the Policy Owners’ Protection Scheme is automatic and requires no further action from the Insured. For more information on the types of benefits that are covered under the scheme as well as the limits of coverage, where applicable, please contact the Company or visit SDIC website (https://www.sdic.org.sg/). No Avoidance of Compulsory Terms 16. Nothing in this Policy (including the Schedule and the Proposal) or any memorandum or endorsement affects the compulsory terms under section 26 of the Act. SPECIMEN PART 2 SCHEDULE TO APPROVED POLICY Policy No. Entity Name (Insured) Business Registration No. (UEN) Address Nature of Business Insurance Policy Commencement Date Insurance Policy End Date Premium No. of Employees Job Category/ Category of Employee Estimated Annual Earnings (S$) Endorsements Applicable: SPECIMEN APPENDIX 3B Illustration Of How The Amount Of Compensation Payable To An Employee Is Arrived At An employee, aged of 40 years next birthday, while working in a building construction site was seriously injured on 1 July 2021 with the result that he became permanently and totally incapacitated (i.e. 100% loss of earning capacity). His monthly earnings in the 12 months immediately preceding the accident were as follows: Month Gross Monthly Earnings (Excluding Bonus) AWS/Bonus Paid During Past 12 Months July 2020 S$1,800.00 S$4,000.00 Aug 2020 S$1,800.00 Sep 2020 S$1,820.00 Oct 2020 S$2,000.00 Nov 2020 S$1,800.00 Dec 2020 S$1,820.00 Jan 2021 S$1,850.00 Feb 2021 S$1,880.00 Mar 2021 S$2,050.50 Apr 2021 S$1,909.50 May 2021 S$1,800.00 June 2021 S$1,870.00 Total S$22,400.00 S$4,000.00 The amount of Permanent Total Incapacity compensation to be arrived at as follows: The average monthly earnings = S$(22,400 + 4,000) / 12 = S$2,200 Amount of compensation = S$2,200 x 144 (see Note below) x 100% = S$316,800 Note: Refer to Section 2(a) of the First Schedule of the Work Injury Compensation Act 2019. In the above example, the employee would be entitled to a lump sum of S$316,800 compensation from his employer. However, this amount exceeded the maximum compensation that an employer was liable for (i.e. S$289,000 x 100%). As such, the employer would need to pay him only S$289,000, i.e. the maximum of his liability to his injured employee. Furthermore, as the employee suffered Permanent Total Incapacity, an additional 25% of the compensation amount would be awarded. Hence, the final amount of compensatory payment that the employee would receive should be: S$289,000 + (25% x S$289,000) = S$361,250 SPECIMEN APPENDIX 3C WORK INJURY COMPENSATION ACT 2019 WORK INJURY COMPENSATION INSURANCE REGULATION 2020 WORK INJURY COMPENSATION (INSURANCE) REGULATIONS 2020 CERTIFICATE OF INSURANCE [Regulation 4(1)] POLICY NUMBER: 1. Name of Insurer : 2. Name of Insured : 3. Date of Commencement : of the Policy 4. Date of Expiry of the Policy : I / We certify that the Policy to which this Certificate relates is issued in accordance with the provisions of the Work Injury Compensation Act 2019. ABC INSURANCE COMPANY LIMITED (Designated Insurer) (A member of the General Insurance Association of Singapore) Chief Executive AN EXTRACT FROM THE WORK INJURY COMPENSATION (INSURANCE) REGULATIONS 2020 5 (1) Where a certificate of insurance has been issued to an employer in accordance with regulation 4, the employer must display a copy of the certificate of insurance in accordance with paragraphs (2) and (3) throughout the period of validity of the insurance policy to which the certificate relates - (a) at the employer’s place of business; or (b) where the employer has more than one place of business, at each place of business at which the employer employs any employee whose claims may be the subject of an indemnity under the insurance policy to which that certificate relates. (2) The copies of certificates of insurance displayed for the purposes of paragraph (1) must be displayed in a manner that is easily seen and read by every employee whose claims may be the subject of indemnity under the insurance policy to which the certificate relates. (3) Copies of certificates of insurance must be displayed at the places mentioned in paragraph (1)(a) or (b) only during the period of validity of the insurance policy to which the certificate relates. (4) In this regulation, the period of validity of an insurance policy is the period - (a) starting on the date of commencement of the insurance policy; and (b) ending on the date of expiry or cancellation of that insurance policy. (5) A person who contravenes paragraph (1) shall be guilty of an offence and shall be liable on conviction - (a) to a fine not exceeding $2,000; or (b) if the person is a repeat offender, to a fine not exceeding $4,000. Policy Owners' Protection Scheme This policy is protected under the Policy Owners' Protection Scheme which is administered by the Singapore Deposit Insurance Corporation (SDIC). Coverage for your policy is automatic and no further action is required from you. For more information on the types of benefits that are covered under the scheme as well as the limits of coverage, where applicable, please contact Income or visit the GIA/LIA or SDIC web-sites (www.gia.org.sg or www.lia.org.sg or www.sdic.org.sg).

chapters.title: LIABILITY AND CONTINGENCY INSURANCE PART II
learningOutcomes: know what Professional Indemnity Insurance is all about
understand the scope of cover, policy provisions and conditions, underwriting information required, exclusions, extensions and claim procedure of Professional Indemnity Insurance
know what Directors’ and Officers’ (D&O) Liability Insurance is all about
know what Libel and Slander Insurance is all about
know what Errors and Omission (E&O) Insurance is all about
know what Carriers and Bailees Liability Insurance is all about
understand the underwriting information required and claims of Carriers and Bailees Liability Insurance
know what Commercial General Liability Insurance is all about
know what Innkeeper’s Liability Insurance is all about
know what Specialist Liability Risk Insurance is all about
understand the coverage, exclusions, underwriting information and claims procedures related to Contingency Insurance

paragraphs.text: In this part of the chapter, we will discuss other classes of Liability Insurance such as Professional Indemnity Insurance, Directors’ & Officers’ (D&O) Liability Insurance, Libel & Slander Insurance, Errors & Omission (E&O) Insurance and Carriers & Bailees Liability Insurance. We will also cover Commercial General Liability Insurance and Innkeeper’s Liability Insurance before examining Specialist Liability Risks Insurance. In the final part of this chapter, we shall discuss Contingency Insurance.

Professional Indemnity Insurance policy is designed to protect professionals, such as accountants, architects, dentists, doctors, engineers, lawyers, surgeons, and even insurance brokers and financial adviser representatives, against claims which may be made against them. The policy covers these professionals against their liability to their principals or clients, or their principal’s clients (third parties) arising out of negligence, omission or error in the discharge of their professional duties. The cover can be made for an individual employee or for the firm.

Besides arising out of negligence, omission or error in the discharge of their professional duties, there are Professional Indemnity policies that cover “wrongful acts” i.e. wider in that they cover negligence, as well as other acts, errors, omissions, misstatement and misleading statements, and even breach of duty whether these are committed, attempted or allegedly committed or attempted.

As required under Section 77 of the Insurance Act 1966, an insurance broking company, apart from meeting the minimum paid-up share capital requirement, must have in force a Professional Indemnity Insurance policy under which a person is indemnified in respect of the liabilities arising out of and in the course of his business as an insurance broker. Details are specified in the Insurance (Intermediaries) Regulations.

The policy covers both actual and alleged negligent acts, errors or omissions. In other words, it is a negligence policy and does not provide cover for claims brought about by wilful, dishonest, fraudulent, criminal or malicious acts or omissions. As mentioned above, besides arising out of negligence, omission or error in the discharge of their professional duties, there are PI policies that cover “wrongful acts” i.e. wider cover in that they cover negligence, as well as other acts, errors, omissions, misstatement and misleading statements, and even breach of duty whether these are committed, attempted or allegedly committed or attempted.

In addition, the policy also makes provision for the payment of legal costs and expenses in the defence of a claim. Some policy limits are on a costs inclusive basis while others may be on a costs exclusive basis. If the claim exceeds the limit of the cover, the insured is expected to bear part of these costs and expenses.
The limit of liability is an aggregate limit for any one period of insurance.
There is no standard policy form available. The policy wording varies from insurer to insurer and also from profession to profession.
Note that the underwriter may require additional information and the details of the required information may differ from insurer to insurer.
The period of insurance for a Professional Indemnity Insurance policy is usually 12 months.

Professional Indemnity Insurance policies carry certain exclusions. Apart from the standard market excluded perils, such as war and nuclear related perils, the main ones are as follows:
▪ claims that should rightfully fall under a Public Liability Insurance policy, Products Liability Insurance policy, or other specific types of liability policies;
▪ libel and slander; and
▪ loss of money and/or securities by theft, embezzlement, misappropriation, act of fraud or dishonesty by any of the insured’s employees.

Like Products Liability Insurance, this policy is also issued on a “Claims Made Basis” which means that the insurer will admit a claim that is made during the period of insurance, regardless of when the act of negligence giving rise to the claim has taken place as long as it is on or after the retroactive date (if any) as stated in the policy. 3. DIRECTORS’ & OFFICERS’ (D&O) LIABILITY INSURANCE

Directors’ & Officers’ (D&O) Liability Insurance is designed to indemnify the insured (a corporation or insured person) for the amount that it/he becomes legally bound to pay for claims made against it/him for wrongful act(s) committed or allegedly committed during the period of insurance of the policy.

The D&O Liability Insurance policy has three sections nowadays, namely: (a) Side A: Directors’ & Officers’ Cover This pays on behalf of each of the individual directors or officers all losses for which the director or officer is not indemnified by the company, and for which the director or officer becomes legally obligated to pay on account for any claim first made against him during the policy period, for a wrongful act occurring before or during the policy period. (b) Side B: The Company Reimbursement Cover This pays on behalf of the company all losses which the company grants to each director or officer indemnification (as permitted or required by law) which the director or officer has become legally obligated to pay on account of any claim first made against him during the policy period, for a wrongful act occurring before or during the policy period. (c) Side C: The Entity Cover For Securities Claims And/Or Employment Practice Claims This provides legal liability protection for the company for employmentrelated and/or securities-related claims.

The term “wrongful act” includes any actual or alleged negligent act, error, omission, misstatement, misleading statement, neglect or breach of duty by the director or officer acting in his official capacity in the insured corporation. An insured person includes the past, present or future director, secretary, officer of the corporation or any natural person, who by virtue of any applicable legislation or law, is deemed to be a director or officer of the corporation.

Section 172 of the Companies Act 1967 referred to as [CA] in this chapter states that provisions purporting to exempt any director or officer of a company from liability in connection with any negligence, default, breach of duty or breach of trust in relation to the company is void. It also states that companies may not indemnify directors or officers against such liabilities, except as permitted under Section 172A or 172B of CA.

Section 172B of CA allows a company to indemnify a director or officer against liability incurred by the director or officer to a person other than the company, i.e. third parties. Section 172(B)(1)(a) of CA goes on to state that such indemnity cannot be provided for: (a) payment of fines in criminal proceedings; (b) payment of penalties in respect of regulatory non-compliance; (c) defending criminal proceedings where the officer is convicted; and (d) defending civil proceedings brought by the company or a related company in which judgment is given against the officer.
However, Section 172A of CA allows a company to purchase and maintain insurance against liability for its directors or officers.

Like Products Liability Insurance and Professional Indemnity Insurance, a D&O Liability Insurance policy is also issued on a “Claims Made Basis”, and the period of insurance is usually 12 months. As such, the insurer will admit a claim that is made during the period of insurance, regardless of when the wrongful act giving rise to the claim has been committed as long as it is on or after the retroactive date (if any) as stated in the policy.

D&O Liability Insurance is often confused with Professional Indemnity Insurance. The difference is that Professional Indemnity Insurance covers an insured’s liability for breach of professional duty, while D&O Liability Insurance covers the individual director’s or officer’s liability for wrongful act by the director or officer acting in his official capacity in the insured corporation as described above.

The policy for the insurance can be issued as a normal stand-alone policy, but more often, it is given as an extension of cover to a Professional Indemnity policy. The period of insurance is usually 12 months. The insurance is normally on a “Claims Made Basis”.

Errors & Omissions (E&O) Insurance policies cover persons with E&O liability exposure, as opposed to professional liability exposure. Professional liability tends to apply to professions demanding a very high standard of care, many years of study, and a formal licensing process, e.g. heart surgeons. On the other hand, E&O liability refers to vocations where performance, formal study and licensing standards are applicable, but less rigorous, e.g. council members of management committees of social clubs and condominiums. Although these council members may offer their services for free, the law does not exempt them if they are found to have failed in their duty of care. Inaccurate advice, delays, not taking the correct action can result in them being sued jointly or severally.

Therefore, E&O Insurance policies will indemnify the insureds against their legal liability to pay damages and claimants’ costs and expenses arising out of neglect, errors or omissions committed or alleged to have been committed by them in their capacity as members of the council. They also indemnify the insured for their own legal costs as well.

Bailees Liability Insurance policies are purchased by businesses who have in their care, custody or control of personal property that belongs to someone else either in their premises or when they are in the process of delivering them to their destination. For example, a warehouse will need a Bailees Liability Insurance policy (e.g. Warehousemen’s Liability) to cover its liability for damage or loss of the cargoes, stocks, etc., that it stores for its clients. Another example will be a transport company which buys a Bailees Liability Insurance policy to cover its liability for loss of or damage to the goods belonging to its clients during delivery.

This policy is also known as Carriers Legal Liability Insurance and is commonly underwritten by the Marine Insurance department of an insurance company. The policy basically covers losses to the clients’ property in the custody of a bailee, as well as all costs and expenses of litigation recovered from the insured by the claimant. Most policies in this class admit a claim only if the insured is legally liable for the loss.
Note that the underwriter may require additional information and the details of the information requested may vary from insurer to insurer.

This policy may be offered on a “Claims Made Basis” or an “Occurrence Basis”. In either case, it is imperative that the insured must promptly notify the insurer of any and every incident that occurs. So long as this is done, any incident that occurs within the policy period of insurance will, subject to all other terms and conditions of the policy, be covered. This is because such a policy usually provides that, if during the period of insurance, the insured becomes aware of any fact or circumstance that may give rise to a claim under the policy and elects to give notice in writing to the insurer of such fact or circumstance, then any claim which may subsequently arise out of such fact or circumstance shall be deemed to be a claim made during the period of insurance. This is provided that such written notice is given to the insurer during the period of insurance.
As with the other classes of liability insurance, the insured must file the claim by submitting a completed claim form with relevant supporting documents to the insurer.

In addition, this policy provides for the payment of medical expenses incurred as a result of bodily injury caused by accidents, subject to the limits as stated in the policy. It usually also offers retroactive cover, i.e. cover extended to a specified number of years before the inception of the policy. This cover will protect the insured in case there are occurring injuries or damage, but for which no claims have yet been lodged. This cover is granted, subject to payment of an additional premium by the insured to the insurer. 1 Advertising injury refers to the legal liability which arises as a result of the content of an advertisement.

The policy can be issued either on a “Claims Made Basis” or “Occurrence Basis”. A limit of indemnity is imposed on each specific liability cover, subject to an aggregate limit for the policy period of insurance. Usually, a general policy is issued, and specific liability cover is given by means of attachment, declaration or endorsement.

The territorial limit, as well as jurisdiction, of this policy is usually on a worldwide basis. This policy is usually purchased by multi-national companies and conglomerates, with diversified operations or business interests in various parts of the world. 8. INNKEEPER’S LIABILITY INSURANCE

Innkeeper’s Liability Insurance is designed to provide cover for the legal liability of the innkeeper or insured (i.e. the owner or operator of any lodging facility, such as hotel, inn, motel and restaurant) under Section 3 of the Innkeepers Act 1970 [“the Act”] for property loss or damage sustained by its guests, while such property is within the premises or in the possession of the insured.

Section 3 of the Act limits the innkeeper’s liability for any loss of or damage to goods or property brought to the inn to an amount not exceeding S$500 provided that a Notice of Section 3 of the Act, printed in plain type in English, is displayed in a prominent part of the hall or entrance of the inn.
The Act also grants the innkeeper the right to refuse to receive for safe custody, any goods or property of any guest where the declared value exceeds S$5,000.

However, the limitation does not apply where:
▪ the goods or property having been stolen, lost or damaged through the wilful act, default or neglect of the innkeeper, or any servant in his employment;
▪ the goods or property having deposited by or on behalf of the guest expressly for safe custody with the innkeeper or his manager; or
▪ the goods or property with declared value not exceeding S$5,000 having been refused safe custody by the innkeeper/manager, or where the guest being unable to deposit the goods or property with the innkeeper owing to the default of the innkeeper/manager.
According to the Act, the innkeeper is not liable for loss of or damage to any vehicle or any property left therein, or any horse or other live animal, or any gear (e.g. harness) connected with it.

Innkeepers Liability cover is often issued as an extension to the Public Liability Insurance policy if the insured is a hotel. This is to overcome the exclusion in a Public Liability insurance policy which specifically excludes property under the care, custody or control of the insured, but not belonging to the insured. Hotel

When this extension is granted, the insurer usually specifies certain conditions which the insured has to fulfil. Some common conditions include:
▪ displaying a copy of the Notice stated under Section 3 of the Innkeepers Act 1970 conspicuously in a place, where it can conveniently be read by the insured’s guests and customers at or near the reception office or desk, or near the main entrance of the inn or hotel;
▪ maintaining proper working order of all locks to its bedroom doors, with a set of keys to be given to each guest; and
▪ depositing immediately in a safe deposit box all valuables and issuing a receipt to the guest who deposits the valuables.

Pollution or Contamination is typically split into three categories:
▪ Air;
▪ Water; and
▪ Land (soil and groundwater).

An organisation faces significant liability exposures relating to bodily injury and third-party clean-up costs in the event of a pollution incident. In addition, the costs associated with the first-party clean-up owing to a pollution incident can be substantial.

The recent acts of terrorism around the world have posed a very serious security concern to governments, the public and organisations. The prevention methods to mitigate and potentially avert such terrorist acts include CCTV, security checks at airports, security checks in office blocks and hotels, 24-hour security guards and patrols, and perimeter fencing, just to name a few. It is important to understand that these measures have been put in place as there is a duty of care owed. Any lapse of this duty of care suggests that there has been a level of negligence. Terrorism Liability Insurance looks to protect against this negligence.

A Terrorism Liability Insurance policy indemnifies organisations in respect of their operations, for the liability imposed upon them by law for damages in respect of a claim, arising out of third-party bodily Injury and / or property damage and / or removal of debris resulting solely and directly from an act or acts of terrorism.

A Cyber-attack occurs when individuals or whole organizations employ any means that target computer information systems, infrastructures, computer networks, and/or personal computer devices. Such attacks are achieved via various malicious acts originating from anonymous sources that either steals, alters, or destroys a specified target by hacking into a susceptible system. Cyber-attacks can range from installing spyware on a PC to attempts to destroy the infrastructure of entire nations. Cyber-attacks have been well publicised as reflected in a number of highprofile incidents happening over the past few years. As such, there is a need for organisations to put in place suitable means to manage and mitigate these cyber risks.

A Cyber Liability insurance policy in general is designed to cover a wide range of exposure including “third-party” legal liability claims arising out of business use of the Internet or computer system whether for marketing or e-commerce purposes, as well as “first-party” financial and even reputation loss suffered by the company, in the event of a cyber-attack. The insured events are defined to include loss of data, transmission of virus, denial of service, failure of computer hardware, network security breach, privacy breach, cyber extortion, disparagement, plagiarism, and intellectual property infringement.

A cancellation of an event may result in event organisers facing very serious financial consequences. Consider the following scenarios:
▪ An international pop star is involved in a car accident just before the start of a world tour and misses a series of concerts.
▪ A music festival has to be cancelled when the venue becomes hazardous owing to certain circumstances.
▪ A major earthquake or flood occurs rendering a hall or venue damaged and therefore unable to host a major sporting event such as boxing.

One can easily imagine how the above scenarios can leave the organisers and sponsors with significant costs and losses of revenues from the cancellation or abandonment of such events. The organisers will face serious consequences such as contractual obligations, ticket refunds and lost revenues.

Event organisers may have Liability insurance. However, Liability Insurance often do not cover financial exposures, such as contractual liabilities, lost revenues and ticket refunds. This is where Contingency Insurance plays a vital role in covering such exposures.

Insurers will require detailed information. We will not list all such information, but examples include:
▪ Details of the Proposer including business and track record and the Performer(s) including type of event, experience, scale of production etc.
▪ Date(s) and Name of Venue(s) of Performance(s) or Event(s).
▪ Details of Coverage being sought i.e. death, sickness, travel delay, venue damage etc
▪ History of non-appearance of insured persons (if any).
▪ Details of written contracts having been signed for venue or appearance
▪ Details of necessary licences, visas and permits and authorisations.
▪ Claims history or incidents that could have resulted or did result in financial loss that would be covered under the proposed insurance
▪ Known circumstances i.e. insured’s awareness of any matter, fact, circumstance or incident existing or threatened that can possibly affect the performance(s) or event(s) and may result in a claim under the proposed insurance.

There are many exclusions in a Contingency Insurance policy. Examples of such exclusions include:
▪ The insured’s or any insured person's lack of care, diligence or prudent behaviour, the result of which will increase the risk, and / or likelihood of a loss, hereunder.
▪ Suicide or self-inflicted injury of an insured person.
▪ Any contractual dispute or breach by the insured or any insured person or participant.
▪ Changes to an insured event without the prior approval of the insurers.
▪ Adverse weather in respect of any insured event in the open or under canvas or in a temporary structure, unless agreed by the insurers in writing.
▪ The insured or any insured person failing to comply with any applicable laws, contracts, authorisations, allowances for travel time etc.
▪ Any order for repatriation, internment, imprisonment, deportation or the refusal of permit to enter any country where the insured event is to be held.
▪ Lack of or inadequate attendance or insufficient interest prior to the date and time scheduled for the insured event.
▪ Any communicable disease, unless such communicable disease infects an insured person and such infection of an insured person is the sole and direct cause of the necessary cancellation, abandonment, postponement, interruption, curtailment or relocation of any insured event.
▪ Fear or threat of any communicable disease.

It should be noted that not all exclusions are cast in stone. It was reported in the press on 3 March 2020 that “global insurers face a hefty bill (billions of dollars!) if the coronavirus outbreak forces the cancellation of the Summer Olympics in Tokyo…while diseases like the coronavirus are often excluded from standard event cancellation insurance, Alli MacLean, the global product leader (contingency) at German insurers Allianz said ‘the majority of large event policyholders would purchase communicable disease coverage’.”2 2 Straits Times report dated 2 March 2020

The insured or policyholder is to notify insurer as soon as possible if an event is threatened or must be cancelled. Insurers will appoint loss adjusters to handle such claims. The loss adjusters can recommend ways to mitigate the financial and reputation impact. For example, an event which has been cancelled could be rescheduled.
In addition, the insured is required to furnish the insurer as much information as possible about the incident and financial implications.

chapters.title: COMMERCIAL MOTOR INSURANCE
outline: Types Of Commercial Motor Vehicle Insurance Coverage
learningOutcomes: understand the statutory requirements pertaining to compulsory motor insurance in Singapore
know the need for Commercial Motor Vehicle Insurance
understand the four main types of Commercial Motor Vehicle Insurance coverage sold by insurers in Singapore: - Act Liability Only - Third-Party Only - Third-Party, Fire & Theft - Comprehensive

understand the purpose and functions of each of the following Commercial Motor Insurance documents: - Proposal Form - Certificate of Insurance - Cover Note - Policy Document - Endorsements - Renewal Notice - Claim Form
know what Commercial Motor Vehicle Insurance is all about
understand the scope of cover, policy provisions and conditions, exclusions, extensions, underwriting information required and claims of Commercial Motor Vehicle Insurance
know what the Motor Claims Framework (MCF) is all about
know what Motor Fleet Insurance is all about
understand how motor fleets are rated
understand the need for Motor Fleet Insurance
know what Motor Trade Insurance is all about
