paragraphs.text:
In this chapter, we will cover the following three types of Marine Insurance which is governed by the Marine Insurance Act 1906 adopted from the English Marine Insurance Act 1906, an Act to codify the law relating to Marine Insurance:
▪ Marine Cargo Insurance;
▪ Marine Hull Insurance; and
▪ Marine Liability Insurance.
We will also touch on Aviation Insurance briefly in this chapter.
It should be noted that the Marine Insurance Act 1906 uses the term “assured” instead of “insured” (to refer to the policyholder/policy owner) as commonly used in this text.
Before we study the types of Marine insurance, let us firstly examine the types of vessels as well as the nature of cargoes they carry. We shall also briefly discuss the parts of a vessel.

sections.title: Types Of Vessels And The Cargoes Carried

paragraphs.text: There are various types of commercial vessels and they come in all shapes and sizes designed to carry a wide variety of cargoes. A study of marine insurance is never complete without first understanding the type of vessels that being insured.

It is not necessary to know everything about the ships covered by an insurance contract. However, a marine insurance underwriter should recognise the various types of vessels he is asked to insure. He needs to have a good knowledge of the hazards faced by ships, the functions of different ships and how such ships are built to withstand these hazards.

The types of vessels commonly insured and their cargoes carried, or usage are shown in the table below. Types of Vessels Cargoes Carried/Usage Dry Cargo Ships They carry traditional dry cargoes or general cargoes Bulk Carriers They are designed to carry dry cargoes with high weight to cost ratios i.e. they are relatively cheap on a per weightage basis. Examples of such cargoes include coal, grain and ore. General Cargo Vessels They are used to carry cargoes in the form of pallets or bags. These are known as breakbulk cargoes which require specialised handling facilities. Container Vessels Containers are versatile in their usage and they can carry goods such as foodstuffs, electrical equipment, automobiles etc. They can also be used to carry bagged and palletised goods, liquids, refrigerated cargo and other types of cargo. Reefer Vessels Perishable goods such as fruit or meat require special care and controlled temperatures in their transit. A reefer vessel is a ship designed to carry refrigerated cargo. Ro-Ro Vessels They carry road vehicles such as cars, trucks etc. Liquid Cargo Ships These are commonly called “tankers” which carry liquid cargoes. Crude Carriers They carry crude oil and transport it to refineries around the world. Liquefied Gas Carriers They are designed to carry Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG) or Liquefied Natural Gas (LNG) under pressure. Passenger Vessels Passengers. Livestock Carriers These ships are designed to carry animals. Heavy-Lift/Project Cargo Vessels These vessels are used to carry extremely heavy or bulky objects such as other ships and large industrial components. Tugs They are often used in the berthing, unberthing and movement of large or less manoeuvrable vessels within port limits. Tugs are also used to tow barges not to mention moving large structures such as offshore platforms and floating storage units.
While ships may vary in size and types, they all have two points in common.

Firstly, ships are made of steel and composite material. Secondly, they comprise three main parts:
▪ Navigation bridge
▪ Hull
▪ Engine room

One can find both the visible and invisible structural parts in a ship. The parts that are visible comprise:
▪ Rudder
▪ Anchor
▪ Bow
▪ Keel
▪ Accommodation
▪ Propeller
▪ Mast
▪ Bridge,
▪ Hatch covers and
▪ Bow thrusters.

The invisible but structural part of ship consists of:
▪ bulkheads
▪ frames
▪ cargo holds
▪ hopper tank
▪ double bottom
▪ girders
▪ cofferdams, side shell etc.
A virtual reality video of the interior and parts of a vessel can be found at: https://dipen1489.github.io/shipdeck/index.html

Goods of all descriptions are constantly being transported not only from one country to another, but also within a country. The goods may be carried by road, rail, sea or air. They are exposed to hazards normally associated with such transportation. Therefore, Marine Cargo Insurance provides the insured with indemnity for loss of or damage to the goods during transit.

A Marine Cargo Insurance policy includes cover for land transit commencing from the moment the goods leave the place of storage until they arrive at the final warehouse. For administrative convenience, most insurers cover air and sea cargo, inland transit, parcel post and carryings by couriers within the Marine Cargo Department.

For the above reasons, the term “Marine Cargo Insurance” is often loosely used to refer to all forms of Cargo Insurance, regardless of how the cargoes are transported. It is sometimes said that this type of insurance should be more appropriately called “Transit Insurance”.

subsections.label: B
subsections.title: The Need For Marine Cargo Insurance

paragraphs.text: Modern trade and commerce cannot function without Marine Cargo Insurance. The movement of raw materials and manufactured goods between countries and the internal movement within the country itself will involve credit and cash transactions worth millions of dollars. Serious loss or damage to a cargo can create financial difficulties for the buyer, seller or any other party having an interest in the goods. Thus, such contingencies need to be protected against via some form of transportation insurance, essentially Marine Cargo Insurance.
In a usual sales transaction, it is important that both the seller and the buyer know which party will be effecting insurance on the goods during transit.

In today’s transaction, the introduction of a trader/middleman makes the issue of effecting insurance even more complicated. The job of a trader is to buy from one party in order to sell to another. In the process, he either makes a commission or makes from the difference in the selling and buying price. Care must be taken in handling these transactions, especially where the sales are done under letters of credit which call for policies to be taken out in a certain way. As such, the seller will rather not let the buyer know the source of the goods or the original buying price.
We will now look at the INCOTERMS as stipulated under the “Terms Of Sale” – a document that shows, among other things, which party should take up the Marine Cargo Insurance cover.

INCOTERMS are key elements of international contracts of sale, since they tell the parties what to do with respect to:
▪ carriage of the goods from seller to buyer; and
▪ export and import clearance. They also explain the division of costs and risks between the parties.

INCOTERMS deal with the rights and obligations of the parties to a sale, i.e. the seller or buyer. They show at which stage of the transit that the interest in the cargo lies with the seller, and at which stage that the interest is transferred to the buyer. The party who has interest in the cargo at a particular stage of the transit is entitled and certainly encouraged to take up an insurance cover, to protect his interest in the cargo. However, there is no compulsion for either the seller or the buyer to take up the insurance cover with the exception of CIF which we will discuss later.

Examples of INCOTERMS are “Ex Works”, “FOB”, “CFR” (formerly known as “C&F”) and “CIF”. From an insurance perspective, the principal difference concerns the time when the risk in the goods has been passed from the seller to the buyer.

We will discuss the meanings of three of the most commonly used terms, namely FOB, CIF and CFR. However, we will concern ourselves only with the insurance implications of these terms of sale. (a) Free On Board (FOB) When goods are sold FOB, it means that the seller is only responsible for bringing the cargo from his premises to the port, and his responsibility ceases when it is safely loaded on board the designated vessel. Any loss or damage occurring up to this stage is the responsibility of the seller. Crossing the “ship’s rail” is the exact point at which interest passes from the seller to the buyer. Once the cargo is loaded on board the vessel, interest in the cargo passes over to the buyer who will then need to effect his own insurance to protect the cargo against loss or damage at this stage, until it arrives at his own premises. (b) Cost, Insurance And Freight (CIF) CIF means that the seller delivers the goods on board the vessel or procures the goods already so delivered. The risk of loss of or damage to the goods passes when the goods are on board the vessel. The seller must contract for and pay the costs and freight necessary to bring the goods to the named port of destination. The seller also contracts for insurance cover against the buyer’s risk of loss of or damage to the goods during the carriage. The buyer should note that under CIF the seller is required to obtain insurance only on minimum cover. Should the buyer wish to have more insurance protection, it will need either to agree as much expressly with the seller or to make its own extra insurance arrangements.1 (c) Cost & Freight (CFR) A contract on CFR (formerly known as C&F) is similar to a FOB contract in that the goods are still at the risk of the seller until they pass the ship’s rail. From this point onwards, interest in the goods will rest with the buyer. Therefore, the buyer can effect Marine Cargo Insurance to protect the goods. In the case of the CFR contract, the responsibility for arranging and paying for the carriage of the cargo on board the overseas vessel is borne by the seller. In contrast, for the FOB contract, the responsibility and payment are borne by the buyer.

Table 5.1 summarises which party should insure the cargo under the three different trading terms. Table 5.1: The Party To Insure Cargo Under CIF, FOB & CFR Trading Terms CIF FOB CFR Seller/Exporter To Insure ✓ Buyer/Importer To Insure ✓ ✓ 1 Source: https://incodocs.com/blog/wp-content/uploads/2020/03/IncoDocs-Trade-Guide-March-2020.pdf
The MAR Form which is an abbreviation for “Marine Form” does not have any terms and conditions on it. Rather, it is simply the schedule of the policy which contains the following information:

Attached to the MAR Form is one of the following Institute Cargo Clauses (ICC) which specifies the terms and conditions of a Marine Cargo Insurance policy:
▪ Institute Cargo Clauses A (ICC A);
▪ Institute Cargo Clauses B (ICC B); or
▪ Institute Cargo Clauses C (ICC C).
The most recent Institute Cargo Clauses are the 2009 version as launched in January 2009 and for the purpose of this text, we shall focus on the 2009 clauses as these are the latest version.

It should be noted that, in addition to the above-mentioned three clauses (which will be discussed in greater detail in the following sub-sections), there are various other special clauses for different trades, some of which are used for the trade of rubber, timber, coal, fats and oils, etc.

Cover provided under the ICC A is sometimes called the “All Risks” cover, since it covers the goods against all risks of loss and/or damage, subject to specific exclusions as mentioned below. The loss and/or damage to goods must be due to accidental and/or fortuitous circumstances.

subsections.label: D3
subsections.title: Institute Cargo Clauses B & C (ICC B & C) 1.1.2009

paragraphs.text: These are restricted covers. Some call them “Named Perils” covers, because the perils that are covered are specifically stated in the clauses. In order to make a claim under this cover, the onus of proving a loss rests with the person making the claim. He will have to show that his cargo was damaged or lost by one of the perils specifically stated in the cover.

The general exclusion clause for ICC B and ICC C is very similar to that of the ICC A, with the exception that the ICC A covers malicious damage which is specifically excluded under the ICC B and ICC C. To have a clearer picture, Table

is a summary depicting the types of risks covered by the three Institute Cargo Clauses. Table 5.2: Risks Covered Under The Institute Cargo Clauses (A), (B) & (C) 1.1.2009 Clauses Risks Covered ‘A’ Clauses All Risks of loss or damage to the subject matter other than those excluded. ‘B’ Clauses Loss of or damage to the subject matter insured reasonably attributable to named risks as follows: fire, explosion, stranding, grounding, sinking, capsizing of vessel, over-turning, derailment of land conveyance, collision, or contact of vessel with any external object other than water; discharge of cargo at port of distress; general average sacrifice, jettison; washing overboard, entry of sea, lake or river water into vessel, craft, hold, conveyance, liftvan or place of storage; total loss of package lost overboard or dropped during loading or unloading; earthquake, volcanic eruption or lightning. ‘C’ Clauses Loss of or damage to the subject matter insured reasonably attributable to named risks as follows: fire, explosion, stranding, grounding, sinking, capsizing of vessel, over-turning, derailment of land conveyance, collision, or contact of vessel with any external object other than water; discharge of cargo at port of distress; general average sacrifice, jettison.

subsections.label: E
subsections.title: Types Of Marine Cargo Insurance Policies

paragraphs.text:
Generally, there are two basic types of Marine Cargo Insurance policies, namely:
▪ Voyage Policy; and
▪ Time Policy.
This is the most common type of policy for insuring cargoes. The policy provides cover for a voyage, and this is clearly mentioned in the policy where it is stated “from … to …”.

As provided under the Transit Clause of the ICC 2009 clauses, the cover commences from the time that the goods are first moved in the warehouse or place of storage named in the policy; and terminates when the goods arrive at the final destination named in the policy. The policy also makes provision for the buyer to opt for another warehouse or place of storage at the final destination which he will use for the purpose of allocation or distribution. Notwithstanding what has earlier mentioned, the buyer has a total time period of up to 60 days after the goods are discharged from the vessel to get them to the final warehouse. The cover may terminate earlier than 60 days, if the goods arrive earlier either at the destination named in the policy, or at the alternative warehouse, or place of storage chosen by the buyer, for the purpose of allocation or distribution. As a result of the scope of cover provided by the clause, a Voyage Policy is sometimes referred to as the “warehouse to warehouse” cover.

There are also various circumstances under which the cover can be terminated, such as:
▪ if the goods are sold during the course of the voyage;
▪ if the voyage is terminated owing to a peril insured; or
▪ if the carrier exercises his rights to terminate the cover as provided under the contract of affreightment.
All these contingencies are taken care of and are clearly spelt out in the Institute Cargo Clauses under the Voyage Policy.

This policy is frequently found in the inland transit cover. It is effected when a supplier wants to protect his cargoes, while they are being delivered to his various customers within a specified geographical area. These transits are usually by road and/or rail conveyance. Therefore, it is difficult and sometimes impractical for the supplier to declare each and every sending, before it takes place. It is also very time-consuming and expensive to effect an individual Voyage Policy for each and every sending.

As an alternative, a Time Policy is then issued to cover risks for a period of time, usually 12 months. Essentially, the cover for any voyage commences from the time that it leaves the insured’s/supplier’s premises and terminates on arrival at his customers’ premises. All voyages during this period will be automatically covered.

There is also a limit per conveyance for each voyage, and this represents the insurer’s maximum liability for any one accident or occurrence. Premiums can be charged on a monthly basis, following declaration by the insured of the total value of cargo carried during that month. Alternatively, a minimum premium, which is calculated at about 85% of the estimated annual premium, will be charged at the inception of the cover. This premium will then be adjusted at the end of the policy year, based on the actual total amount of cargo carried for the whole year.

Time policies are also now used for sea and/or air shipments, where the insured may not require individual policies or certificates of insurance for each sending. Calculation of premium is done as mentioned in the earlier paragraph.

Terms and conditions together with the rates applicable for each voyage or type of cargo will be agreed upon in advance. Once this is done, it is incorporated into a contract called an Open Cover. At the time that the Open Cover is effected, the usual practice is that no premium is charged. Only when the actual cargo is shipped out, then the premium for that shipment will become payable. However, some Open covers are done on an adjustable premium basis i.e. a provisional or minimum deposit premium is charged at inception based on the insured’s estimated annual shipment turnover. The premium is subject to adjustment based on the insured’s declaration of the actual shipment turnover.

It is obligatory for the insured to declare each and every shipment which falls under the scope of the Open Cover. Declarations of shipments can be made either for each individual shipment as it takes place, or on a monthly basis. Covers will be issued based on the declarations made and premiums charged.

It is also obligatory for the insurer to accept all declarations made by the insured under the Open Cover and within the terms as specified. For clients that the insurer know very well, they may even be allowed to issue the insurer’s Certificate of Insurance at their own office, thus saving time and enabling them to have their insurance documents quickly. Today, this is done by the insurer allowing the client access to its Internet portal and issuing the policy document online.

One of the advantages of having an Open Cover is the automatic protection granted to the insured for shipments that fall under the terms and conditions of the agreement. It also relieves the insured from having to arrange cover for each and every shipment. This can be important when shipments are very frequent.

The need for a Marine cover note arises when the insured wishes to effect Marine Cargo Insurance, but he does not have all the details sufficient to issue a policy. For example, when the insured wishes to import goods and to get a Letter of Credit (LC) from a bank, proof of insurance is required. At this point in time, the seller may not have fixed a vessel that will bring in the cargo, nor has the sailing date been fixed. The bank then requires a cover note to be presented. Once details of the shipment are known to the insured, they are given to the insurer, and the policy document is then issued.
The cover note is usually valid for three months. It expires upon full declaration being made or upon expiry of its validity, whichever is earlier.

In accordance with the provision of Section 18 of the Marine Insurance Act 1906, failure to disclose a material fact entitles the insurer to avoid the contract of insurance. Therefore, it is important that clients be advised to disclose all material facts on the proposal form.
In Marine Cargo Insurance, the general information that needs to be gathered by the underwriter from the intending insured for assessing the insurance application is described below.

(a) The intending insured’s company name and address. (b) The intending insured’s exact nature of business, such as whether the intending insured is in the manufacturing or trading business. Generally, the trading business involves only buying and selling of goods, without physically handling them. This has a bearing on the risk assessment, as the insurer needs to know how the goods are being packed and handled, before they are put on board a vessel. (c) The intending insured’s experience in its line of business, its financial standing and reputation in the field. (d) The intending insured’s previous insurer and previous claims history.

(a) Whether the cargo gives off or absorbs moisture. (b) Susceptibility of the cargo to damage owing to rough handling. Whether the cargo comprises fragile items, such as china or glass, or sensitive machines, such as computers and medical equipment or instruments used for calibration. (c) Whether the cargo is attractive to thieves, such as high-end integrated circuits, fashion goods, watches, cameras, precious stones, perfumes and cosmetics. (d) Whether the cargo is easily contaminated, such as refined edible oils or some chemicals that are susceptible. (e) Whether the cargo is prone to weight shortage.
Note that for bulk oil cargo, bagged cargo and fragile goods, a deductible or excess will usually apply.

(a) The types of packing or packing material to be used, such as shipment in drums, cartons, wooden crates/cases and bags. Goods are sometimes placed on pallets, as this facilitates easier handling by forklift trucks. (b) Whether goods are crated or packed in cartons. Goods that are crated are a better risk than those which are packed in cartons. However, certain cargoes, by their nature, cannot be crated. Most consumer and household goods, such as television sets have to be packed in cartons, as it will not be economical to crate them individually. (c) Whether the cargoes are packed into containers. (d) If cargoes are packed into containers, whether they are of a Full Container Load (FCL) or a Loose Container Load (LCL). In the case of FCL, the cargo is stuffed into the container at the supplier’s premises and is opened only on arrival at the final consignee’s warehouse. In the case of LCL, cargoes are stuffed at the container freight station and are similarly unstuffed at the destination of the corresponding freight station. There is the added risk of the goods being brought in their original packing from the supplier’s premises to the container freight station, and on arrival at the destination, they will have to be unstuffed from the container and taken by road and/or rail to the consignee’s and/or final warehouse. Usually, a FCL is a better risk than a LCL. (e) For cargoes that are packed into containers, it is important to ensure that they are properly stored within the container, and that depending on the nature of the cargo, there is adequate ventilation within the container. (f) Whether the cargoes are heavy equipment and machinery which cannot be packed and will be shipped “bare”2. (g) Whether the cargoes are dry bulk cargoes, such as sugar, wheat and fertilisers that are shipped in bulk3.

(a) A vessel’s structural form, e.g. wooden, steel, or glass-reinforced plastic (GRP); and age. (b) The type of vessel used, e.g. container, roll-on roll-off vessel, tug-barge, bulk carrier and tanker. (c) Whether the vessel is classed by any of the approved ship Classification Societies that are members of the International Association of Classification Societies. A vessel bearing such a class denotes that it has been built and maintained up to certain standards acceptable and recognised by the shipping industry. (d) A ship’s flag, i.e. its country of registration. The flag governs among other things, standards of maintenance and manning (numbers and composition of crew members, certification of officers, etc.) in accordance with the shipping registration of the country in question. (e) The size or tonnage of the vessel. This is important information to the insurer, especially when heavy density cargoes like steel and steel scrap, or cargoes that tend to “move” during the voyage e.g. logs that are being carried. (f) Whether the carrying vessel is entered with a recognised Protection and Indemnity4 (P&I) Club. The details of the P&I Club are important, as it presents a possibility for the insurer to recover a loss. (g) If goods are carried by other types of vessels, such as landing craft, pontoons (barge), or non-merchant vessels (e.g. military vessels), the details of these vessels must also be furnished.

(a) The length of voyage. The longer the voyage, the longer the cargo on board is exposed to loss of or damage. (b) Weather conditions of the trading areas. 2 The shapes and dimensions of such equipment and machinery do not allow them to be packed in a normal way. 3 These cargoes are not packed, but put into the bulk carrier either by conveyor belts, bucket grabs, or feed system. 4 A Protection and Indemnity (P&I) cover insures the liabilities of a vessel owner or operator against exposure, such as loss of life, personal injury and illness of crew members, passengers, and third-party individuals; damage to cargo on board an insured ship; damage to piers, docks, buoys and fixed or floating objects; damage to other vessels and their cargo; pollution; fines; wreck liabilities; etc. (c) Ports of call and ports of discharge. Certain ports have a reputation for good handling facilities and faster customs clearance. Some are notorious for theft and pilferage. (d) Any transshipments involved? Transshipments will mean additional handling of the cargoes during the voyage, and this increases the risk of loss and/or damage to the cargoes.

(a) Consideration must be given if the final destination is some distance inland. The road infrastructure and means of getting the goods to the final destination will have an impact on the condition in which the goods will be delivered. Some inland destinations have the notoriety of having high incidences of theft and/or hijacking, and these must be considered in underwriting the risk. (b) A majority of shipments are carried by vessels that ply regular or scheduled routes. These routes are advertised and are run regularly. They tend to be considered safer because of the regularity of the shipping routes, waters and weather conditions. The port and harbour facilities are known to the master of the vessel, and this aids the smooth loading and discharging of cargoes. In contrast, there are shipments that are carried by chartered vessels. Care must be taken not only in the type of vessel that is used in the charter, but also the experience and reputation of the charterer and the master, officers and crew members who man the vessel. The unfamiliarity of varied routes and voyages, which chartered vessels may be called to make, increase the risk of damage to the ship and, subsequently, to the cargoes. (c) Some vessels may not call directly at the required port of destination. The goods may have to be discharged at an intermediate port of call and transshipped onto another vessel that will bring her to the final destination. The additional handling of cargoes at the transshipment port will increase the risk of loss and/or damage to the cargoes.
Note that the underwriter may require other additional information, and the details of the required information may differ from insurer to insurer.

A Marine Cargo Insurance policy is generally a valued policy as stated in Section 27(3) of the Marine Insurance Act 1906. This means that, in the absence of fraud, the value that has been agreed upon and stated in the policy shall be taken as the value of the cargo for the purpose of settling a claim. This is very different from say, Motor Insurance, where in the event of a total loss, the claim is settled based on the market value of the vehicle at the time of the loss.

If a client has discovered damage or loss to his cargo, he should do the three things as described below. (a) Minimise The Loss & Prevent Further Losses Make every effort to reduce the loss or take steps to prevent further loss. This can include rebagging, and/or separating sound cargo from damaged cargo or in some cases, putting a film of oil over damaged machinery to prevent rusting or further rusting of the bare metal surface. Reasonable expenses incurred in taking such steps will be reimbursed in addition to any payment of the claim itself. The insured is expected to do exactly what he would do as if the shipment were uninsured. (b) Notify The Insurer Or Nominated Survey Agent Notify the insurer or its nominated survey agent, so that a survey of the damaged cargo can be arranged promptly. The carrier or its agent should also be informed and invited to attend the survey. As far as possible, the damaged cargoes, packaging and container(s) should be retained in the condition received until after the survey has been conducted, unless retaining them in this condition could result in aggravated damage. (c) File A Claim Against The Carrier File a claim in writing against the carrier (inland, ocean or air) as soon as the loss is known. This can be in any form, but must include the Bill of Lading5/Airway Bill number, name of conveyance, description of the loss or damage, and a document which states that the carrier will be held responsible for the loss or damage. As far as the insured is concerned, the future premium rates charged for his Marine Cargo Insurance would depend on his overall claims experience. Therefore, it is in his best interest that claims be kept to a minimum, or when claims arise, that he immediately informs the insurer, so that the insurer is put in a good position to take action against the carrier to recover part or all of what the insurer has paid him.

Submitting the above-mentioned documents promptly to the insurer will certainly speed up the processing of the claim. In special and complicated cases, other documents may be requested by the insurer as well.

In an earlier part of this chapter, we discussed the various types of vessels as well as their parts. This is useful to a marine hull insurance underwriter who should recognise the various types of vessels he is asked to insure. He needs to have a good knowledge of the hazards faced by ships, the functions of different ships and how such ships are built to withstand these hazards.

Likewise, a marine hull claims handler or loss adjuster needs to know the types of vessels. Knowing the types of vessels, how they are built , the hazards faced etc. will enable claims practitioners to be more effective in adjusting such losses i.e. reviewing the reasonableness of machinery repairs, hull repairs, grounding damage etc.
Now, we shall discuss Marine Hull Insurance.

Marine Hull Insurance covers the shipowner against the physical loss or damage to a vessel or ship, i.e. the actual hull or the steel plates, as well as the engines and auxiliary machinery, found on board a vessel.

In addition, it also covers some of the liabilities to third parties that he, as a shipowner, can incur resulting from the operation of the vessels, such as liabilities resulting from collision with third-party vessels.
Pleasure Craft Insurance, as well as Shipbuilders Risk Insurance, can also be included under the umbrella of Marine Hull Insurance.

subsections.label: B
subsections.title: The Need For Marine Hull Insurance

paragraphs.text: There is a need for shipowners to purchase Marine Hull Insurance as such cover affords protection against physical loss or damage to the ship. Having such insurance in place means that the shipowners will have peace of mind, and that their capital can be used for other purposes, such as for expanding their operations and for overseas trade.
On the other hand, banks and/or other financial institutions that provide finance for the purchase of ships may also need to protect the loan against the security of the vessel.
The parties to a Marine Hull Insurance contract will be the insurer and the ship owner. If the ship is chartered, this can then include the demised charterer.
Basically, all types of vessels from tug and barges to ocean-going vessels can be insured under a Marine Hull Insurance policy.

Unlike a Marine Cargo Insurance policy, there is usually no “All Risks” cover for vessels. Instead, these covers are in the form of “Named Perils”. This means that a Marine Hull Insurance policy will respond only if the loss of and/or damage to the vessel is caused by the perils as specified in the clauses that attach to the policy.

A variety of Hull Clauses has been designed to insure vessels. Some of the common clauses are:
▪ Institute Time Clauses – Hulls 1/10/83 CL.280;
▪ Institute Time Clauses – Hulls 1/11/95 CL.280-95; and
▪ International Hull Clauses 1/11/03 CL.601.
It is a common practice for Marine Hull Insurance policies to contain deductibles.
A Marine Hull Insurance policy can either be a Time Policy or a Voyage Policy. However, most Marine Hull Insurance policies are effected on a time basis and usually for a period of 12 months.

The general underwriting information to be obtained is described below. (a) The Details and Physical Condition Of The Vessel (i) The physical condition can only be discovered through competent surveys. For small vessels, underwriters may call for a survey to be done before making any underwriting decisions. (ii) The type and the nature of use of the vessel. (iii) Gross register tonnage (GRT), Net register tonnage (NRT), Flag and Classification of the vessel. (b) The Voyage (i) The territorial limits within which a vessel may trade or operate. Some ports of call and some sea-lanes are more hazardous than others. (ii) Proximity of any salvage and/or ship-repairing facilities and the area of operation of the vessel. (c) Ownership & Management (i) Good ownership is reflected in good seaworthy crafts that are properly maintained and well managed. Good ownership and management of the vessel have an impact on the quality of the crew members as well. (d) Previous Claims Experience (i) Past claims history: This can give an indication of the quality of management of the ship owner and, if unchanged, may indicate a continuing claims history. (ii) Comprehensive prior information on claims: This should also be obtained, so that a proper assessment can be made. (e) Valuation Of The Vessel (i) It is important that the sum insured in the policy be reasonably close to the value of the vessel. This is because any claim for partial loss on the hull is payable in full, up to the sum insured, without reference to the actual market value of the vessel. In addition, a low-insured value will also mean that a constructive total loss will be easily reached.
Note that the underwriter may require additional information, and the details of the required information may differ from insurer to insurer.

On the occurrence of any loss, the main objective is to establish whether the event causing the loss will be a peril covered by the policy, which will then determine whether the loss is recoverable from the insurer.
There are basically two types of losses that occur under a Marine Hull Insurance policy, namely partial loss and total loss, as described below.

For total loss, this is sub-divided into “Actual Total Loss” and “Constructive Total Loss” as described below. (a) Actual Total Loss There is an actual total loss when the subject matter insured, e.g. the hull itself, is destroyed. Examples are the sinking of a vessel without hope of salvage, and the complete destruction of a vessel by fire. Another example of an actual total loss of a vessel is one when a ship is deemed “missing”. In practice, if a ship is posted as missing, a reasonable period of time is allowed to elapse, and if no news is received, an actual total loss may be presumed. (b) Constructive Total Loss A constructive total loss occurs when the vessel is damaged to such an extent by an insured peril that, although it is not an actual total loss, the expenditure required to prevent it from becoming an actual total loss, as well as the estimated cost of repair would be greater than the insured value of the vessel. In such a situation, the insured is entitled to give a Notice of Abandonment to the hull insurers.

Partial loss, or particular average, means any loss that is not a total loss. An example of a partial loss is a ship being damaged by an insured peril and needs to be repaired. This could include damage following a collision or by fire or engine failure. As such, under the Hull Clauses, the replacement of an old part by a new part is payable in full by the insurer, without deducting “new for old”.

Similar to a Marine Cargo Insurance policy, whenever a claim arises, the insured has the responsibility to take reasonable measures to minimise or avert a loss or prevent further losses and/or damage.

Where expenses are incurred by the insured with a view to averting or minimising a loss recoverable under the policy, such expenses are known as Sue and Labour Charges. The insured derives his right to recover these charges from the insurer by reason of the Duty Of Assured (Sue and Labour) Clause in a Marine Hull Insurance policy. 6. BUILDERS’ RISKS INSURANCE
A Boat or Ship builder may suffer loss of or damage to the hull and machinery of vessels, which it is constructing (including materials in its possession), from a wide variety of causes.
Builders’ Risks Insurance is available for vessels in the course of construction, as well as for single risks or as an open cover.

Subject to the exclusions detailed in the insurer’s policy wording, Builders’ Risks Insurance is typically against all risks of loss of or damage to the subject matter insured caused and discovered during the period of insurance, including latent defect. In case of failure of launch, the policy covers all subsequent expenses incurred while completing the launch.

Builders’ Risks Insurance policy also covers loss of or damage to the subject matter insured caused and discovered during the period of insurance arising from faulty design of any part or parts thereof, but not including the cost or expenses of repairing, modifying, replacing or renewing such part or parts, nor any cost or expense of betterment or alteration in design.

In addition to those, there are certain Marine Liabilities covers that can be obtained from Protection and Indemnity Clubs, commonly known as P&I Clubs. These include a wide range of ShipOwner’s Liability covers, such as Collision Liability Insurance, Crew and Cargo Liability Insurance, and Pollution Liability Insurance. A P&I Club is a mutual (i.e. co-operative) insurance association that provides cover for its members who are typically ship owners. There are no shareholders, and the ship owner members of the association insure each other.

In this section, we will give an overview of the more common types of Marine Liability covers in a local context, namely Ship Repairer’s Liability Insurance, Charterers’ Liability Insurance, Port Authorities Liability Insurance and Stevedores’ Liability Insurance.

The term “Ship Repairing” covers an area of activity extending from “wash and brush ups” to major conversions. Regardless of the size of their operations, ship repairers have similar basic responsibilities and incur similar legal liabilities arising from the nature of their operations. The responsibilities and obligations of a ship repairer can be covered under a Ship Repairer’s Liability Insurance policy.
Note that the underwriter may require additional information, and the details of the required information may differ from insurer to insurer.
Chartering is the usual method of arranging sea transport for large volumes of bulk commodities, where an entire vessel is chartered to carry a particular cargo shipment.

A Charterers’ Liability Insurance policy is designed to protect charterers of vessels against various legal and contractual liabilities which can arise from their obligations under a Charter Party (i.e. a contract between the ship owner and the charterer for the use of a vessel).

A Charterers’ Liability Insurance policy is not an all-embracing guarantee cover, in the sense, that it does not cover financial/commercial trading risks and also does not cover contractual liabilities, other than in relation to the vessel itself. Nevertheless, it provides valuable protection to the Charterer in respect of unforeseen incidents which may give rise to a liability.

Charterer’s Liability Insurance provides protection in respect of three main areas of liability exposure, namely:
▪ damage to the vessel which can arise in various ways, e.g. from an unsafe or foul berth, from stevedoring damage or from using incorrect bunker fuel;
▪ liability to third parties other than cargo owners, such as liability for removal of wreck or liability for injury to crew members; and
▪ damage to cargo which can arise where third-party cargo is being carried.

Damage to vessel and liability to third parties other than cargo owners are usually the basic covers as provided, whereas cover for damage to cargo is usually issued as an extension with the payment of extra premium.
Note that the underwriter may require additional information, and the details of the required information may differ from insurer to insurer.

Port authorities around the world are constituted in a variety of ways. Therefore, the facilities provided, and the responsibilities of any individual port authority will vary widely, depending on the type of port in question.
The insurance requirements of a port authority controlling a small fishing harbour will obviously differ from a port authority controlling a large commercial harbour.
A Port Authorities Liability Insurance policy, which covers the responsibilities and risk exposure of a port authority, is mainly arranged by general insurance brokers.
Note that the underwriter may require additional information, and the details of the required information may differ from insurer to insurer.
A stevedores’ primary activity is the loading and unloading of vessels.

Therefore, Stevedores’ Liability Insurance covers against legal liabilities incurred for damage caused to vessels, to cargo being handled, to property (buildings, wharves, etc.) or for third-party death or injury.
The scope of cover includes indemnification against the legal liabilities and legal defence costs which stevedores may incur as arising out of their cargo handling activities.

In the Oil and Gas industry, there are three main activity categories:
▪ Upstream activities (prospecting, exploration, development and extracting/production)
▪ Midstream activities (transport from offshore field to marine terminal/refinery/petrochemical plant/gas processing & transmission)
▪ Downstream activities (refining, petrochemical, processing, marketing & distribution)

Energy Insurance serves to protect upstream, midstream and downstream organizations cum operations against certain risks. These risks include equipment damage, rig physical damage, business interruption, loss of production income, control of well costs etc.
The type of underwriting information required by insurers depends on the nature of the organisation cum operation involved as well as the types of risk coverage being sought.
Taking Offshore Physical Damage as an example, insurers will require the following information such as ownership / loyalty, record, type of unit, value and age etc.

If Contingent Business Interruption cover is required, insurers will wish to know the level of inter-dependence between insured items / units, probability of a total loss to the policy caused by a partial damage to one item only etc.

So, what are the main differences between marine and energy insurance? Energy Insurance involves covering risks of drilling into unknown underground conditions and high-risk operations. The insured objects are stationary while operating while Marine Insurance generally covers the subject matter while it is in transit. There are also tailormade items and parts with long lead time not to mention high values and significant retention/deductible levels involved in Energy Insurance.

Aviation Insurance is insurance coverage tailored to the operation of aircraft and the risks involved in aviation. In the aviation market, there is a wide range of insurance coverage available to meet different needs of individuals to international airlines. There is also cover available for ancillary activities like manufacturers and airports.
The main aim of hull and liability insurance is to cover physical damage or loss to the insured aircraft in addition to liabilities arising in respect of third parties, passengers and their baggage.
This type of policy covers liability for any loss, damage or injury to third parties caused by the aircraft or any part thereof, usually resulting from a faulty design or defective materials.

This cover is required by airport operators. The cover under this policy is divided into three sections:
▪ Section 1 Airport premises liability;
▪ Section 2 Hangar keeper’s liability; and
▪ Section 3 Product liability.
A war policy covers war and allied risks excluded under the Hull and Liability Policy.
