paragraphs.text: In this chapter, we will look at the four types of Construction and Engineering Insurance policies, namely Contractors’ All Risks (CAR), Erection All Risks (EAR), Boiler & Pressure Vessel and Machinery Breakdown including its associated Consequential Loss cover. We will also discuss the insurance of Lifts, Cranes and Handling Equipment as well as Deterioration of Stocks Insurance. In the final part of this chapter, we shall examine Insurance Bonds which is an example of a Pecuniary Insurance. 2. CONTRACTORS’ ALL RISKS (CAR) INSURANCE

subsections.label: A
subsections.title: What Is Contractors’ All Risks (CAR) Insurance?

paragraphs.text: A Contractors’ All Risks (CAR) Insurance policy provides financial security for all parties involved in a construction project during the course of construction. It is a comprehensive policy that covers any sudden and unforeseen physical loss or damage relating to the construction project, subject to a number of exclusions.

subsections.label: B
subsections.title: Parties To A Contractors’ All Risks (CAR) Insurance Contract

paragraphs.text: A CAR Insurance contract involves three parties, namely the contractor, the principal and the insurer.

The principal is the party who commissions the work, usually by way of a tender. He is usually the owner of the land and/or buildings when completed. Therefore, he is sometimes referred to as the property owner. 1 Direct losses from bad workmanship, lack of skill, negligence, malicious act, or human error are usually not covered under a CAR Insurance policy. However, if bad workmanship, lack of skill, negligence, malicious act, or human error, has resulted in, say, a defective wall, falling and damaging another wall which has been correctly constructed, a CAR Insurance policy will pay for the damage to the latter wall.

The contractor is under contract to the principal for the purpose of carrying out the contract works. The contractor will usually employ specialist subcontractors who may or may not have a direct contractual relationship with the principal. Examples of contractors or sub-contractors are builders, roofing subcontractors, lift contractors, etc.

There is a contract between the principal and the contractor for the contract works undertaken. The standard contract conditions will usually require the contractor to arrange for a CAR Insurance policy with an insurer to cover the contract works during the period of construction and maintenance period, so that the interests of the principal, the contractor himself and all sub-contractors are protected. The Principal will also require himself to be added as an insured in the CAR policy to take care of his interests which arises from his potential vicarious liability for the actions of the contractor.

subsections.label: C
subsections.title: The Need For Contractors’ All Risks (CAR) Insurance

paragraphs.text: Although a CAR Insurance policy is not compulsory by law, it will be prudent to insure all contract works, major or minor, whether it is a civil engineering project, simple retro-fitting work, or a complex property development project.

Under the contract, both the principal and contractor shall have responsibilities and obligations towards each other. Generally, the contractor shall be responsible for:
▪ the care of the works; and
▪ duty of care to ensure the safety of people or property at the contract site.

In connection with these, it would be prudent on part of the contractor to seek CAR Insurance to insure against potential risk exposures. As far as possible, the CAR Insurance policy should be arranged such that it complements the contract conditions between the principal and the contractor.

The nature and types of property construction projects can vary greatly. The subject matter of a CAR Insurance policy can be for projects as follows:
▪ construction of residential properties, such as houses and apartments;
▪ construction of industrial and commercial properties, such as factories, warehouses, offices and shopping complexes; and
▪ construction of infrastructure works, such as roads, bridges, tunnels, MRT tracks, reservoirs, etc.
There are generally two types of policies in use today, namely a specific project policy and an annual policy as described below:

A CAR Insurance policy is usually taken up on a specific project basis. The contract of a specific project usually requires the contractor to arrange for a CAR Insurance policy to cover the contract works during the construction period and the maintenance period.

Contractors can arrange for the CAR Insurance policy to cover their own part of the project or the whole of the project, including all works performed by subcontractors, if they are the main contractor or project manager.
Although less common in practice, the contractor may seek to insure his various project works under an annual policy, rather than have each insured under a specific project basis.

Essentially, an annual policy is designed to cover all works undertaken during a 12-month period. Some insureds may find it potentially more convenient and cost effective to insure on an annual policy basis. This is especially if the characteristic, nature and scope of the various project works are generally the same, each of generally short project duration, singularly low in contract value and performed at same contract sites, or by the same contractors/subcontractors.

CAR Insurance policies are usually written with two sections as described below:
▪ Section I: Deals with the material damage aspect. This section provides “All Risks” cover on buildings or infrastructure works in the course of construction; and
▪ Section II: Covers third-party liability arising from the construction project (including property damage, death and bodily injury to persons, i.e. third parties).

The property insured is set out in the schedule of the policy, and any form of sudden and unforeseen physical loss or damage to the property is covered, subject to a number of exclusions (see later section).

The indemnity may be the repair, reinstatement, or replacement of any property lost or damaged, or a monetary payment equivalent to the cost of repair, reinstatement or replacement. In practice, a monetary payment is the most common mode.

subsections.label: F2
subsections.title: Exclusions to Section I – Material Damage

paragraphs.text:
This section will indemnify the insured against any damages which he is legally liable to pay in consequence of:
▪ accidental bodily injury to or illness of third parties; and
▪ accidental loss of or damage to property belonging to third parties; occurring in direct connection with the construction or erection of the items insured under Section I of the CAR Insurance policy.
This section also covers all costs and expenses of litigation recovered by any claimant from the insured or incurred by the insured himself, with the consent of the insurer.

subsections.label: F4
subsections.title: Exclusions To Section II – Third-Party Liability

paragraphs.text: Some of the above-mentioned exclusions are negotiable. This will be subject to payment of additional premiums, coupled with the insured bearing a higher deductible amounts.

The period of cover is specified in the schedule of the policy, beginning with the date of commencement of the contract works and expiring on the estimated date of completion of the construction period. The period may be shorter or longer than 12 months for a CAR Insurance policy taken up based on a specific project, and 12 months for an annual policy.

In addition to the construction period, there is often a maintenance period as stipulated in the contract. Often, the maintenance period is for three, six or 12 months commencing from the expiry of the construction period. The contract conditions usually stipulate that the contractor must pay periodic visits to inspect or make adjustments, or to remedy defects which may arise during the maintenance period. As such, insurance coverage during the maintenance period is available with payment of extra premiums.

The liability of the insurer commences according to the effective date of the insurance policy and ends when the contract work has been handed over to the principal, or when the insurance cover expires, whichever is earlier.

For CAR Insurance policies, it is not the industry practice to use standardised proposal forms as the primary means of obtaining information to assess a risk. Some insurers may have proposal forms for their agents’ usage while some brokers may have their own questionnaires, submission slips etc.
The customarily required underwriting information is described below:

(a) Who is to be insured? All parties involved in the project, i.e. the principal, contractors, sub-contractors, or only one or some of these parties. (b) How long has the insured’s business been established? Brief description of contracts undertaken in the last three years. (c) The previous claims experience of all the parties to the contract, including any liability to third parties.

(a) Estimated value of the whole project, including details of any work or materials for which the contractor will be responsible. (b) Sum insured for debris removal. (c) Contract conditions (between the principal and the contractor) under which the work is undertaken and responsibilities of the parties involved are clarified. (d) Estimated period of construction and maintenance period. (e) The amount of indemnity required for any one accident in respect of thirdparty liability.

(a) The exact location of the construction site, whether it is located near the sea or a congested area, or whether it is a new MRT line. (b) Full description of the work to be done together with supporting documentary plans, sketches, and so forth. (c) Detailed description of the method of carrying out the work. (d) Any demolition work to be performed. (e) Any piling work to be involved. If piling is involved, information on the piling work, e.g. type, number of piles and penetration depth, are essential information to the insurer. (f) Any blasting to be performed. If so, state the total amount of explosives to be used and the details of the safety precautions to be taken. (g) Any partial handover. The insurer needs to know details of responsibilities for handover work together with values, anticipated handover dates, and so forth. (h) Soil Profile/Summary Report. (i) Proximity of surrounding properties. (j) If a basement is to be constructed, the depth of excavation and the methods used are important considerations. The underwriter will have to consider whether there is a likelihood of damage to the contract work by floods. (k) A plan of the site showing the proposed location of temporary buildings and other storage areas for materials and equipment, together with values and exposure to hazards.
Note that the underwriter may require additional information, and the details of the required information may differ from insurer to insurer.

subsections.label: I
subsections.title: Other Types Of Cover Related To Contract Works

paragraphs.text: Currently, there is a trend in the construction industry to design and build, i.e. a contractor may undertake to carry out all stages of the project from the design onwards, and not just the actual building work. The contractor must recognise that some of the liabilities previously attributable to other firms will now be internal. The principal also has more reasons to claim against the contractor. Previously, where the principal may have claimed against the architect, the surveyor, or engineer, the contractor is now also responsible.

The professional risk relating to the design stage is of particular importance. Therefore, the contractor will need to take out Professional Indemnity (PI) Insurance3 to cover these risks. As this kind of PI Insurance is taken out to cover professionals involved in a project, it is commonly called “Project PI Insurance”. Its insurance is the same coverage as that of a standard PI policy, except that it has higher limits and a longer duration of coverage than a standard PI policy. 3 Further discussions on Professional Indemnity Insurance are found in Chapter 3 of this Study Guide.

As discussed in Chapter 3 of this Study Guide, prior to 1 September 2020, Work Injury Compensation insurance (WICI) could be issued under a “project policy” basis. A project WIC policy refers to the portions of project insurance policies that provide coverage for work injury sustained at a project site, or while doing work for a specific project. MOM has stipulated that from 1 September 2020, employers should not get new project WICI policies. There are Work Injury Compensation claims that are covered under both project WICI policy and employer’s WIC policy. Such duplicate coverage can lead to delays and disputes over who should compensate the injured employee or to reimburse the employer. Companies can potentially reduce insurance premiums by avoiding duplicate coverage. The reader can refer to Chapter 3 of this study guide for more details.

In the event of loss or damage to the contract works, the contractor and or principal may suffer a loss of advance profits4, or incur increased expenditure in order to keep the project going. The contractor may also incur liquidated damages, owing to the delay in completing the contract works.

It is not only the contractor who is vulnerable to such losses. The principal may also encounter problems arising from the delay in completing the contract works. The principal, who may also be acting as the developer, may be paying interest charges to the bank and will also encounter a delay in receiving rent on the completed building. If the principal is intending to sell the development, he will also suffer a loss of the development profit owing to the delay.
To protect themselves from exposure to business interruption losses, some contractors will take out an Increased Cost Of Working cover5, whereas others will take out a Full Revenue cover.

A performance bond is often required by the principal e.g. the owner of the land, for large capital projects. Such bonds are required by the principal i.e. it secures payment for the principal in the event the contract is not being fulfilled. In the final part of this chapter, we shall examine Insurance Bonds in greater detail. 4 Loss of advance profits is due to delay in completing the contract works which result in a delay in earning future profits. 5 Further discussions on Business Interruption Insurance are in Chapter 2 of this Study Guide.

subsections.label: A
subsections.title: What Is Erection All Risks (EAR) Insurance?

paragraphs.text: An Erection All Risks (EAR) Insurance policy is primarily intended to cover risks in the installation and erection of ready-built engineering projects, such as power plants, refrigeration plants, electrical generator stations, overhead gantry cranes, machinery, equipment, other fittings in factories and complexes, etc.

It provides financial security for all parties involved in an installation and erection project. An EAR Insurance policy is similar to a CAR Insurance policy, except that the subject matter to be insured under an EAR Insurance policy involves more fixing, assembling and installation work, but less of construction work.

subsections.label: B
subsections.title: Erection All Risks (EAR) Insurance Versus Contractors’ All Risk (CAR) Insurance

paragraphs.text: An EAR cover is very similar to a CAR cover, in terms of both the perils provided and the markets involved. However, there is one major difference, and this concerns the testing and commissioning risk. In view of the nature of the subject matter, and of the very diverse nature of different risks, the testing and commissioning risk may be either relatively small, or become a major risk for insurers.
Nevertheless, the wording in the main body of an EAR Insurance policy will be very similar to a CAR Insurance policy.

subsections.label: C
subsections.title: The Need For Erection All Risks (EAR) Insurance

paragraphs.text: All mechanical or electrical installation and erection projects are formalised by contract between the principal and the contractor. The contract conditions impose an obligation on the contractor, to provide the principal with an indemnity in respect of loss of or damage to the contract works. Therefore, there is a need for the contractor to take out insurance cover for these obligations.

Like a CAR Insurance policy, an EAR Insurance policy basically involves three parties, namely the contractor, the principal and the insurer. Besides protecting the interests of the principal and the contractor, the EAR Insurance policy may also be extended to protect the interests of any sub-contractors involved.

Similar to a CAR Insurance policy, the scope of cover for an EAR Insurance policy also consists of two sections, namely Section I that deals with the material damage aspect, and Section II that deals with third-party liability arising from the installation and erection project.
One major difference that distinguishes the two types of policies is the cover for testing and commissioning risk as provided under an EAR Insurance policy.

Cover for testing and commissioning is to provide for loss, destruction and/or damage to the property insured occurring during testing and commissioning work carried out by the contractor (or sub-contractor) within the terms of the installation or erection contract.

Depending on the nature of the plant, different kinds of work may be necessary, both during and after the installation or erection of the plant. If the plant’s building has few parts within, the testing and commissioning risk is usually minimal. However, the plant presents a totally different risk if there are more parts within and where most parts may be moving and interacting with each other. The risk of breakdown is ever present at the commissioning stage, as well as physical risks, such as fires and/or explosions.

The risks are greater during the testing and commissioning period than during the installation and erection period. This is owing to not only the additional perils exposure but also increased risks of fire and explosion. Moreover, the works has become a working unit and the full value of the contract may be at risk at this stage.

Cover for testing and commissioning usually ceases upon the satisfactory completion of tests as required under any contract, or when the property insured is put into commercial use or taken over by the principal, whichever occurs first.

The period of cover is expressed in the schedule of the policy commencing with the start date of installation/erection and expiring on the estimated date of completion of the installation and erection period. Like a CAR Insurance policy, the period may be shorter or longer than 12 months for a specific project policy, and 12 months for an annual policy.

In addition, a testing or commissioning period is further expressed in the schedule of the policy. This is usually specified, such as 30 days of testing/commissioning. This is a period after the installation and erection period during which items, such as installed machinery, are test run.

subsections.label: G
subsections.title: Extensions For Contractors’ All Risks (CAR)/Erection All Risks (EAR) Insurance

paragraphs.text: The extensions for both CAR and EAR Insurance policies are similar. Some of the more common extensions are described below:
The insurer allows the policy sum insured or limits to be automatically reinstated after a loss,as long as the insured pays the appropriate extra premium on the amount of loss.

The insurer is willing to cover all insured parties named in the schedule of the policy against third-party liability, as if a separate policy has already been issued to each party. However, the insurers will not pay for:
▪ loss of or damage to items insured under Section I – Material Damage of the policy; or
▪ injuries or illnesses of employees or workmen who should have been insured under a Work Injury Compensation Insurance policy.

With the payment of additional premium, the insurer is willing to extend cover for the maintenance period to cover solely the loss of or damage to the erection or installation works in the course of carrying out the maintenance job.

subsections.label: G4
subsections.title: Cover Of Extra Charges For Overtime, Night Work, Work On Public Holidays,

paragraphs.text: Express Freight

Both the CAR and EAR policies can be extended to cover extra charges for overtime, night work, work on public holidays and express freight (excluding air freight), which are incurred owing to the loss of or damage to the insured items.

The indemnity granted under Section II – Third-Party’s Liability of the CAR/EAR Insurance policy can be extended to include liability in respect of damage to property, land or building caused by vibration or by removal or weakening of support.

subsections.label: H
subsections.title: Contractors’ All Risks (CAR)/Erection All Risks (EAR) Insurance Claims

paragraphs.text: The claim handling and settlement procedures for both CAR and EAR Insurance policies are similar.
In the event of a claim, the contractor will inform his insurance agent/broker or notify the insurer directly.
The CAR/EAR claim form or the loss adjuster’s report will provide most of the information needed to ascertain whether or not the loss is covered and, if so, the likely claim costs to be incurred.

If the loss is straightforward, e.g. theft of loose materials on site, the claim procedures will be simple. Where the loss is complex, such as how much time that it will take to effect repair, the insurer will appoint a loss adjuster (or insurer’s own claim specialist) to prepare a report. The insured will need to provide the following information to the loss adjuster: the value of property prior to or after loss; the extent of loss or damage; the cause of loss or damage; the likely cost to repair; any uninsured element (apart from the deductible); the effect of extensive delays to the work; an estimate of any likely loss under business interruption for: - increases in future costs of construction owing to inflation, etc. (if insured); - loss of earning ability at the planned opening time owing to delay in completion (if full revenue cover has been arranged); and details of availability of labour and materials at the later rather than planned dates (this is necessary information as the original schedule has been disrupted).
▪ details of third-party injury or property damage including vibration or by removal or weakening of support (VRWS) claims (if any).

The need to establish the real cause of damage in CAR/EAR claims is very important, because the insurer may want to exercise subrogation rights against a supplier of designs, specifications or other materials for the recovery of claim outlay owing to a fault, error, or omission in the services or goods supplied.

The need to assess the effect of delays is also particularly important in CAR/EAR claims, as the later stages of the project works will be disrupted by the delays, and the uncompleted works will need to be re-scheduled.

sections.title: Insurance Of Lifts, Cranes & Handling Equipment

paragraphs.text: As mentioned before, the CAR policy may extend to cover contractors’ equipment materials and mobile equipment at site. However, it is also common to have such equipment covered under a separate insurance policy as described below.

There are various types of equipment included in this category ranging from common lifting equipment, such as passenger lifts, escalators, and forklifts, to the very large equipment, such as gantry cranes, trucks, mobile cranes, loaders, loading shovels, excavators, etc. The purpose of use and the way these types of equipment operate may differ one from the other, but they have one thing in common, which is that they are used for lifting and lowering loads either stationary at one location or from one place to another.

The insurance cover for these types of equipment (with the exception of Passenger Lifts and Elevators) is commonly known as Contractor’s Plant and Equipment (CPE) policy or Heavy Equipment Insurance policy. These policies normally cover all losses arising from any cause whatsoever other than those specifically excluded. One of the main policy exclusions is internal mechanical or electrical breakdown such as rupture, cracking or deformation due to operational failure, strain or other internal failure. Notwithstanding this however, the coverage for internal mechanical or electrical breakdown can be included into the policy by way of endorsement, with the insurer’s agreement.

subsections.label: A
subsections.title: What Is Boiler & Pressure Vessel Insurance?

paragraphs.text: A Boiler & Pressure Vessel Insurance policy is designed to cover the damage to property and liability of the insured arising from all types of boilers and pressure plants.

subsections.label: B
subsections.title: The Need For Boiler & Pressure Vessel Insurance

paragraphs.text: Boilers are used to produce steam to drive engines, turbines and other heavy machinery, and the heat absorbed by the steam can be used in cooling and heating. However, this same steam can potentially cause great destruction, resulting in substantial loss of lives and money.

Thus, regulations have been passed for the purpose of ensuring that vessels which operate under pressure and lifting appliances are regularly inspected to meet specified requirements, so as to reduce the risks of accidents.

Despite careful inspection, accidents may still happen owing partly to human error in operating the boilers and partly to unforeseen circumstances. Hence, there is a need for insurance protection against such loss exposure for boilers and pressure plants.
In most cases, the parties to a Boiler & Pressure Vessel Insurance contract will be the insurer, as well as the owners/operators of an industrial plant.
There are two aspects to the policy coverage of Boiler & Pressure Vessel Insurance, namely the “material damage” aspect and the “liability of the insured” aspect.
To understand the scope of cover better, it is important to take note of the definitions as described below.
The period of cover is usually 12 months.

Some of the common provisions and conditions specific to a Boiler & Pressure Vessel Insurance policy are described below: (a) Alteration To Plant The insured must give notice in writing of any departure from normal working conditions, repair, removal, alteration of, or addition to any item of the plant. If the insurer does not approve, insurance on that item may be cancelled and a proportion of the premium for the unexpired period of insurance returned to the insured. (b) Suspension Of Liability The insurer is not liable for any damaged item of the plant until such an item has been repaired to the satisfaction of the insurer. (c) Right Of Access The insurer shall at all reasonable times have the right of access to the premises of the plant. He is also permitted at all reasonable times during the term of the policy to inspect the plant and also the structures in which it is contained. (d) Limitation Of Loading Boiler and pressure vessels are certified for a stipulated working pressure. The insured will not be covered if, at the time of explosion or collapse, the safety valve load exceeds that of the boiler inspection regulations.

Note that a detailed discussion on the policy provisions and conditions common to most general insurance policies is in the “Basic Insurance Concepts & Principles” Study Guide, published by the Singapore College of Insurance Limited.
Note that the underwriter may require additional information, and the details of the required information may differ from insurer to insurer.

Other than the usual general exclusions that relate to war, riot, strike and nuclear risks, some common exclusions specific to this class of insurance are as follows: (a) defects owing to wearing away or wasting of the material of the plant by leakage, corrosion, action of fuel or otherwise; (b) slowly developing deformation or distortion of any part of the plant; (c) overheating, cracks, fractures, blisters, laminations, flaws of grooving or failure of joints even when accompanied by leakage. (d) the failure of tubes in boilers or superheaters or economizers (unless such defects result in explosion or collapse); (e) damage to property caused by fire rising from explosion or collapse, or damage to the plant resulting from any extraneous cause; (f) damage or liability caused by or arising out of the wilful act or wilful neglect of the insured; and (g) any test other than test at a pressure not exceeding the maximum pressure permitted by the inspecting authority.
However, note that explosion or collapse arising from (a), (b) and (c) of the above-mentioned defects is not excluded.
Note that the exclusions of a Boiler & Pressure Vessel Insurance policy may vary from insurer to insurer.
Note that the extensions of a Boiler & Pressure Vessel Insurance policy may vary from insurer to insurer.
The common claim conditions specific to a Boiler & Pressure Vessel Insurance policy are described below:

The insured must give immediate notice to the insurer of any occurrence of accident. This must be confirmed in writing within a stipulated time of the occurrence. The insured must also notify the insurer, once he knows of any impending prosecution inquest or fatal enquiry relating to such accident.

The insurer may, at its option, repair, replace or reinstate, or may pay in cash the amount of the damage. In the event that the insurer decides to repair the damage, he is only liable to repair the damage as may be possible and pay the insured the cost of parts that are not obtainable. In the event that the insurer decides to pay cash for the damage, it is liable to pay, only within the limits of the policy, the reasonable cost of repairing the damage and the cost of parts not being obtainable.

The insured is required to pay extra charges for express delivery and overtime. The insurer is not liable for the cost of any repair undertaken by the insured, without the insurer’s permission in writing.
Machinery Breakdown Insurance is designed to cover expensive plant, machinery and mechanical equipment against unforeseen and sudden damage caused by breakdown.

subsections.label: B
subsections.title: The Need For Machinery Breakdown Insurance

paragraphs.text: As machinery involves moving parts, such operation presents hazards to life and property. Although there are regulations set up to ensure a certain degree of safety for the use of machinery, as in the case of boilers and pressure vessels, accidents can still occur. In fact, any industrial or manufacturing concern which operates machinery will need the protection of Machinery Breakdown Insurance.
The parties to a Machinery Breakdown Insurance contract will be the insurer, as well as the owners/operators of an industrial or manufacturing enterprise in most cases.

Machinery Breakdown Insurance is important not only to large industrial enterprises which operate large units or fully automated production plants, but also to medium and small enterprises, where machinery failure may result in much more serious financial and economic consequences.

All types of machinery, plant, mechanical equipment and apparatus may be covered under Machinery Breakdown Insurance, such as:
▪ power generating units, e.g. boilers, turbines and generators;
▪ power distribution plants, e.g. transformers, high and low tension equipment; and
▪ production machinery and auxiliary equipment, e.g. machine tools, paper machines, pumps, compressors, tanks, pipes and tubes.
The individual machinery to be insured, as well as its type and model, are to be included in the schedule.

Machinery Breakdown Insurance provides coverage for the insured machinery, while it is:
▪ working or at rest; or
▪ being dismantled, moved or re-erected for the purpose of cleaning, repair or installation in another position within the insured premises.
Note that the underwriter may ask for additional information, and the details of the information requested may differ from insurer to insurer.
Note that the exclusions of a Machinery Breakdown Insurance policy may vary from insurer to insurer.

It has been mentioned above that deterioration of stock in the refrigerating machinery is one of the extensions available in Machinery Breakdown Insurance. Alternatively, Deterioration of Stock Insurance can also be arranged on an individual policy basis, provided that it is done in conjunction with a Machinery Breakdown cover.
Certain types of stocks such as food, pharmaceuticals, wine, etc. need to be stored in refrigerating machinery at a regulated cum controlled temperature.

The Deterioration of Stock Insurance is designed as a cover against deterioration of stock in cold storage owing to a breakdown of the refrigerating plant/machinery as a result of any material damage to the refrigerant plant, which is indemnifiable under the Machinery Breakdown Insurance. The mechanical damage or failure of electrical supply under certain conditions can cause the rise or fall in temperature, allow unforeseen escape of refrigerant gas, or result in incorrect storage temperature in a controlled environment.

The event resulting in deterioration of stock in cold storage must always be an indemnifiable breakdown of machinery i.e. loss of or damage to the refrigerating equipment, which is covered under a Machinery Breakdown insurance policy.

Therefore, a Deterioration of Stock Insurance policy can be taken out only in connection with a Machinery Breakdown cover. Insurers are generally reluctant to offer Deterioration of Stock Insurance on a standalone basis but only in conjunction with a Machinery Breakdown cover as presumably, the underwriters for the latter would have underwritten the risks of the refrigerant plant/machinery before issuing the Machinery Breakdown policy. As discussed above, the event resulting in deterioration of stock in cold storage must always be an indemnifiable breakdown of machinery i.e. loss of or damage to the refrigerating equipment.

A few common claim conditions specific to the Machinery Breakdown Insurance are described below: (a) Notice Of Claims In the event of a claim, the insured must take all reasonable steps to minimise loss or damage and preserve any damaged or defective parts for inspection by the surveyor or representative of the insurer. He must also furnish all relevant supporting information and documentary evidence as the insurer may require. Note that there are some insurers who state that they will not be liable for any loss or damage if notice has not been received by them within a specific period (usually 14 days) of its occurrence. (b) Reasonable Precautions The insured has to take all ordinary and reasonable precautions and comply with all reasonable recommendations of the insurer to prevent loss or damage, and to comply with statutory and manufacturer’s recommendations. (c) Inspection The insurer shall have the opportunity to inspect the loss or damage before any repairs or alterations are carried out, except for minor damage to items, where the insurer may let the insured proceed with the repairs without inspection. If repairs are carried out without the insurer’s consent, or if the machinery is being used without proper repairs to the satisfaction of the insurer, the insurer may cease their liability under the policy.
Machinery breakdown leads to an interruption of production and can potentially cause significant loss in terms of reduced turnover or increased costs of working during down time.

The cover provided under Machinery Breakdown Consequential Loss Insurance ensures business continuity, in conjunction with a Machinery Breakdown Insurance policy. It gives the insured a peace of mind it seeks in the event of any unforeseen breakdowns that can affect its business.

Machinery Breakdown Loss of Consequential Loss insurance provides coverage against loss of gross profit due to business interruption caused by an accident indemnifiable under Machinery Breakdown Insurance. The loss of gross profit is as a result of reduction in turnover due to decreased in production and increased in cost of working.

In Singapore, it is a common practice for owners of projects (both public and private) to require an insurance bond to be furnished by contractors. At times, the requirement for an insurance bond is a contractual requirement which the contractor must fulfil. Insurance bonds not only serve as substitutes for the security deposit6 which the contractors would otherwise have to pay in cash to the project owner, but also guarantee that the contractors would have to adhere to the terms and conditions of the contract which they had entered into with the project owner. These bond sums will usually range from 5% to 10% of the contract value.

Besides contractors, persons and/or entities applying for licences and permits also use insurance bonds as substitutes for the security (cash) deposit. These will be for licences and/or permits as required by the authorities (e.g. Controller of Immigration, Immigration & Checkpoints Authority). Bonds are also often required by landlords from their tenants in connection with leasing of premises.
In this chapter, you will learn about insurance bonds, including their underwriting requirements.

A bond is a writing of obligation (sometimes under seal), whereby a person/company undertakes to pay a sum of money or to perform a contract. An insurance bond is a three-party instrument, whereby one party (the insurance company or bank7) guarantees a second party (the principal) financial protection against a third party (the contractor or insurance company’s client) for failing to perform in accordance with the terms and conditions of a contract. In other words, the insurance bond guarantees the fulfilment of a legal obligation on the part of the contractor. Such a relationship is known as suretyship.

Figure 6.1 illustrates the relationship among the three parties to an insurance bond. 6 A security deposit is the amount of cash deposit that a project owner/government authority requires the contractor/applicant for a licence to place with any of them. This deposit would be forfeited if the contractor/applicant for the licence did not adhere to the terms and conditions of the contract that they had entered into with the project owner/government authority. 7 Other than an insurance company, a bond can also be issued by a bank. The insurance company/bank should be an independent body from the contractor/client, so that it could discharge the obligation irrespective of the financial situation of the contractor/client. Figure 6.1: Parties To An Insurance Bond
As you can see from Figure 6.1 above, a contract or obligation is formed between the principal and the contractor/client. This forms the basis for the guarantee that the insurer provides.

By issuing the bond, the insurance company guarantees the principal that the obligation will be performed by the contractor/client who undertakes the contract/obligation. In the event that the contractor/client fails to meet the obligation or complete the contract in time, the insurance company has to pay the principal for losses incurred up to the amount as specified in the bond, or undertake to complete the project. Upon paying up the bond, the insurance company can seek recovery by way of the counter guarantors of the contractor/client. Let us take a look at Example 6.1 which illustrates how an insurance bond works. Example 6.1: Illustration Of How An Insurance Bond Works Andy Ang, the owner of a fast-food chain, wants to build a new outlet. He engages Bailey Contractor to construct a building. Thus, a contract is formed between Andy and Bailey Contractor. However, Andy has suffered in the past because of defaulting contractors. As such, he wants assurance from Bailey Contractor that, if it defaults, he will not lose money, and that the project will be completed in a timely and efficient manner with the desired quality of work. Therefore, Bailey Contractor purchases a bond from Crown Insurance Company and gives the bond to Andy. The resulting three-party contractual situation is as follows: contract/ obligation issuance of guarantee application of guarantee Insurer Principal Contractor/Client Crown Insurance Company Andy Ang Bailey Contractor In this case, Bailey Contractor is still primarily liable to Andy Ang. Crown Insurance Company will either pay the money due, or ensure performance of the project contract, whichever is necessary, to satisfy Bailey Contractor’s obligation to Andy.

Advance payment bonds are required in cases where funds are advanced by the project owner to the contractor for the purpose of the pre-purchase of equipment, mobilisation of materials or equipment, or pre-work site preparation, etc. It is used to secure the repayment of the advance payment made by the project owner to the contractor.

Bid Bonds (also known as Tender Bonds) are usually required by the project owner, when a project is out for tender. The contractor’s tender has to be accompanied by the bid bond. The amount of the bond is usually a percentage (e.g. 5%) of the tendered sum.

The Bid Bond is an indication of the contractor’s capability and genuine interest to carry out the work being tendered for. It is effectively also a financial fall back, in the event that the successful contracting party is unable to carry out any part of the tender as submitted, and it becomes necessary to place the project, or part of it out for tender again.

Credit bonds are required in cases, where the owner of the project extends credit to the contractor during the contract term. This bond guarantees the payment of the sum guaranteed by the insurer for the outstanding charges and liabilities incurred by the contractor during the credit period.

Security bonds are usually required in respect of two categories of people. The first category will comprise visiting individuals requiring to remain in Singapore beyond the duration of the standard visitors’ entry passes. This will apply to foreigners coming to Singapore; e.g. to study or seek medical treatments, etc.

The second category refers to employers employing non-professional foreign workers (excluding Malaysians), such as domestic workers, construction workers, or workers in the manufacturing, marine, process or services sector. Such people are subject to Regulation 21 of the Immigration Regulations of the Immigration Act 1959 under which they are required to deposit a sum of S$5,000 per person with the Controller of Immigration. In lieu of the S$5,000 deposit in cash, they are allowed to substitute it with an immigration bond. The immigration bond serves as a guarantee that the foreign person and the employer will both observe the conditions as specified in the Visit Pass/Work Permit, and that, in event of a breach, the Controller of Immigration will turn to the insurer to pay up the S$5,000 deposit.

A licence and permit bond is required to guarantee that a corporation or individual will operate in accordance with government statutes and regulations, i.e. it guarantees that the client will conduct his business properly and not breach the contract. Examples of this class of bonds are the HDB renovation licence bonds, MDA (Media Development Authority) licensing bonds and travel agency licence bonds.

Maintenance bonds may be required by the owner of a project, upon the completion of the project, for protection against defective workmanship and materials during the warranty (also known as latent defects8) period. The purpose of such bond is to protect the owner against the contractor not being able to carry out the rectifications during the period. This is usually six to 12 months from the completion date of the project. 8 Latent defect is a hidden defect which cannot be discovered during inspection before the acceptance that a project has been successfully completed. Bond
In practice, this type of bond is seldom issued, as most performance bonds have a provision that incorporates maintenance periods. This is why performance bonds usually go beyond the project life.

Payment bonds are required by the owner of a project as a guarantee that the insurer will pay for certain labour, materials and supply bills, as well as subcontractors’ wages in the event that the contractor fails to pay these costs.
Performance bonds are usually required by the project owner after a project has been awarded to a particular contractor.

It is an indication that the contractor has the necessary skills and capabilities to carry out the required work and comply with the agreed terms and conditions of the contract. It secures payment for the project owner in the event the contract is not being fulfilled.

Rental Bonds are typically used by those who rent shop-houses from the Housing and Development Board (HDB)/Jurong Town Corporation (JTC). The HDB and JTC will usually require these tenants to place a rental deposit and reinstatement deposit in cash with them. The reinstatement deposit is only needed if the tenant is making some renovations to the unit he is renting. Instead of having to pay the deposit in cash, the tenant can purchase a Rental Bond and submit it to these statutory boards. This rental bond serves as a guarantee that, in the event the tenant fails to comply with the terms and conditions of the HDB/JTC rental contract, the board can rely on the insurer to pay the deposit.

Sub-contractor bonds are actually performance bonds that the sub-contractor9 is required to purchase according to the terms of the contract with the main contractor. This bond serves the same purpose as that of the performance bond which the owner of the project requires the contractor to purchase, and which is earlier discussed in this chapter. 9 A sub-contractor is an individual or a business firm contracted to perform part or all of the main contractor’s contract.
The owner of a project requires Supply bonds to ensure that the contractor who has been awarded the contract will supply the goods and services according to the terms of the contract.

Most bonds are required by the owners of a project (principal), or the authorities issuing licences or permits. They are accepted in lieu of the compulsory cash security deposit and, for the reasons (as described below), are actually often preferred by the principal to cash deposits. The risk of non-performance always exists, irrespective of who the contractor/client is. With the bond, the principal is assured that he will be compensated in the event of any non-performance.

The fact that the insurer also plays a part in ensuring the compliance with the contract will help to reduce the chances of incompetence, bankruptcy or poor workmanship, causing the project to be abandoned.
Bonds are subject to stringent underwriting. As such, this provides the principal with some comfort that there is a good chance that the contractor will complete the project.

In turn, the contractor/client also tends, for the reason mentioned below, to favour especially insurance bonds:
▪ It helps with the cash flow. This is because, instead of having to pay a large amount of cash deposit as security with the principal, the contractor/client needs to pay only a small premium for the bond. In this way, its liquidity and working capital are not affected and can be used for other business purposes.

Like insurance policies, bonds are also subject to underwriting. Usual underwriting practices include:
▪ hazard identification and evaluation;
▪ pricing; and
▪ monitoring the project.

When underwriting bonds, underwriters will need the following information to assess the risks:
▪ details of the contractor/client; and
▪ the contract document.
Details of the contractor are an important consideration in the underwriting of a contract bond. This involves evaluating the 3 C’s, i.e. Character, Capacity and Capital of the contractor.
The objective of assessing the character of the contractor is to ascertain that the contractor is honourable and dependable i.e. he can be counted on to perform.
The objective of assessing the capacity of the contractor is to determine the ability of the contractor to perform an undertaken project successfully.

In respect of capacity, the following information is to be obtained:
▪ plant size and suitability of equipment, including whether they are owned or leased;
▪ efficiency of the contractor’s management control; and
▪ details of the contractor’s continuity plan.
The objective of assessing the contractor’s capital is to find out his financial ability to assume the risks of business activity.

The customarily required underwriting information is the following audited:
▪ balance sheets;
▪ profit and loss accounts; and for the past three years or longer
▪ cash flow statements.
▪ Character
▪ Capacity
▪ Capital 3 C's

Besides the 3 C’s, underwriters will also take into account other information, such as the strength of counter guarantors. [Counter guarantors are people who guarantee the insurer that they will pay for any losses incurred in the event that a claim is made. In fact, they are paying the insurers after the insurers make payment under the bond should the contractor default. Counter guarantors are typically the owners of the firm and directors, but they can also be third parties.] Hence, this piece of information (the counter guarantor’s financial strength) will enable the underwriter to assess the application. The insurers usually require the counter guarantors to complete a Deed of Indemnity, also known as Letter of Indemnity (see Appendix 6A). Insurers will not release the bond until they have received the duly executed and signed copy of the Deed of Indemnity.

Some insurers provide questionnaires, which aim to extract the necessary underwriting information for general insurance advisers to get their clients to complete. A sample copy of the questionnaire is available in Appendix 6B.

The contract issued by the owner of the project to the contractor allows the underwriter to determine:
▪ whether the contractor is familiar with the type of work at hand;
▪ how soon the contract must be completed; and
▪ the terms and conditions that will influence the underwriter’s decision.

In addition, some information in the contract will also be used in issuing the bond document. As such, the contract needs to be submitted together with the other underwriting documents to the underwriter.

Once an application for a bond is approved, the underwriter will also decide on the premium to be charged. It is a one-time payment subject to charges for extensions. The premium rates for bonds can range from 1% to 3% per annum applied on the bond amount. Normally, the rate of premium charged by insurers is higher than that charged by the banks because of the level of collateral involved. Banks normally require full collateral for the amount of bank guarantee issued, whereas insurers seldom ask for full collateral. The extent of collateral sought by insurers is dependent on their assessment of the contractor’s financials. Note that the premium warranty clause does not apply to bonds, as bonds are irrevocable.

Underwriters will monitor the contractor’s progress on projects for which they have issued bonds to the contractor, as this information will be used for the future assessment of bond applications from the same contractor.

The bond wording usually begins with a bond number, this number is essential, and the parties involved need to quote this number in all their correspondence. The first paragraph of the bond serves the same purpose as the insuring clause of an insurance policy, i.e. it states the liability of the surety in the event of any default by the contractor. See Appendix 6C for a specimen of the bond wording.

The first paragraph explains why the bond is required, while the second paragraph is merely a formal way of saying that, if the contractor/client does not perform the duties as prescribed in the contract, the insurer will pay. The wording for the second paragraph differs according to the form of the bond being issued (see later section of this chapter for details).

A fourth paragraph is sometimes added to explain how to report claims, to describe how to terminate the bond, or to set forth other conditions or terms of the bond agreement. The General Insurance Association of Singapore (GIA) strongly encourages its members to adopt the best practice guideline10 to indicate the expiry date and/or latest date by which claims must be made when issuing guarantees. Insurers are also required to state clearly in the bond documents that the maximum claim period permitted is three months after the expiry of the guarantee.

The insurer usually issues the bond in duplicates. The original of the bond is meant for the principal, while the duplicate is meant for the contractor/client. Before delivering the bond documents to the contractor/client, the names of the principal and contractor/client, the bond amount, as well as the bond period, should be carefully and thoroughly checked to ensure that they are correctly reflected in the bond.

Bonds are usually issued in one of the following forms:
▪ unconditional on demand basis;
▪ unconditional general indemnity basis; or
▪ conditional indemnity basis. 10 GIA circular February 2008. The only exception to this practice would be guarantees issued for legal/court cases and for beneficiaries who are overseas and subject to foreign law.

When a bond is issued on an “unconditional on demand” basis, the insurer will have to make payment to the principal, once the principal makes a claim on the bond (see Appendix 6C for a sample of this bond). The contractor/client is not allowed to interfere to prevent payment under the bond. One exception to this rule is when the contractor/client obtains an injunction to prevent the insurer from paying out the bond.

On the other hand, if a bond is issued on an “unconditional general indemnity” basis, the principal cannot call on the bond if the contractor has properly performed in accordance with the terms of the contract. The condition for the bond to be called is that there must be default under the contract, and as a result of this default, the principal must suffer a loss for which the insurer must make payment to the principal. The bond is said to be unconditional in that any subsequent variation to the contract, or any forbearance granted to the contractor by the principal, will not affect the liability of the insurer under the bond.

As for bonds issued under “conditional indemnity” basis, the insurer has the right to examine any change in or variation to the contract, so as to determine whether the claim is admissible. In the event of any default by the contractor, the insurer has a right to arrange for another contractor to take over and complete the contract if it is economical for the insurer to do so.

In the event that a bond is called, the insurer will usually deal directly with the principal, i.e. the intermediary will not be involved. For demand bonds, the insurer will usually make the payment to the principal immediately and without any verification. For non-demand bonds (those issued on “unconditional general indemnity” and “conditional indemnity” basis), instead of calling for the bond, the principal may allow another contractor to complete the project. This may be done by way of a Novation Agreement. Similarly, for demand bonds, if the principal allows another contractor to complete the project, it can also be done by way of a Novation Agreement. The Novation Agreement is made among the parties, namely:
▪ the contractor;
▪ the new contractor; and
▪ the owner (principal).

The new contractor is bound by the terms made between the original contractor and the owner. Note that a Novation Agreement cannot be executed once the contract is terminated. Usually though, the owner will require the insurer to issue a new bond for the revised or same sum, once the Novation agreement is executed.

At the same time, the insurer will look to the contractor for reimbursement of the loss and expenses incurred. It is important that the contractor responds to the insurer’s request for information in a timely manner, as this will help to minimise the cost to all parties involved. Novation Agreement APPENDIX 6A To: ABC INSURANCE COMPANY (SINGAPORE) LIMITED 21 Any Street, ABC Centre, Singapore 654321 Policy No.: Agency: INDEMNITY The Contract:- The Contractor/Sub-Contractor/Employee:- Address:- The Principal/Owner/Developer/Employer:- The Amount Guaranteed: Singapore Dollars S$____________________________________________ In consideration of ABC INSURANCE COMPANY (SINGAPORE) LIMITED, (the Company) having at the request of THE CONTRACTOR agreed to provide an insurance guarantee/performance bond (which shall include guarantees or performance bonds arranged by and at the discretion of ABC INSURANCE COMPANY (SINGAPORE) LIMITED to be issued or provided by any other parties or institutions) for The Sum Guaranteed to THE PRINCIPAL guaranteeing the satisfactory performance completion of The Contract in accordance with terms as may be agreed, (the Undertaking). In consideration of the Company entering into or otherwise accepting liability under the Undertaking, the Sureties agree with the Company as follows:- SPECIMEN 1. Indemnity against liability under the Undertaking The Sureties convenant and undertake jointly and severally to indemnify the Company and irrevocably keep the Company indemnified against all demands, claims, actions, suits, liabilities, losses, costs and expenses whatsoever including all legal costs on a Solicitor and Client basis (Indemnity Basis) and other costs, charges and expenses that the Company may incur in connection with the Undertaking or in enforcing or attempting to enforce the Company’s rights under this indemnity. The Sureties irrevocably convenant and undertake jointly and severally to pay and reimburse such sums to the Company on demand together with interest on such sums from the date when such sums were first paid by the Company until payment of such sums by the Sureties in full, calculated from day to day at the rate of 1.5 per centum per month both before and after any Judgement. 2. Indemnity against Judgment or Order The Sureties agree that if any judgment or order against one or more of the Sureties is given or made for the payment of any amount due under this indemnity and is expressed in a currency other than that in which such amount is payable by the Sureties under this indemnity, the Sureties irrevocably jointly and severally convenant and undertake that they shall indemnify the Company against any loss incurred by the Company as a result of any variation having occurred in rates of exchange between the date as of which such amount is converted into such other currency in or for the purpose of enforcing such judgment or order and the date of actual payment pursuant to it. This clause shall constitute a separate and independent obligation on the part of the Sureties and shall continue in full force and effect notwithstanding any such judgment or order as stated above. 3. Authorisation to make payments The Sureties irrevocably authorise the Company to pay immediately on or to compromise any claim or to otherwise comply with any demand for payment which appears or purports to be made under the Undertaking without any reference to or further authority from all or any of the Sureties and without inquiry into the justification for such claim or demand or into the validity, genuineness or accuracy of any such claim or demand notwithstanding that the validity or amount of such claim or demand shall be disputed by one or more of the Sureties. The Sureties further agree that any such claim or demand on the Company and any payment made by the Company thereon shall be binding on the Sureties and shall, as between the Company and the Sureties, be conclusive evidence that the Company was liable to pay or comply with it and the Sureties liability to indemnify the Company. 4. Notices Any notice, demand or communications given under this indemnity to any party to the indemnity shall be in writing and shall be deemed to be duly served if left at or sent by pre-paid registered first class mail (or by pre-paid registered airmail, if overseas) or, by telefax, telex authenticated by answer back code to the last known address of the addressee. Any notice, demand or communication so sent shall be deemed to be received, if sent by pre-paid registered first class mail, 2 business days after posting (or 5 business days in the case of pre-paid registered airmail) or, if sent by telefax or telex, at the moment of dispatch and if left at the above mentioned last known address, at the time of delivery PROVIDED that if in the case of notices, demands or communications made or given by telex, telefax or delivery, the moment of dispatch or the time of delivery does not occur on a day which is a business day at the place where such notice, demand SPECIMEN or communication is received, such notice demand or communication shall not be deemed to have been received until the first business day afterwards at such place of receipt. In case of the death of any of the Sureties and until receipt by the Company of notice in writing of the Grant of Probate or Letters of Administration of the estate of the deceased, any demand sent as set out above to the last known address of the deceased shall, for all purposes, be deemed a sufficient demand by the Company to the deceased and his personal representatives and shall be as effectual as if the deceased were still living. 5. Effect of other indemnities The Company’s rights under this indemnity shall be in addition to and shall not be in any way prejudiced or affected by any one or more other indemnities, guarantees, securities or other obligations which the Company may now or may subsequently hold whether from one or more of the Sureties or from any other person. The Company may at any time and without reference to the Sureties give time for payment or grant any other indulgence and give up, deal with, vary, exchange or abstain from perfecting or enforcing any other indemnity, guarantee, security or other obligation held by the Company at any time and discharge any party to them or any of them, and realise them or any of them, and compound with, accept compositions from and make any other arrangements with the beneficiary of the Undertaking or any person, as the Company think fit, without affecting the liability of the Sureties under this indemnity. 6. Effect of other means of payment The Company is to be at liberty but not bound to resort for the Company’s own benefit to any other means of payment at any time and in any order the Company may think fit without in consequence diminishing the Sureties’ liability and the Company may enforce their rights under this indemnity either for the payment of the ultimate balance after resorting to other means of payment or for the amount due at any time notwithstanding that other means of payment have not been resorted to and in the latter case without entitling the Sureties to any benefit from such other means of payment so long as any money remains due or owning or payable whether actually or contingently from or by the Sureties to the Company. 7. Modification of the Undertaking The Undertaking may from time to time, without need for the consent or knowledge of the Sureties, be modified or altered. Without prejudice to the generality of the foregoing, the amount of the Undertaking may be increased or decreased or the Undertaking may be renewed or further extended either in accordance with its original terms, or upon request and the agreement thereto of the Company and the beneficiary of the Undertaking. The Sureties liability under this indemnity shall continue to apply to the Undertaking as so modified, altered, renewed or extended from time to time. 8. Joint and several liability of partners Where a Surety is a partnership or otherwise consists of more than one person the liability of that Surety under this indemnity shall be deemed to be the joint and several liability of the partners and any demand for payment made by the Company to any one or more of the persons so jointly and severally liable shall be deemed to be a demand made to all such persons. The Company may release or discharge any one or more of such persons from liability under this indemnity or compound with, accept compositions from or make any other arrangements with any of such persons without in consequence releasing or affecting the Company’s rights and remedies against the other of such persons. SPECIMEN 9. Release and Discharge The Company may release or discharge any one or more of the Sureties from liability under this indemnity or compound with, accept compositions from or make any other arrangements with any one or more of the Sureties without in consequence releasing or affecting the Company’s rights and remedies against any of the remaining Sureties. 10. Invalid Execution If any one or more of the intended Sureties under this indemnity who may have been expressly or impliedly contemplated to be Co-Sureties under this indemnity is not bound by the provisions of this indemnity whether by reason of his, her or their lack of capacity or improper execution of or failure to execute this indemnity or for any other reason whatsoever, the remaining Sureties shall continue to be bound by the provisions of this indemnity as if each of such other intended Sureties whose execution of the indemnity is so invalid, had never been a party thereto. 11. Not to claim in competition Each of the Sureties waive all rights of subrogation and agree not to claim any set off or counterclaim against any other Surety or to claim or prove in competition with the Company in the event of the bankruptcy, liquidation winding up or insolvency of any other Surety or to have any benefit of or share in any guarantee, other indemnity or security now or hereinafter held by the Company until the Company has received a full indemnity in accordance with this indemnity. 12. Effect of delay or omission No delay or omission on the Company’s part in exercising any right, power, privilege or remedy in respect of this indemnity shall impair such right, power, privilege or remedy, or be construed as a waiver of it, nor shall any single or partial exercise of any such right, power, privilege or remedy preclude any further exercise of it or the exercise of any other right, power, privilege or remedy. The rights, powers, privileges and remedies provided in this indemnity are cumulative and not exclusive of any rights, powers, privileges or remedies provided by the law. 13. Governing law and jurisdiction This indemnity shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the law of Singapore and it is irrevocably agreed for the exclusive benefit of the Company that the courts of Singapore are to have jurisdiction to settle any dispute which may arise out of or in connection with this indemnity and that accordingly any suit, action or proceeding arising out of or in connection with this indemnity (in this clause referred to as “proceedings”) may be brought in such courts. Nothing in this clause shall limit the right of the Company to take proceedings against any one or more of the Sureties in any other court of competent jurisdiction, nor shall the taking of proceedings in one or more jurisdictions preclude the taking of proceedings in any other jurisdiction, whether concurrently or not. 14. Severability Notwithstanding that any provisions of this indemnity may prove to be illegal or otherwise unenforceable the remaining provisions of this indemnity shall continue in full force and effect. 15. Interpretation In this indemnity where the context permits the singular includes the plural and vice versa and reference to persons include references to companies. SPECIMEN 16. Headings The headings of each clause of this indemnity are inserted solely for ease of reference and shall not at all affect the interpretation of the clauses.
