chapters.title: PECUNIARY INSURANCE
learningOutcomes: know what Fidelity Guarantee Insurance is all about
realise the need for Fidelity Guarantee Insurance
know the parties to a Fidelity Guarantee Insurance Contract
know the various types of Fidelity Guarantee Insurance policies
understand the scope of cover, period of cover, policy provisions and conditions, underwriting information required, exclusions, extensions and claims of Fidelity Guarantee Insurance
understand the coverage for Crime Insurance and the key differences between Crime Insurance and Fidelity Guarantee Insurance
know what Money Insurance is all about
realise the need for Money Insurance
understand the scope of cover, period of cover, policy provisions and conditions, underwriting information required, exclusions, extensions and claims of Money Insurance
know what Credit Insurance is all about
realise the need for Credit Insurance
know the parties to the Credit Insurance contract
list the types of Credit Insurance policies offered in the market
understand the scope of cover, period of cover, policy provisions and conditions, underwriting information required, exclusions, claim procedures and claim documents of Credit Insurance
know the scope of cover and underwriting information required of Political Risks Insurance

paragraphs.text:
The word “Pecuniary” means “relating to money and pecuniary insurance covers businesses against purely financial losses (e.g. from fraud, legal expenses or business interruption) rather than physical damage to property”.1 We shall be discussing three classes of Pecuniary insurances in this chapter, namely:
▪ Fidelity Guarantee Insurance;
▪ Money Insurance; and
▪ Credit Insurance.

The word “fidelity”, in its simplest meaning, implies “faithful or loyal performance of a duty”. A lack of fidelity, resulting from dishonesty or disloyalty of employee(s), can be insured against. Insurers use the term “fidelity guarantee” for these policies because they are accepting the fidelity risk of employees within the policy wording. Some insurers have recently dropped the word “guarantee” from the heading and referred to such covers solely as Fidelity Insurance. Specifically, Fidelity Guarantee (FG) Insurance is an undertaking by the insurer to make good any financial loss incurred by the insured (employer) resulting from an employee’s defalcation (i.e. the act or an instance of embezzling), forgery or fraudulent acts.

subsections.label: B
subsections.title: The Need For Fidelity Guarantee Insurance

paragraphs.text: Employers should consider FG Insurance to guard against any potential exposure to financial loss arising from fraud or dishonest acts committed by their employees. These losses can be potentially huge. Even with the most stringent employment criteria, the tightest security systems, check and control, the opportunities for fraud still exist. In many cases, employees who defraud their employers were prompted by various reasons, such as financial difficulty or sudden emotional strain. An example will be an employee in the finance department quietly drawing off a small amount of petty cash every week, thus making a huge pile of money over an undiscovered lengthy period of time. 1 Source: http://www.cii.co.uk/membership/new-starters/types-of-insurance/

A FG Insurance policy involves three parties, namely:
▪ the insurer;
▪ the insured (the employer); and
▪ the insured person (the employee).

The majority of FG Insurance policies are issued to commercial firms to indemnify the employer (known as the insured under the policy) against direct pecuniary loss that the employer may sustain through acts of dishonesty by the employee(s) in the course of employment. The pecuniary loss usually refers to loss of money or other goods and property.

Such acts must have been committed during:
▪ the period of insurance;
▪ the uninterrupted continuance of the employment of the defaulting employee; and
▪ must be in connection with the occupation and duties of the employee.

In addition, the insurer is not liable, unless such acts are discovered during the “discovery period”. There is no standard definition for “discovery period”, but obviously, it includes discovery during the period of insurance as stated in the schedule of the policy. However, most insurers give allowances for the dishonest or fraudulent acts to be discovered:
▪ within a stipulated time (e.g. six or 12 months) after the termination of the policy; or
▪ within a stipulated time (e.g. six or 12 months) after the resignation, retirement, dismissal or death of the defaulting employee, whichever happens first.

The policy will also usually state that, upon discovery of the default/loss, the insured must promptly notify the insurer. The insurer will not be liable for any claim if the loss is not reported within 12 months of discovery of it being committed. The insurer’s liability will be limited to the sum insured, i.e. the amount of guarantee. This is of particular importance, as fraud cases may be spread over a long time, covering several periods of insurance. The insurance cover is not cumulative from year to year.

subsections.label: E
subsections.title: Types Of Fidelity Guarantee Insurance Policies

paragraphs.text: As described in Table 7.1 below, there are four forms of FG Insurance policies available in the insurance market. Table 7.1: FG Insurance Policies In Insurance Market Policy Type Description (a) Individual Policy This type of policy is used where an individual employee is covered, by name, for a stated amount. The loss must be discovered within 24 months (maximum) of the event. (b) Collective Policy This type of policy is preferable when two or more employees are to be covered. As with an individual policy, the schedule sets out the names and positions of the employees, and the amount for which each is covered. (c) Floating Policy A Floating policy is similar to the Collective policy in that the individual employees are named, but only one amount of guarantee is given under a Floating policy. This means to say that only one amount of insurance “floats” over all persons covered. This amount of guarantee stipulates the maximum amount for which the insurer is liable, regardless of the number of faults occurred or the number of employees involved in the default. (d) Blanket Policy This is a form of unnamed policy, which includes all employees without showing their names or positions. Staff members are covered based on broad categories, such as “inside staff” or “outside staff”. The amount of cover may be per employee or per loss occurrence.

This has already been discussed in the above section – the policy operates during the period of insurance (usually 12 months), and for any acts of dishonesty or fraud discovered during the “discovery period”.

The employer is obliged to retain money which it may legally control belonging to the defaulting employee (e.g. wages), and such amounts shall be deducted from any amounts payable by the insurer under the policy. This condition is specific to a FG Insurance policy.

Note that a detailed discussion on the policy provisions and conditions common to most general insurance policies are in the “Basic Insurance Concepts & Principles” Study Guide, published by the Singapore College of Insurance Limited.
The customarily required underwriting information is described below.

The policy pays for loss of cash and stock, the potential risks are associated with the incidence of cash-handling and the value of stock. The larger the amount of cash circulating within the business premises, the greater will be the possibility of employees misappropriating the funds. Similarly, the higher the value of stock coupled with its portability, the greater will be the probability of loss. Take the example of a manufacturer of high-end electronic chips. Apart from being small in size and highly portable, such chips are also highly valuable in the electronics market.

Since the rate varies directly with the loss potential, classification of the employees is essential, as jobs which require frequent cash-handling are of higher risk as compared to other jobs which do not.

In practice, one may find the following classification: CLASSIFICATION OF EMPLOYEES Class 1 Not directly involved in the handling of cash or in the immediate control of stock. Class 2 Considerable involvement with cash and goods, such as a stock and store supervisor. Class 3 Direct involvement with cash and/or goods, such as bill collectors and warehouse managers.

The common type of cover provides for a limit on any employee, but the premium does not increase proportionately as a higher limit is insured. In fact, the premium rate decreases as the limit is increased, since there is no correlation between the sum insured and the risk.
An insured’s claims experience will have an impact on the amount of guarantee and the type of FG Insurance policy chosen.
Note that the underwriter may ask for additional information, and the details of the information requested may differ from insurer to insurer.
Note that the exclusions of a FG Insurance policy may vary from insurer to insurer.

Optional extensions vary from insurer to insurer. The list below identifies some of the common ones available as extensions to the basic cover. (a) Auditors’ Fees An auditor may be engaged to substantiate the amount of loss. This extension is meant to indemnify the insured for the auditors’ fees, but subject to the approval of the insurer. (b) Automatic Additions & Deletions This extension provides automatic covers for all additions and deletions of employees falling within the same classification of employees as specified in the policy. The insured undertakes to declare such listing of additions and deletions to the insurer at the end of the period of insurance (usually 12 months). (c) Premium Adjustment Blanket policies covering all employees will normally be adjustable annually on the declared payroll, or on the number of employees employed during each policy period of insurance.

In addition, the insured must permit the insurer to enquire into, investigate, and examine the circumstances of the claim. Some insurers also stipulate as a policy condition that the insured needs to take all steps to obtain the conviction of the guilty person in the event of any act of fraud or dishonesty.

Regardless of size, all organisations are vulnerable to workplace fraud. Fraud can take many forms, including embezzlement, forgery, theft of inventory and other assets, and computer crime. Often, these fraud acts can continue undetected and unchecked for years. The financial impact of these so-called white-collar crimes can be devastating.

Besides the traditional FG Insurance as described above, certain insurers now offer a wider form of coverage known as Crime Insurance. Crime Insurance can protect the assets (both financial and non-financial) of an organisation, particularly against white-collar crimes. Higher limits can be offered on a standalone basis. The definition of employees is broader as compared to that of the traditional FG Insurance.

The Crime Insurance policy can provide coverage such as the following: (a) Employee Theft: Loss of money, securities and other property caused by theft or forgery by an employee; (b) Premises Cover: Loss sustained owing to the destruction, disappearance or abstraction of moneys and securities within or from the client's premises by third parties; (c) Transit Cover: Loss sustained owing to the destruction, disappearance or abstraction of moneys and securities outside the client's premises by a third party, while being conveyed by the insured or any authorised person; (d) Depositors Forgery Cover: Loss resulting from instruments which have been fraudulently drawn upon the company's accounts by a third party; and (e) Computer Theft & Funds Transfer Fraud Cover: Loss resulting from the intentional taking of moneys or securities through the use of a computer or fraudulent instructions to a financial institution to transfer, pay or deliver moneys or securities.

The table below summarises some of the differences between FG Insurance and Crime Insurance. Fidelity Guarantee Insurance Crime Insurance Covers "act" of theft. Covers "conduct" of fraud and dishonesty. The onus is on the insured to identify the employees who acted. Covers loss even by unidentifiable employees. The insured may be required to secure a prosecution of the perpetrator, before the loss is payable. There is no such requirement. Does not cover a loss perpetrated through electronic means. Covers loss perpetrated through the electronic medium. Does not cover overseas employees. Covers all employees, including those located overseas. The insured may be required to declare to the insurer if there are any changes to the job scope, remuneration or internal controls that affect the employees. There is no such requirement. Does not cover investigative costs that are incurred by the insured in establishing the loss. Includes coverage for investigative costs incurred by the insured, in order to establish the quantum of the fraud. Limits are small because of underwriting restrictions. Insurers are prepared to offer high limits on a stand-alone basis.

However, the underwriting information required by the insurer of Crime Insurance is far more extensive than that of FG Insurance. Specific underwriting information required for Crime Insurance includes details of human resources and payroll, internal and external auditing procedures, electronic funds transfers, purchasing controls, account payable controls, inventory controls, money and securities controls, computer systems protection, client services and loss experience over the last five years.

In addition, the insurer of Crime Insurance requires the intending insured (proposer) to furnish the following certified documents:
▪ a copy of the internal auditors’ audit plan for the current year;
▪ a copy of the most recent audited financial statement; and
▪ a copy of the Certified Public Accountant (CPA) management letter on internal controls and management’s response.
The insurer can possibly undertake an investigation and inquiry in connection with the completed proposal form that it deems necessary to do so.

Money Insurance is an important class of Pecuniary Insurance. As its name implies, it is an insurance for “money”. The definition of “money” in this context includes cash, bank and currency notes, cheques, bank drafts, money orders, credit company sales vouchers belonging to the insured (organisation), and current postage stamps.

Certain trades handle large volumes of cash transactions, such as money changers, retailers and departmental stores, or store money in various places, such as in the business premises or during transit to or from a bank. When such large amounts of money go missing or get stolen, organisations may experience serious cash-flow problems. Therefore, it is wise that these organisations consider taking up Money Insurance to protect against the risk of physical money loss.
However, for each of the covers, the insurer is liable only up to the amount stated in the schedule of the policy as the limit of liability for any one loss or occurrence.
Money Insurance policies provide coverage for loss of money which occurs during the period of insurance (usually 12 months).

Some of the common provisions and conditions which are specific to a Money Insurance policy are described below. (a) Reasonable Precautions The insured must act as if uninsured i.e. take all due precautions for the safety and protection of the property insured. (b) Adjustment Where the premium for this policy is based on the estimates furnished by the insured, the insured must keep an accurate record of all relevant particulars (e.g. money in transit during the period) and must allow the insurer to inspect such record. Within one month from the expiry of the period of insurance, the insured must furnish such record and information as required by the insurer, so that the premium may be adjusted accordingly.

Note that a detailed discussion on the policy provisions and conditions common to most general insurance policies is in the “Basic Insurance Concepts & Principles” Study Guide, published by the Singapore College of Insurance Limited.
Note that the underwriter may require additional information, and the details of the required information may differ from insurer to insurer.
Note that the exclusions of a Money Insurance policy may vary from insurer to insurer.

Some common extensions specific to the policy cover are described below. (a) Loss From Locked Vehicles This will be subject to a specified limit. (b) Loss Of Insured’s Money Within The Private Residence Of Employees A sub-limit will apply. This limit will be nominal; usually not more than S$2,000. (c) Personal Accident Benefit In the event of an assault on the insured person resulting in bodily injury, the insurer will pay Total Permanent Disability or Permanent Partial Disability or death benefits, but subject to a maximum of two employees for any one accident, and the limit per employee is usually S$10,000. (d) Accumulation Clause This extension clause allows the limit of liability for any one loss to be raised to a higher level for cash-peak periods, such as weekends, public holidays, festive seasons, bonus payment period, where the insured may tend to hold a higher amount of cash.

On discovery of any event which may give rise to a claim under this policy, the insured must:
▪ give immediate notice to the police and the insurer;
▪ take all practical steps to discover any guilty person and recover the lost money; and
▪ within seven days (or further time as allowed by the insurer) deliver a written claim to the insurer, together with all necessary information and other relevant documentary evidence relating to the claim. The supply of such information and documents shall be at the insured's expense.

Credit is the system of buying or selling of goods or services, without immediate payment being made. It is a practice, whereby the purchaser receives the goods or accepts the service, in return for his promise that payment will be made on or before a future specified date. The granting of credit to the buyer is an important tool for the seller, in order to compete in the market. The buyer will always request for credit from the seller or supplier, in order to improve his cash flow. Therefore, credit has become both a boon and bane in trading.
Credit Insurance provides cover to companies or corporations in the event of default in payments by their customers or buyers for the goods or services provided.

Credit Insurance deals with the risk to sellers and businesses of non-payment by their buyers and customers. This is to say that, when the time comes for payment to be made, the buyer may be unable to pay. By means of this form of insurance, the seller ensures that, if his debtors (buyers) fail to meet their obligations, he can recoup his losses, at least in part, from his insurer. Essentially, this type of cover will improve one’s credit management.

A Credit Insurance involves two parties, namely:
▪ the insurer; and
▪ the insured (the “seller” of the goods or services).
There are different types of policies offered by the insurer. The type of policy offered to the insured will depend on the requirements of the insured.

The most common form of Credit Insurance is the Comprehensive policy which generally provides cover on the total business portfolio of the insured. However, exclusion of covers, such as related companies’ sales and cash sales, are permitted.

The Comprehensive policy provides credit cover for the insured on his exports (i.e. transactions made with an overseas buyer), as well as his domestic sales (i.e. transactions made with a buyer in Singapore). However, there are also different types of Comprehensive policies available, such that if the insured chooses to insure his overseas transactions only, a Comprehensive policy (Shipments) will be issued. The risks insured and terms of cover can vary depending on the needs of the Insured.

The Comprehensive policy covers short-term credit risks of up to 180 days and this is suitable for goods which are sold on a repetitive basis. The other common short-term policies which are available for manufacturers and retailers are mentioned in the following page.

This type of cover is issued to insure credit sales made between a Singapore manufacturer/retailer and his local buyers. The Domestic Credit Insurance insures against the buyer’s insolvency and payment default.

This type of policy provides coverage against pre-shipment risk of the insured who manufactures goods that are custom-made. The Contracts policy provides indemnity to the insured on the “loss” incurred, in the event that the buyer goes insolvent during the manufacturing period before shipment and delivery of goods. However, cancellation of contract by the buyer during the manufacturing period is not covered.

This policy is usually recommended to large companies, which have its own internal credit management and control. The insured will take a pre-approved amount as an “Annual Aggregate Deductible” for the policy. For example, if the pre-approved Annual Aggregate Deductible amount is set as S$500,000 per insured period, the insured will absorb the approved losses up to S$500,000, occurring during the insured period. Any amount suffered which is above S$500,000 will be indemnified by the insurer.

The Specific policy is offered to the insured who sells semi-capital goods with credit terms of one to two years' credit. As its name implies, the Specific policy provides special terms of cover relating to a specific contract between the insured and the buyer.

Most of the Credit Insurance policies provide cover for both commercial and political risks. However, for the short-term transactions, the insured can also request for insurance cover on commercial risks only, if the insured transacts within countries in the OECD2 markets. Premium for cover on commercial risks only will be lower than policies with full risk cover.

Except for the Domestic Credit Insurance policy, commercial and political risks cover is available under the Comprehensive policy, Contracts policy and Specific policy. Commercial risks relate to the risks of the buyer, such as the buyer’s insolvency and payment default. Political risks relate to political changes or instability in a country and they will include foreign exchange transfer, cancellation of export or import licences, war and civil disturbances in the buyer’s country.

Most Credit Insurance policies do not provide full indemnity. The insured indemnity in general is up to 90% only. The insurer may also reduce the insured indemnity if the insured wants to take a higher retention.

The period of cover for a Credit Insurance policy is 12 months for the short-term policies, and cover can be renewed or terminated by written notice given to either the insurer or the insured. The period of cover for the Specific policy will normally follow the credit terms of the sales contract with the buyer. 2 The Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) is the international organisation of the industrialised, market-economy countries. At OECD, representatives from 30 member countries meet to exchange information and harmonise policy with a view to maximising economic growth within member countries and assisting non-member countries develop more rapidly.

Note that a detailed discussion on the policy provisions and conditions common to most general insurance policies is in the “Basic Insurance Concepts & Principles” Study Guide, published by the Singapore College of Insurance Limited.
Note that the underwriter may require additional information, and the details of the required information may differ from insurer to insurer.
Note that the exclusions of a Credit Insurance policy may vary from insurer to insurer.

The insured is required under the terms of the policy to notify the insurer of any non-payment by his buyer, and take all necessary steps to minimise loss and recover the outstanding debts, including (if necessary) the institution of legal action.

The insured is required to submit details of the outstanding debt (by completing a Claim Form) together with all necessary supporting documents to file his claim. Under the terms of the policy, all valid claims will be paid at the end of the claim waiting period, provided that the insured has fulfilled the terms of the policy. Recovery action will continue after the claim payment. In this respect, the insurer will assess the prospects of recovery and advise the insured on the appropriate action to be taken to recover the debt. Accredited debt recovery agents and legal firms are normally used to recover outstanding debts.
Credit Insurance covers non-payment for goods or services. Political Risks Insurance (PRI) covers this as well but also covers the destruction of physical assets.

Another situation is where a company has physical assets, equipment, contractors’ plant, construction equipment, consignment stocks held abroad. PRI covers such assets against:
▪ Confiscation, expropriation, nationalisation or deprivation (CEND) of the assets. Deprivation would, for example, include the host government refusing to allow the equipment to be exported back to the owner’s country;
▪ Forced abandonment - where the assets have to be abandoned because of political violence or because the host government has expelled the insured’s personnel; and
▪ Political violence causing destruction of the assets, stocks of goods, etc.
Insurers require information such as details of the applicant, project details, asset details, project financial structure, loss history, applicant’s relations with host government etc.

chapters.title: FOREIGN WORKER INSURANCE
outline: Insurer’s Letter Of Guarantee For Security Deposit
learningOutcomes: know the employer’s responsibility for employing foreign workers
know the compulsory Medical Insurance coverage provided by the majority of Foreign Worker Medical Insurance policies, and the exclusions applicable
understand the functions of the Insurer’s Letter of Guarantee for Security Deposit
identify the policy conditions and underwriting information required for Foreign Worker Medical Insurance
know the claim procedure of Foreign Worker Medical Insurance

paragraphs.text: Singapore is an industrialised city state with limited land and resources. To aid in the rapid development and growth, Singapore employs many foreigners in various occupations, from source countries as approved by the Ministry of Manpower, Singapore (MOM).
Under employment regulations, a foreigner who wishes to work in Singapore must apply for a work pass, which is administered, approved and issued by MOM.

MOM issues various types of work passes based on different requirements, for example:  Employment Pass (EP): This is for foreign professionals, managers, and executives. Candidates need to earn a minimum salary per month and have acceptable qualifications.  EntrePass: For eligible foreign entrepreneurs who are keen to start and operate a business in Singapore that is venture-backed or possesses innovative technologies.  Personalised Employment Pass: For high-earning existing Employment Pass holders or overseas foreign professionals. The PEP offers greater flexibility than an Employment Pass.  Overseas Networks & Expertise Pass: For top talent in business, arts and culture, sports, as well as academia and research.  S Pass (SP): This is for mid-level skilled staff. Candidates also need to earn a minimum salary per month and meet the assessment criteria.  Work Permit (WP) for migrant worker: This is for semi-skilled foreign workers in the construction, manufacturing, marine shipyard, process, or service sectors.

Employers who employ workers on a work permit for foreign worker need to be aware of the following requirements (among others): (a) Medical Insurance Requirements For Foreign Worker The employer needs to buy a medical insurance plan for each foreign worker he employs. He also needs to submit the insurance details online before the Work Permit can be issued. The Ministry of Manpower (MOM) has implemented enhancements to the mandatory medical insurance (MI) for all new and existing Work Permit (including Migrant Domestic Workers) and S Pass holders. Stage 1 of these enhancements was implemented on 1 July 2023 while stage 2 will be implemented for insurance policies, renewals or extensions that have a start date effective on or after 1 July 2025.1 Employers of work permit and S Pass holders need to provide their workers medical insurance coverage with an annual claim limit of at least $60,000. This implementation of the enhanced requirements by the MOM took effect for policies with start date effective on or after 1 July 2023 (i.e. stage one implementation). For policies with start date before 1 July 2023, the minimum annual claim limit was S$15,000. The insurance should cover inpatient care and day surgery, including hospital bills for conditions that may not be work related. Employers also have to co-pay 25 per cent of claim amounts that go above the first $15,000, up to an annual claim limit of at least $60,000. This means that if a worker incurs a $60,000 bill for heart surgery, for instance, the employer will have to pay $11,250, which is 25 per cent of $45,000. The insurer will cover the other $48,750.2 What if a worker incurs a larger hospitalisation bill? The apportionment of pay out between the insurer and the employer for several scenarios is illustrated in the figure below (denoted as ANNEX A by MOM) extracted from the MOM website.3 1 https://www.mom.gov.sg/newsroom/press-releases/2023/0331-implementation-of-enhanced-medicalinsurance- for-foreign-employees-to-better-support-employers 2 Extracted from Straits Times article “Employers must provide medical insurance coverage of at least $60,000 for maids, migrant workers from July” dated 31 March 2023. 3 https://www.mom.gov.sg/-/media/mom/documents/press-releases/2022/enhanced-medical-insurancecoverage- for-work-permit-and-s-pass-holders---annex-a.pdf Note that “Current MI” in the above figure refers to the medical insurance requirements in effect for policies incepting before 1 July 2023 and “Enhanced MI” refers to the requirements that take effect for policies incepting from 1 July 2023 onwards. From 1 July 2025 (i.e. stage two implementation), several other changes will also be implemented.4 Employers will no longer need to pay for their workers’ medical treatment upfront and claim the amount from insurers later. Insurers will be required to reimburse hospitals directly. 5 There will also be a standard list of allowable exclusions for insurers, such as procedures that are not medically necessary, such as cosmetic surgery. Insurers will not have to cover the treatments for pre-existing illnesses occurring within the first 12 months of work under the same employer. 4 Extracted from Straits Times article “Employers must provide medical insurance coverage of at least $60,000 for maids, migrant workers from July” dated 31 March 2023. 5 ibid The standard list of allowable exclusions as mentioned above are detailed in the table below as extracted from the MOM website, denoted as ANNEX B by MOM.6 ANNEX B List of allowable exclusions Insurers are only allowed to exclude the following treatment items, procedures, conditions and activities in their medical insurance product for Work Permit and S pass holders: Group A: Treatment that is elective and not medically necessary  Ambulance fees  Cosmetic surgery  Dental work (except due to accidental injuries)  Vaccination Infertility, sub-fertility, assisted conception or any contraceptive operation, including their related complications  Sex change operations, including their related complications.  Expenses incurred after the 7th calendar day from being certified to be medically fit for discharge from inpatient treatment and assessed to have a feasible discharge option by a medical practitioner  Optional items which are outside the scope of treatment  Health screening examinations for the purpose of diagnosis and any treatment of a preventive nature Group B: Treatment resulting from employers’/workers’ acts  Treatment of conditions or injuries arising from any malicious / willful / illegal acts by employer or employer’s family members  Treatment for conditions or injuries arising from any criminal acts committed by worker  Maternity charges (including Caesarean operations or abortions, and their related complications)  Treatment for conditions or injuries arising from voluntary participation in hazardous sports  Treatment of conditions or injuries arising directly or indirectly from nuclear fallout, war and related risk  Repeat occurrence of: (a) Treatment of conditions or injuries arising from drug addiction (except that of illicit drugs) or alcoholism (b) Treatment of conditions or injuries arising from participation in civil commotion, riot, or strike (c) Treatment of mental conditions and conditions or injuries arising from self-inflicted injuries and attempted suicide (d) Treatment of venereal diseases and/or sexually transmitted diseases 6 https://www.mom.gov.sg/-/media/mom/documents/press-releases/2022/enhanced-medicalinsurance- coverage-for-work-permit-and-s-pass-holders---annex-b.pdf Group C: Others  Treatment of conditions deemed as pre-existing illnesses within the first 12 months of employment under the same employer  Overseas medical treatment  Private nursing charges  Purchase of medical equipment  Medical repatriation  Outpatient rehabilitation such as physiotherapy, occupational therapy and speech therapy, unless recommended by a medical practitioner during hospitalization period  Traditional Chinese medicine or any forms of alternative treatment  Treatment which has received reimbursement from Work Injury Compensation Insurance (WICI) and other forms of insurance coverage Insurers must also offer different premiums for workers aged 50 and below, and those above the age of 50, from 1 July 2025.7 The employer cannot pass on the cost of purchasing the medical insurance to Work Permit holders. In summary, the implementation of the enhanced requirements by the MOM is detailed in the table below.8 7 ibid 8https://www.mom.gov.sg/-/media/mom/documents/press-releases/2023/annex_implementation-ofenhanced- medical-insurance-for-foreign-employees-to-better-support-employers.pdf (b) Security Bond Requirements The employer has to post a S$5,000 Security Bond in the form of an insurance/banker's guarantee in respect of each foreign worker (except that, if the foreign worker is a Malaysian). The bond is between the employer and the Government of Singapore. It is used to ensure that both the employer and his workers comply with the conditions of the Work Permit. The employer cannot ask the worker to pay for the bond.
Employers of S Pass employees are subject to the same requirements for Medical Insurance, but there is no requirement to put up the Security Bond.

In addition, under the Work Injury Compensation Act 2019 referred to as [WICA] in this chapter, employers are required to purchase Work Injury Compensation Insurance (WICI) to insure all employees (local and foreign) who are involved in manual work, regardless of their level of earnings. As for employees who are in non-manual work, the employers are required to have them covered under WICI for only those persons having monthly earnings of S$2,600 or less each. We had discussed WICI in Chapter 3 of this Study Guide.

There are in the insurance market stand-alone Medical Insurance policies (see an example as shown in Foreign Worker Medical Insurance-Product Summary and Provisions in Appendix 8A) to cover the mandatory Medical Insurance requirements for Work Permit and S Pass workers. Note that in connection with these, the policy wording differs among insurers in respect of coverage, provisions, terms, exclusions and conditions. As mentioned above, from 1 July 2025, there will also be a standard list of allowable exclusions for insurers, such as procedures that are not medically necessary, such as cosmetic surgery.

It is important to note that, under a Foreign Worker Medical Insurance policy, the insured person is the Foreign Worker (FW), while the employer is the insured or policy owner (also known as policyholder) who is responsible for paying the premiums due under the policy.

Immigration Bonds are also available to employers to satisfy the S$5,000 Security Bond requirement for each worker on a work permit for foreign worker. A Security Bond Application Form for FW is shown in Appendix 8B.
This is the compulsory Medical Insurance coverage that employers must provide for S Pass and work permit holders. This Medical Insurance is usually offered as a stand-alone policy.

The insurer will indemnify the insured (i.e. the employer) for hospital and surgical expenses incurred in Singapore by the insured person (i.e. the FW), in the event that the insured FW requires to be hospitalised because of accidental bodily injury, sickness or disease.

Coverage for hospital and surgical expenses usually includes:  hospital room and board;  intensive care unit (ICU);  surgical fees, including day surgery;  in-hospital doctor’s visit and consultation;  hospitalisation miscellaneous expenses;  pre-hospitalisation diagnostic X-ray and laboratory tests (incurred within 90 days prior to hospital confinement or surgical operation);  pre-hospitalisation specialist consultation fees (incurred within 90 days prior to hospital confinement or surgical operation); and  post-hospitalisation treatment (incurred within 90 days immediately following discharge from the hospital).
Coverage for hospital and surgical expenses are provided on an “as charged” basis, but subject to an aggregate maximum sum of S$60,000 for each insured person per policy year or period of insurance.

The other important points are: (a) Coverage is usually restricted to hospitalisation in a Singapore government hospital or restructured hospital. Some insurers further stipulate that the hospitalisation must be in Class “C” or “B2” ward in such a hospital only. (b) Some policies impose co-insurance and/or deductible. The co-insurance and deductible may be removed by paying higher premiums. Employers also have to co-pay 25 per cent of claim amounts that go above the first $15,000. (c) Hospitalisation miscellaneous expenses include: charges for operating room, X-ray examinations, and medicines, dressings, ordinary splints, plaster casts, electrocardiograms (ECG), basal metabolism tests, laboratory tests/examinations, intravenous infusions, physiotherapy, blood transfusions and other customary services rendered or supplied during hospitalisation. (d) Pre-hospitalisation diagnostic X-ray and laboratory tests are payable only if they are incurred within 90 days prior to hospitalisation or surgery. Payment will not be paid by the insurer for clinical treatments, or if the insured person is not subsequently hospitalised or surgically treated after such examinations or consultations. (e) Similarly, pre-hospitalisation specialist consultation fees are payable only if they are incurred within 90 days prior to hospitalisation or surgery. Payment will not be paid by the insurer for clinical treatments, or if the insured person is not subsequently hospitalised or surgically treated after such consultations or examinations.

Other than the usual exclusions that relate to war, warlike operation, strike, riot, civil commotion and nuclear risks, some common exclusions specific to this class of insurance are as follows:  treatment for pre-existing condition or illness, unless this section/policy has been in force for 12 months or longer, although some insurers do not exclude pre-existing illness for certain plans;9  expenses in respect of sexually transmitted disease, HIV (Human Immune Deficiency Virus) and/or any HIV related illness, including AIDS (Acquired Immune Deficiency Syndrome), AIDS Related Complex or other communicable diseases; 9 From 1 July 2025, insurers will not have to cover the treatments for pre-existing illnesses occurring within the first 12 months of work under the same employer.  expenses resulting from criminal or unlawful act of the insured person, attempted suicide, intentionally self-inflicted injury, mental condition, drug abuse or alcoholism;  services and supplies not recommended, approved and performed by a legally qualified registered medical practitioner;  expenses in respect of preventive treatment or medicine, or routine medical examination (e.g. vaccination);  expenses related to pregnancy, childbirth, abortion, miscarriage and contraception;  medical expenses recoverable under Work Injury Compensation Act 2019 or similar Act, legislation or regulation;  dental care or treatment, except for accidental injuries to sound natural teeth; and  treatment of optional nature, such as cosmetic (aesthetic) surgery.

Note that the exclusions may vary from insurer to insurer. As mentioned above, from 1 July 2025, there will also be a standard list of allowable exclusions for insurers, such as procedures that are not medically necessary, such as cosmetic surgery.

It is common for insurers to offer more than one plan for FWMI. Each plan offers different benefits, exclusions etc. Certain plans have deductible/co-insurance while others are without deductible/co-insurance etc.

subsections.label: C
subsections.title: How Is Medical Insurance For Foreign Workers Different From The Other

paragraphs.text: Insurance Requirements?10
The medical insurance requirement provides cover for a foreign worker’s medical expenses, including hospital bills arising from treatment for causes that may not be work-related.

It serves a different purpose from the other employer-paid insurance requirements, such as: (a) Work injury compensation insurance. This covers compensation, including medical expenses, for work-related injuries only. It applies to both local and foreign workers. (b) Personal accident insurance for foreign domestic workers (FDWs). This insures against accidental death and permanent disablement, not medical expenses. 10 Source: MOM website at https://www.mom.gov.sg/faq/work-permit-for-foreign-worker/how-is-medicalinsurance- for-foreign-workers-different-from-the-other-insurance-requirements

subsections.label: D
subsections.title: How can an employer guarantee that the medical insurance (MI) for foreign

paragraphs.text: worker (migrant worker or MDW) meets MOM's requirements?11

MOM has worked with insurance associations for insurers to include precontract disclosures for all MI policies. This is to be transparent for all employers on the purchased MI’s coverage. The employer should request for insurers to show whether the product offered complies with MOM’s minimum requirements before buying the MI.

MOM would consider the MI to meet the requirement if there is disclosure by insurers, or an insurance policy clause stating that the benefits of the coverage are not worse than MOM’s enhanced MI requirement. See an example of such disclosure in Appendix 8A: Foreign Worker Medical Insurance-Product Summary and Provisions.

subsections.label: E
subsections.title: Can employers buy riders or plans that offer 100% buyback of the 25% copayment

paragraphs.text: component by employers?12

Yes. They can purchase riders or additional plans to reduce the 25% co-payment component for employers for claims above $15,000. This includes riders or plans that cover the entire co-payment component by employers, such that employers do not need to co-pay anything above the first dollar coverage of $15,000, up to the annual claim limit of $60,000. 3. INSURER’S LETTER OF GUARANTEE FOR SECURITY DEPOSIT

The insurance guarantee issued by the insurance company to MOM is in the form of a Letter of Guarantee. This is in lieu of the physical cash security deposit. This “guarantee” represents a “promise” by the insurance company that it will pay for the forfeiture of the deposit on behalf of the employer if the need arises. In return, the insurance company will charge a fee to the employer for issuing this guarantee. The employer must sign a Letter of Counter Indemnity, which reiterates the employer’s duty under Common Law to indemnify the insurance company for any amount paid under this guarantee.

Strictly speaking, the offer of the Letter of Guarantee by the insurer to MOM is not an insurance benefit, since there is no real transfer of risk in this situation. The employer will have to reimburse the insurer for any money paid to MOM. Nevertheless, the employer benefits from the convenience of not having the required security deposit tied up in cash with MOM. 11 https://www.mom.gov.sg/faq/work-permit-for-foreign-worker/how-can-i-guarantee-that-the-medicalinsurance- for-foreign-worker-or-fdw-meets-moms-requirements 12 https://www.mom.gov.sg/faq/work-permit-for-foreign-worker/can-employers-buy-riders-or-plans-thatoffer- buyback-of-the-25p-copayment-component-by-ers

The employer will be discharged from the security bond liability for a worker only if he meets all of these conditions:  He has cancelled the Work Permit.  The worker has returned home.  He did not breach any of the conditions of the security bond.

The employer’s security bond may be forfeited if:  The employer or his workers violate any of the conditions of the Work Permit or the security bond.  The employer did not pay his employees’ salaries on time.  The employer fails to send the workers back when their Work Permits are expired, revoked or cancelled.  His worker goes missing. The employer must do these within 1 week of knowing that his worker is missing: (i) File a missing person police report. (ii) Cancel the Work Permit.
If the worker is not found within 1 month from the date the Work Permit was cancelled, $2,500 (half of the $5,000 security bond) will be forfeited to cover the repatriation and other related costs.
Subsequently, if the worker has left Singapore, the employer can apply for a refund of the forfeited security bond.
If a worker goes missing, half of the security bond (S$2,500) will be forfeited if the employer has made reasonable effort to locate the worker and filed a police report.

The employer will not be liable for his workers' violations (such as those relating to pregnancy) if he can prove that he has:  informed them of the Work Permit conditions they must comply with; and  reported a violation when he first become aware of it.

Should there be a breach of any of the specified conditions, the Security Bond’s amount (known as “the Obligation” in the MOM’s Security Bond Form) would be forfeited partially or in whole depending on the situation. Details are specified in the Security Bond Form available on the MOM Website at: www.mom.gov.sg
Some of the policy conditions to take note of under the Foreign Worker Medical Insurance include the following:  Termination of Cover;  Cancellation of Policy; and  Other Insurance.

The cover for the insured person will cease:  on the date of termination of the policy;  on the date of termination of employment with the insured;  upon attaining the specified age, such as 60 years old (maximum age limit as allowed by MOM); or  if the insured fails to pay the required premium for the insured person on the premium due date.

The policy may be cancelled either by the insurer or the insured by giving usually 30 days’ notice in writing. If the policy is cancelled by the insurance company, a pro-rata refund of the premium is usually given, unless there is any claim made by the insured, or the policy is being cancelled by the insurer owing to a breach of any of the policy terms or conditions by the insured.

However, if the policy is cancelled by the insured, short-term premium rate will apply, subject to a specified minimum premium payment (e.g. S$25) imposed by the insurer. No premium will be returned if there is any claim made by the insured.

The benefits payable under the policy is limited to the balance amount of medical expenses or charges not covered by any benefits payable from the Work Injury Compensation Insurance policy as required under WICA, or any other Medical Expense Insurance covering the insured person, whichever is the lesser amount.

The customarily required underwriting information is as follows:  name, address and contact details of the employer;  place and nature of business of the employer;  period of insurance; and  details of each FW to be insured, e.g. name, gender, date of birth*, passport/work permit number, country of origin. * Insurers must offer different premiums for workers aged 50 and below, and those above the age of 50, from 1 July 2025.

Note that the underwriter may require additional information, and the details of the required information may differ from insurer to insurer. An example of a Foreign Worker Medical Insurance Proposal Form is shown in Appendix 8C.

The insured or the insured person must inform the insurer within the specific number of days (usually 30 days) following the date of hospital discharge or surgery. The insurer typically provides a prescribed claim form (usually to be downloaded from its website) and require relevant supporting documents of evidence, such as original itemised hospital bills, medical receipts, invoices, inpatient hospital discharge summary and medical report, to be submitted at the expense of the insured.

As mentioned earlier, from 1 July 2025, employers will no longer need to pay for their workers’ medical treatment upfront and claim the amount from insurers later. Insurers will be required to reimburse hospitals directly. Important notes (1) The limits of cover are subject to Overall Maximum Limit as stated in Benefits Table (2) Emergency Outpatient Treatment, Outpatient Kidney Dialysis and Cancer Treatment, Special Grant and Repatriation of Mortal Remains are separate benefits and do not form part of the Overall Maximum Limit as stated in Benefits Table. (3) The Policy covers all pre-existing conditions from inception for Work Permit holders and S Pass holders (4) Information on disclosure of distribution costs, charges and expenses are available upon request. (5) The premium payable for this cover are not guaranteed and may be revised at each renewal date, at the full discretion of ABC. (6) Enrolment requirements: (a) Foreign Worker Medical Insurance Applica􀆟on Form to be completed (b) ACRA form with UEN (7) This compulsory medical insurance requirement was introduced in tandem with the withdrawal of healthcare subsidies for foreigners in order to help employers manage the potentially high medical bills of their foreign workers. This requirement is for basic coverage, but employers are still responsible for any uninsured medical expenses of their foreign workers. (8) The Policyholder and/or any Insured Person must disclose fully and truthfully to ABC all material facts and circumstances that may influence the cover provided under this Policy up to the date of issue, commencement of cover in respect of the Policyholder and any Insured Person. Any non-disclosure or misrepresentation will allow ABC to declare the Policy or cover for an Insured Person void from the start and no benefits shall be payable by ABC under the Policy or in respect of that Insured Person Descrip􀆟on of cover The amount payable will not exceed the actual costs of medically necessary services. The maximum liability of ABC shall not exceed the limits of cover less any deduc􀆟ble that is for the account of the Insured Person. The limits of cover and the deduc􀆟bles (if any) are shown in the Benefits Table and are based on a single policy year. Hospital room & board – ABC will repay the daily charges for room and board accommoda􀆟on, general nursing services and meals for each day of confinement in a hospital, subject to daily maximum limit not exceeding the en􀆟tled ward en􀆟tlement, stated in the Benefits Table, in Singapore Government or Restructured hospitals. Intensive care unit/high dependency ward/ cri􀆟cal care unit – ABC will repay charges for an intensive care unit/high dependency/cri􀆟cal care unit, provided it is cer􀆟fied medically necessary by the atending physician or surgeon. Surgery charges, theatre and anaesthe􀆟st fees – ABC will repay the fees charged by the surgeon for the opera􀆟on, theatre and anaesthe􀆟st charges. Hospital miscellaneous services & supplies – ABC will repay charges incurred during a hospital confinement for medically necessary hospital supplies and services which shall include prescribed medicines, dressing, supplies, blood transfusions, rental of appliances, implants, treatment fees, therapy fees, laboratory fees, X-rays, and oxygen and its administra􀆟on. ABC will also pay for ambulance services for transpor􀆟ng an Insured Person to a hospital, which results in an inpa􀆟ent treatment or surgery. Pre-hospitalisation / surgery specialist consultation – ABC will repay specialist consultation and referral fee including the cost of a second opinion prior to hospitalisation provided such consultation was recommended in writing by the attending physician and incurred within 90 days prior to inpatient treatment or surgery. Pre-hospitalisation/surgery diagnostic services – ABC will repay charges for diagnostic procedures and laboratory examinations prior to hospitalisation provided incurred within 90 days prior to inpatient treatment or surgery. In-hospital physician’s visit – ABC will repay fees charged by the attending physician for visiting a bed paying patient, limited to one visit per day. Post hospitalisation/surgery treatment – ABC will repay expenses incurred for follow-up treatments after hospitalisation or surgery, by the same physician, within 90 days immediately following discharge from hospital or surgery. Emergency outpatient treatment – ABC will repay expenses incurred as a result of an accidental injury for treatment as an outpatient at any registered clinic or hospital within 24 hours of the accident causing the injury. Charges for follow-up treatment by the same physician up to 31 days from the date of the accident will also be covered (if the benefit is applicable). Outpatient kidney dialysis & cancer treatment – ABC will reimburse charges for kidney dialysis at a registered dialysis centre or unit and cancer treatment (chemotherapy and radiotherapy) at an outpatient department of a hospital or registered cancer treatment centre on recommendation of a registered medical practitioner (if the benefit is applicable). Special grant – ABC will pay to the Policyholder or legal representative, the stated sum in the event of death of the Insured Person in connection with a claim resulting from: (a) An injury (b) A sickness during or after treatment for such sickness at hospital or in a day surgery ward Repatriation of mortal remains – ABC w ill p ay t he s um s tated i n t he P olicy s chedule t o t he Policyholder o r h is l egal representative in the event of death of the Insured Person for the transportation of mortal remains to the airport in Singapore or burial overseas. Personal Accident – ABC will pay a lump sum payment stated on the benefit schedule upon loss of life or permanent disablement arising solely and directly from a non-work related accidental injury on accident death, total paralysis, permanent bedridden, total and irremediable lost of sight of one eye, complete and incurable insanity. Key Provisions Age limits – subject to insurability for a Group Member whose age at entry is below 65 years old, cover will be renewable until the Insured Person reaches the age of 72. Cancellation – The Policyholder may cancel the Policy at any time by notifying us in writing and by registered post stating the effective date of cancellation of the said Policy. ABC shall charge premiums for that period of insurance coverage based on the Short Period Rates as stipulated in the Policy contract provided no claim has been paid or is payable under the Policy. Eligibility – All present full time employees shall be eligible to join on the commencement date of the Plan. If an employee is not actively at work on the date that he or she would otherwise be eligible in accordance with the above, then the eligibility date shall be deferred to the first working day of active employment. Group member – A foreign employee who is not a citizen or Permanent Resident of Singapore who seeks or is offered employment in Singapore, h olds a v alid W ork P ermit o r S P ass, a nd i s a ctively a t w ork f or a n o rganisation i ncluding i ts subdivisions as defined on the Application form. Geographical scope – Worldwide as stated in Policy Schedule is applicable to Group members who work overseas on assignment for the Policyholder only. Residence requirement – The Insured Person must be a resident of Singapore or Johor Bahru. Resident include all foreigners who hold valis S-Pass or Work Permit and must have a residential address in Singapore or Johor Bahru. The Policy will not cover you if you reside or travel for more than 90 days unless the Company has been informed and you have paid the additional premium, if any. Restoration of cover for the same disability (applicable for benefits based on a per disability basis only) In the event the Hospitalisation and surgery benefit is exhausted in respect of any in-patient hospitalisation and surgery benefits (including day surgery) only, any illness, sickness or injury arising from the same medical condition or cause including any and all complications arising therefrom or closely related thereto shall be considered as a new disability provided always: (a) the Policy remains in force; (b) the Group Member is in active service at the time of the claim and (c) the first consultation with a registered medical practitioner for the recurrence or relapse is made 14 days after the latest discharge from the hospital Non-guaranteed premium – Premiums payable for this cover are not guaranteed and may be increased on the policy renewal date, at the discretion of ABC. Reasonable & customary charges – Benefits payable are limited to reasonable and customary charges for the treatment provided and to the limits of the plan type you choose. Upgraded clause – If the cover to an Insured Person is increased while it is in force or at the time of renewaland if the Insured Person is afflicted with a disability at the time the cover was increased, the limits of cover payable in respect of such preexisting conditions shall not exceed the limits of cover prior to the date of the upgrade. This limit will remain in place for 12 months following the date on which the cover was increased. Any upgrading of Plan shall take effect at each renewal only.
