chapters.title: GROUP PERSONAL ACCIDENT AND CORPORATE TRAVEL INSURANCE
outline: What Is Group Personal Accident Insurance?
Differences From Group Term Life Insurance
learningOutcomes: know what Group Personal Accident (GPA) Insurance is all about
understand why GPA Insurance is a benefit policy
identify the key differences between GPA Insurance and Life Insurance
understand the meaning of “accident” under a GPA Insurance policy

understand the scope of cover, benefits (accidental death, total and permanent disablement, temporary total disablement, temporary partial disablement and medical expenses), policy provisions, limitations, conditions, underwriting information required, and exclusions
understand the various claim documents and procedure of GPA Insurance
know the scope of cover, benefits and exclusions of Corporate Travel Insurance

sections.title: What Is Group Personal Accident Insurance?

paragraphs.text: The purpose of a Group Personal Accident (GPA) Insurance is to provide financial protection and relief of financial burden to the insured persons against the risk of accidental death or bodily injury happening to them. In the event of any insured person’s accidental death, this policy will pay monetary sum to their beneficiaries. It also serves to mitigate the potential loss of income and to cover additional expenses incurred if any insured person is disabled and unable to attend to his usual occupation or work.

GPA Insurance plans are often purchased by corporate employers (insureds being policyholders or policy owners) to provide their employees (insured persons) with accidental death and disability benefits (capital sums insured) at affordable premiums much lower than would be required for Group Term Life Insurance policies1. They are also supplementary insurance contracts if the employers wish to enhance their employee benefits covered under the Group Term Life Insurance policies. GPA Insurance can also be extended to cover the insured employee’s spouse and children (insured persons) at lower benefit limits.

GPA Insurance is also popular with private education institutions. They usually purchase GPA Insurance to cover their students. In addition, associations and social clubs with sizable membership also have GPA Insurance plans for their members to enrol in, to take advantage of lower premium rate for group participation than if they were to individually insure on their own.

Generally, a GPA Insurance policy is not a contract of indemnity2. Under a contract of indemnity, the amount recoverable is measured by the extent of the insured’s financial loss, and for which the contract indemnifies or reimburses the insured for the losses that the insured has incurred. On the other hand, a GPA Insurance contract is generally considered a benefit policy2 i.e. a contract to pay a sum of money in the event of a certain contingency, irrespective of whether or not the insured sustains a financial loss. Unlike a contract of indemnity, there is no need to prove the quantum of loss. For example, if an insured person who is a sales manager loses his little toe resulting from an accident, the GPA Insurance policy will pay the insured amount as specified in the group policy. It does not matter that the loss of his little toe has not caused any financial loss, as he can still carry on with his usual work as a sales manager. 1 Discussions on the Group Term Life Insurance policies are in the “Life Insurance and Investment-Linked Policies” Study Guide, published by the Singapore College of Insurance Limited. 2 Discussions on the concepts of “contract of indemnity” and “benefit policy” are in the “Basic Insurance Concepts & Principles” Study Guide, published by the Singapore College of Insurance Limited.

sections.title: Differences From Group Term Life Insurance

paragraphs.text: Both Group Term Life and GPA Insurance policies provide death and disability benefits. However, there are differences between the two classes of insurance.

Firstly, GPA Insurance covers death and disability by accident, but does not cover that arising from natural causes, such as disease, illness and sickness. Life Insurance covers both causes. Thus, GPA Insurance does not constitute a policy of Life Insurance in accordance with the Insurance Act 1966. As such, an insurer does not necessarily need to hold a Life Insurance licence to transact this kind of business. Indeed, numerous life and general insurers offer GPA Insurance policies in the insurance market.

Secondly, with the exception of Term Life, Life Insurance policies offer savings or investment benefits on top of death benefits. In addition, an Endowment Insurance policy pays the sum assured (i.e. living proceeds) if the insured survives to the maturity date of this policy. The amount payable at maturity frequently includes bonuses reflecting the investment income. In contrast, when an employer purchases a GPA Insurance policy, the premiums paid are mainly for the cost of insurance protection. Thus, a GPA Insurance policy carries no savings or investment benefits, nor any bonuses reflecting the investment income. In this respect, Term Life Insurance policies are similar to GPA policies i.e. they also do not offer any savings or investment benefits, nor any bonuses reflecting the investment income.

There is no single definition of “accident” universally used by insurers. All the definitions require the event leading to conditions for which benefits may be claimed under a GPA Insurance policy to be “accidental” in nature, although this term itself is often undefined and insurers rely on court interpretations as precedents to decide whether or not an event was accidental. Some insurers define “accidental” as “unintended” and / or “unexpected” but it is not clear whether these definitions improve the precision of the definition.

With a view to reduce ambiguity, some insurers add on the conditions of the event requiring it to be “violent, accidental, external and visible” for it to qualify as an “accident” for the purpose of the policy. This definition is commonly found in the more traditional policy wording. However, these additional conditions lead to other ambiguities, such as whether insect’s bite, drowning, or inhalation of toxic gas is deemed as an accidental event. To clarify its position, the insurer has to disclose or state categorically in its policy document or product brochure, whether or not its policy covers any such event.

The “violent, accidental, external and visible” condition does make it clear that infection, which is largely unintended, does not constitute as an accident for the purpose of the policy. Some insurers avoid this potential area of dispute by specifically excluding illness and infection from coverage.

Some Group PA insurance policies include, in addition to a definition of “accident”, a definition of “injury”. However, many of these “definitions” do not actually define the word “injury”, but merely require the injury to be sustained as a result of an accident.

More importantly, the insurance policy requires that the insured benefits are payable only if death or disability is caused by an injury. There may be occasions where the insured employee (insured person) is involved in an accident but dies or is disabled during the accident owing to a cause which is not an injury. An example is such that an insured person who was involved in a traffic accident and died of a heart attack. The requirement that the insured person must have died of an injury makes it clear that no benefit is payable under such a situation.

The basic GPA Insurance policy provides for payment of benefits upon the insured person’s death or total and permanent disability (TPD) which results from injuries sustained by the insured person in an accident, occurring during the period of insurance as specified in the group policy.

It is important to note here that the insured event is defined in relation to an accident which occurs while the insured person’s insurance is in force. Note further that there is usually a time limit attached to it. Insurers often stipulate that the death or TPD has to occur within a certain time frame (e.g. 12 months) from the date of the accident giving rise to the claim. It may be possible that the policyholder terminates or decides not to renew the GPA Insurance policy, and the insured person only dies or becomes disabled after the group policy has become invalid. If this happens, the insurer remains liable to pay the benefit arising should the loss (death or PTD) occur within 12 months from the date of the accident covered by the group policy.

The death and TPD benefits are usually paid in lump sums or capital sums. TPD benefits can be paid for a variety of disabilities, most of which involve physical severance or total and permanent loss of use of limbs, fingers, toes, eyesight, hearing or speech. The benefit payable for any particular disability is expressed as a percentage of the capital sum insured. For major disabilities, like loss of two arms or legs, the benefit payable may be higher than the capital sum insured, e.g. 150% of the capital sum insured.

Commonly, death and TPD benefits are reflected under a “Table of Compensation”, especially if the group policy covers any other permanent disability. Here is an example of the benefits offered under a “Table of Compensation” (also known as the “Schedule of Compensation”): Table 9.1: Example Of A Table Of Compensation Description Percentage Of Capital Sum Insured 1. Death 100% 2. Loss of two or more limbs 150% 3. Loss of one limb by amputation at or above wrist or ankle 125% 4. Total and irrecoverable loss of all sight in two eyes 150% 5. Total and irrecoverable loss of all sight in one eye 100% 6. Total paralysis 150% 7. Injuries resulting in insured being permanently bedridden 150% 8. Total and Permanent Disability preventing the insured from engaging in any gainful occupation or employment for wage or profit or from giving attention to any business whatsoever 150% 9. Eye - loss of sight in one eye, except perception of light - loss of lens in one eye 50% 50% 10. Loss of four fingers and thumb of one hand 50% 11. Loss of four fingers 40% 12. Loss of speech 50% 13. Loss of hearing - both ears - one ear 75% 15% 14. Others – See details in the sample policy in Appendix 10A.

Where the benefit paid is less than the full capital sum insured, the insurance for the insured person concerned may continue, but the sum insured during the remaining period of insurance of the group policy will equal to the original capital sum insured reduced by the amount of benefit paid.

subsections.label: B
subsections.title: Temporary Total Or Temporary Partial Disability

paragraphs.text: The policyholder may choose to insure temporary disability benefits, in which case, the insured person need not suffer severe injury, before such a benefit becomes payable. If, as a result of the accident, the insured person is totally unable to perform all normal activities, or is able to perform some, but not all of his usual activities, the GPA Insurance policy pays temporary total or temporary partial disability benefits as the case may be.

The temporary disability benefit is usually a weekly benefit expressed as a percentage, such as 1% of the insured person’s capital sum insured, subject to a maximum of an absolute monetary amount, or a percentage (e.g. 75%) of the insured person’s last drawn weekly income, if also expressed as a weekly benefit. The percentage for temporary partial disability is obviously smaller than that for temporary total disability. Very often, the temporary partial disability benefit is set at 50% of the temporary total disability benefit. For example, temporary total disability benefit is S$500 per week, while temporary partial disability benefit is S$250 per week. Such benefits are usually payable up to 52 or 104 weeks, i.e. for one or two years.

It is important to relate the temporary disability benefits to the weekly income of the insured person. For this reason, most insurers will not provide temporary disability benefits cover to other than persons who are in gainful employment. If the weekly benefits are higher than the person’s income, or are even a substantial percentage of it, these benefits would tend to encourage the insured person to delay his return to full-time work. In the case of a working person, if the employer continues to pay the employee his salary and retains these benefits as compensation for loss of the employee’s contribution to the organisation, it too has less incentive to encourage the employee to return to work. Such a situation can lead to higher claim costs and, hence, to higher premiums.

As it is possible for an injury to first result in a temporary disability, but subsequently, develop into a total and permanent disability, a GPA Insurance policy will pay the TPD benefit amount only after deducting the sum of temporary disability benefits already paid. If this condition is not in place, it is possible for the employer (policyholder) to increase the amount of benefits that it receives, by claiming temporary disability benefits first and subsequently claiming TPD benefits, even if the insured employee is actually permanently disabled immediately as a result of the accident.

The premium charged for temporary disability benefits is computed on the assumption that the disability is not permanent and that the insured employee is able to return to work at some time after the accident.

The policyholder may also choose to insure the insured persons against any medical expenses incurred for the treatment of injuries resulting from accidents. This is notwithstanding that the insured persons may be insured under separate Individual Hospital & Surgical (IHS) or Group Hospital & Surgical Insurance (GHS) policies3. The reimbursement benefit covers all forms of medical and surgical treatment, including that provided in an outpatient clinical setting, which is not normally covered by a typical IHS or GHS Insurance policy.
While GPA is not a contract of indemnity, the reimbursement of medical expenses is subject to indemnity.

There can be an overlap in coverage. This is especially so of a person who may have a personal IHS Insurance policy and to find that he is also covered by his employer under his employer’s GHS Insurance policy. As the cost of the reimbursement benefit is relatively small, some employers may not mind paying the additional premium. Also, to keep the benefit payment conditions simple, the reimbursement of hospitalisation and surgical expenses is not subject to sub-limits on room and board charges, surgery fees, etc. As such, benefits may still be payable if, following an accident, the employee decides to be admitted to a ward class, where the charges are likely to exceed his entitlement under his employer’s GHS Insurance policy.
The reimbursement amount per accident is subject to a maximum limit (e.g. S$5,000 per accident), which is also often expressed as a fraction of the sum insured.

To differentiate their products from their competitors, some insurers offer additional benefits, such as:
▪ daily hospital income benefits, where a fixed amount of cash benefit is payable for each day that the insured person is confined in a hospital (some insurers even extend this benefit to hospitalisation for causes other than accidents); and
▪ reimbursement of expenses incurred for treatment of injuries provided by alternative therapists, like Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) practitioners, subject to a maximum amount which is relatively low. 3 Discussions on Hospital & Surgical Insurance policies are in the “Health Insurance” Study Guide, published by the Singapore College of Insurance Limited.

Extended benefits may include: (a) strike, riot, civil commotion and terrorism; (b) hijack, kidnap, unprovoked assault and murder; (c) drowning and suffocation by gas, poisonous fumes or smoke; (d) exposure (to the natural element immediately following an accident); and (e) disappearance (usually relating to an accident of an aircraft or vessel in which the insured person is travelling as a fare-paying passenger, and his body cannot be found after a certain period of time, e.g. one year).

subsections.label: E
subsections.title: Application Of The Limits On Benefits

paragraphs.text: The total of all benefits payable for death and permanent disability, regardless of whether or not the death and permanent disabilities all result from the same accident, is usually limited to the highest benefit payable for any one disability. For example, if 50% of the insured person’s capital sum insured has been paid when that insured person loses all four fingers and a thumb of one hand resulting from an accident and, subsequently, loses two legs in another accident, the maximum benefit payable will be 100% of the insured person’s capital sum insured, even though the benefit payable for loss of two limbs may be 150% of the insured person’s capital sum insured according to the Table of Compensation.

Very often, the insurance on the insured person ceases immediately upon the payment of such a benefit which brings the total of such benefits paid to date at the time equal or not exceeding the insured person’s capital sum insured.

The weekly benefits payable for temporary total and partial disability is usually limited to 52 or 104 weeks. This duration limit applies to both total and partial disability benefits together and not separately. For example, if the contractual limit is 104 weeks and the insurance has paid 60 weeks of total disability benefits, and the insured person then returns to work, but in such a limited capacity, the person shall remain entitled to temporary partial disability benefit payment, but in this case, the payment of such a benefit will be limited only to 44 weeks (104 less 60 weeks of temporary total disability). This duration limit usually applies on a per accident basis. If the insured person has claimed, say, six weeks of temporary disability benefits, and the insured person returns to full employment, but then is disabled owing to another accident, the employer will be entitled to another 104 weeks of temporary disability benefits.
The maximum limit on any medical expense reimbursement benefit also usually applies on a per accident basis.

Owing to the possibility of the policyholders and the insured persons being able to take a broader definition of the intended insured benefits terms, insurers have found it necessary to provide specific definitions to these, so as to avoid any ambiguity which can result in a dispute when settling a claim.
These are the most critical terms in a GPA Insurance policy as they define the insured event. The definition and interpretation of these terms are covered above.

Permanent disability is defined by the various disabilities which are listed in the schedule of permanent disability compensation. The concept of permanence is regarded as self-explanatory and is very seldom defined (but see the definition of “loss of use” in the later section).

TPD is often one of the disabilities included in the Table of Compensation for permanent disability. This benefit is payable only in the event that the insured person is as a result of an accident, totally unable to engaged in any gainful occupation or employment for wages or profit or from giving attention to any business whatsoever. The insured person will also be able to claim compensation if for example, he has suffered any loss of limb or eyesight. The definition of TPD tends to follow that of the insurer’s Group Term Life Insurance policy. However, the TPD must be the result of solely accidental bodily injury. Sometimes, the insurer may delete the requirement of a waiting period, as the TPD following an accident tends to be easier to certify than a TPD which is a consequence of illness.

At one time, benefits were payable only if the insured person lost an arm or a leg by actual physical severance or amputation. However, almost all GPA Insurance policies these days pay benefits if the attending physician is able to certify that the insured person has lost the use of the limb totally and permanently. The concept of “total” and “permanent” can be difficult to define. Some insurers follow the practice of assessing the degree of loss of use adopted by insurers and tribunals in determining the compensatory amounts under the Work Injury Compensation legislation. The permanent loss requirement is often reinforced further by the addition of the word “irrecoverable” to the definition.

These disabilities must be defined carefully. Otherwise, more benefits than anticipated may become payable. To reduce the possibility of ambiguity, it is best to define Total Disability as the total inability to perform ANY occupation. However, it is commonly defined as the total inability to perform OWN occupation.

Unfortunately, many insured employers and employees tend to take medical leave certificates, as evidence of the total disability, when physicians often prescribe such leave for recuperative and rehabilitative purposes. So, an insured employee may actually be able to return to work and perform light duties, but does not do so, as to recover more quickly from his injuries.
By definition, the insured person is then entitled to only Partial Disability benefits, but will claim for Total Disability benefits instead, since they are higher amounts.

The presence of Partial Disability benefits encourages insured employees to return to work, even on a part-time basis, and reduces the claim cost. The definition of Partial Disability usually requires that the insured person is unable to perform significant duties of his own occupation.

These terms may be used especially in specifying the conditions required for the disability and medical expense reimbursement benefits to be payable (e.g. certification of disability by a physician; expenses incurred in a hospital). For consistency purposes, most insurers adopt the same definitions which they use in their Group Hospital & Surgical Insurance policies.
Some insurers define illness or sickness for the purpose of excluding death, disability and incurred medical expenses not resulting from an accident.

For clarity purposes, some insurers specifically cover pre-existing conditions within commercial policies whereas other insurers explicitly exclude from coverage any disability which has already existed prior to the effective date of insurance of the insured person concerned. This may be important if, for example, the insured person who already has loss of sight in one eye is unfortunate to lose the sight in the other in an accident, after he is insured. The policy terms and conditions must be clear as to whether or not the policyholder is entitled to compensation for loss of sight in one eye only, or for the total loss of sight.

Among other standard provisions and conditions applicable, they may specifically include such as those described below: (a) Geographical Limits The coverage is usually 24 hours a day, worldwide, unless otherwise endorsed or amended in the group policy. (b) Termination Of Policy The insured and the insurer may terminate the policy before its anniversary date by giving written notice within a certain period of time (usually 14 to 30 days). However, if the insured cancels the policy before its renewal date, the insurer will charge premiums based on short period rates. (c) Contracts (Rights Of Third Parties) Act 2001 Any person who is not a party to this policy contract will have no right or entitlement under the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 2001 to enforce any of its terms.
Note: There is no free-look period for a GPA Insurance policy.

An example of category of employees/occupation with basis of coverage is as follows: Category of Employees / Occupation Basis of Coverage (i) Senior Management (Director, General Manager, Senior Manager) 100,000 (ii) Manager & Executive 50,000 (iii) All Others 25,000 Alternatively, the basis of coverage can be based on a number of times the basic monthly salary for all employees as shown in the following example: (i) All Employees 24 x Basic Monthly Salary

In addition, under regulatory and industry guidelines, any request for a quotation for certain group insurance (including GPA Insurance) must be accompanied by a completed Group Insurance Fact-Finding (GIFF) Form.

It is important for an insurance agent/representative to obtain, in the case of an employer arranged programme, the relevant information on a corporate client’s existing employee benefit plans, details of employees with severe medical conditions and/or high occupational risks, policy claims experience (if any), and put this information through proper needs analysis, before making appropriate recommendations.
The GIFF Form serves as a means for the collection of certain basic underwriting information, needs analysis and documentation of any recommendations made.

Exclusions under a GPA Insurance policy may be classified under several categories as described below: (a) Injuries which are not deemed to be caused by accidents: These include self-inflicted injury, suicide or attempted suicide, while sane or insane. (b) Injuries resulting from accidents but which arise out of actions which are deemed to be against public policy: These include committing or attempting to commit any criminal or unlawful act, and driving or operating any mechanical device while under the influence of alcohol or drugs. (c) Injuries sustained while participating in hazardous activities: The hazardous activities which are commonly excluded from GPA Insurance policies are the more dangerous ones, such as sky diving, mountaineering, driving or riding in any kind of race, and aerial activity or private flying other than as a fare-paying passenger of a commercial aircraft, including chartered or unscheduled aircraft or helicopter. Hazardous occupations are managed by underwriting. Injuries sustained while performing duties as a full-time police and armed forces personnel are usually excluded. (d) Catastrophic events: While most insurers do not exclude injuries sustained in natural disasters, most still exclude injuries resulting from war, warlike operation, other military conflicts and nuclear risk. (e) Pre-existing condition or disability: Some insurers feel that it is necessary to ensure that they do not pay benefits for losses or disabilities sustained before the employee is insured. Others rely on other terms and conditions of the group policy, like the requirement that the accident occurs while the employee is insured, and that the loss or disability results solely from the injuries sustained in such an accident, so as to exclude payment of benefits for any pre-existing physical defect or infirmity. (f) Illnesses: Again, some insurers exclude losses resulting from illnesses and diseases for clarity purposes. (g) Consequences of accident: These exclusions are rare as the circumstances under which they have to be invoked are also very rare. As an example, an insured person meets with an accident which results in a head injury. He subsequently leaves employment, as the injury causes him to have frequent blackouts. After he has left employment, whereby he is no longer insured, he suffers a blackout, falls and hurts himself again. It may be argued that the latest injury is the result of his earlier accident. Most insurers rely on the insurance clause stating that the injury must result directly from the accident covered by the group policy while it is in force. (h) Accidents which occur before commencement or after termination of insurance: Some insurers include these exclusions to avoid any ambiguity.
Note that the exclusions vary from insurer to insurer. Refer to the sample policy document in Appendix 9A for details.
Notice of claim must be given to the insurer within a certain reasonable period of time, usually 30 days after the happening of an accident, although market practices may vary.

The insured or insured person (or claimant), at own expense, is required to furnish relevant supporting documents, information and evidence in the form as required by the insurer. In practice, the insurer issues a printed claim form (see Appendix 9B) so drafted, as to elicit the necessary required information. This must be completed by the insured and insured person (or the claimant in the event of accidental death of the insured person), as well as the attending physician, and returned to the insurer.

If the insurer decides to arrange a medical examination (at its own expense), it enables the insurer to be satisfied that:
▪ the alleged accident has caused the injuries; and
▪ such injuries are preventing the insured person from working; or
▪ the injury is as described and is causing disability.
The insurer has the right to have a post-mortem examination done (if not forbidden by law) which is useful when the cause of death is questionable.

The above-mentioned conditions are those precedent to liability, meaning that these must be complied with if there is to be a valid claim. Obviously, the insurer is not liable for any claim which is fraudulent.

Employees are one of a company’s most valuable assets and business travel is an essential part of almost every company. It is not necessary to buy an individual travel insurance policy every time the employee travels abroad. A company can buy a single policy for all its employees via a Corporate Travel Insurance policy.

Employees’ travel can be compromised by trip cancellations, lost baggage and travel documents, personal accidents, even a hospital stay in a foreign land. What if the employee has to miss a meeting due to an illness? If the employee needs to be replaced for such a meeting overseas, a Corporate Travel Insurance policy can provide protection against such risks.

A Corporate Travel plan covers any accident, sickness, loss and even medical attention in an emergency that may occur while travelling. It allows a company’s employees to travel with complete peace of mind.

The key features of a Corporate Travel Insurance Policy include: (i) Personal deviation and/or any trip for personal purposes whether before, during or immediately after the business trip are automatically covered. (ii) Optional leisure travel coverage. (iii) Complimentary leisure travel extension for senior management (director and above). (iv) Cover for business trip up to maximum of 182 days (and no sub-limit applicable for personal deviation). (v) Covers valid for multiple trips. (vi) Home Leave Extension of up to 30 days for expatriates. (vii) Automatic Extension of coverage period up to 30 days due to Hospitalisation and/ or Quarantine. (viii) Option to choose different insurance plans for employees and different coverage limits. a) Minimum administrative burdens and cost saving to you. b) 24-hour Worldwide Medical & Emergency Assistance through multilingual facilities. c) 24-hour Travel Information.
The period of coverage of a Corporate Travel Insurance policy is on an annual basis. During this year, all single overseas trips (not exceeding 182 days) made by each and any employee is covered.

This is a unique benefit for Business or Corporate Travel Insurance whereby the insurer will pay when the Insured sends a substitute employee to complete another employee’s work duties if during the Overseas Trip: (i) he suffers Injury or Sickness and a Medical Practitioner has certified in writing that he is unfit to continue the Trip; or (ii) he encounters the unexpected death, sudden Serious Injury or Serious Sickness of a Special Person.

Insurers will indemnify the Insured in respect of all reasonable transportation costs necessarily incurred for sending a substitute employee to complete another employee’s work duties. Such costs shall be limited to economy airfare, travel and accommodation expenses for transportation of the substitute employee.

Most of the exclusions are a similar to that found in an individual travel insurance policy. There are numerous general exclusions in such policies. Examples include pre-existing conditions, intentional self-injury, suicide, use of drugs not prescribed by a doctor, sexually transmitted diseases, pregnancy, travel to restricted countries, war risks, participation in extreme sports/activities etc.

subsections.label: E
subsections.title: COVID-19 Travel Insurance for Outbound Travellers

paragraphs.text: The following table is a brief summary of what certain insurers are covering for Covid-19 as at Jan 2022: Covid-19 Coverage (Outbound) Description Overseas Medical Expenses due to Covid-19 Pays the medical expenses that the insured has incurred overseas up to 90 days of his trip as a result of him being diagnosed with covid- 19 whilst during his trip. Trip Cancellation or Postponement If the insured has to cancel or postpone his trip as a result of himself, his family or sole travelling companion being diagnosed with Covid-19. Trip Curtailment or Rearrangement If the insured has to cut short his trip to return to Singapore or rearrange his itinerary as a result of himself, his family or his sole travelling companion being diagnosed with Covid-19. Emergency Medical Evacuation and/or Repatriation If the insured has to be evacuated or repatriated back to Singapore as a result of him being diagnosed with Covid-19. Overseas Hospitalisation Allowance Pays the insured a daily allowance if the insured is hospitalized whilst during his trip as a result of being diagnosed with Covid-19. Overseas Quarantine Allowance* Pays the insured a daily allowance if he is quarantined overseas as a result of being diagnosed with Covid-19. *The insured can claim for either Overseas Hospitalisation Allowance or Overseas Quarantine Allowance, but not both.

It would be noted from the above that certain insurers exclude one-way trips from their coverage of Covid-19. However, there are other insurers who cover one-way trips but under limited circumstances. For these insurers, new travel insurance policies purchased from say, June 2021, would cover certain situations pertaining to COVID-19, depending on whether the insured is purchasing a return trip or a one-way trip. While the coverage is similar to that outlined above, the only benefit available for one-way trips is Travel Cancellation and Postponement.
As there are variations in cover offered by each insurer, it is best to check and study the policy wording and coverage offered by different insurers in this respect.
The general conditions for this type of policy are similar to those found in most Travel Insurance policies, and some insurers might add a 14-day waiting period from the date of purchase.

The insurer would require a proof of a positive COVID-19 test for claims under this specific policy on top of the standard related documents for claims under a standard Travel policy. Appendix 9A ABC INSURANCE COMPANY (SINGAPORE) LIMITED (Registration No: 2009-12345-A) 21 Any Street, ABC Centre, Singapore 654321 Tel: (65) 6789 8181 Fax: (65) 6789 8282 E-mail: contactus@abcinco.com.sg Website: www.abcinco.com.sg GROUP PERSONAL ACCIDENT INSURANCE POLICY INSURING AGREEMENT In consideration of the payment of a premium stated in the Declarations, ABC Insurance Company (Singapore) Limited (herein called “the Company”) agrees with the Policyholder to insure eligible Insured Persons to the extent hereto provided and subject to the exclusions and all other limitations and provisions of this Policy. THIS POLICY WILL NOT BE VALID UNLESS THE DECLARATIONS PAGE SIGNED BY AN AUTHORISED REPRESENTATIVE OF ABC INSURANCE COMPANY (SINGAPORE) LIMITED ARE ATTACHED HERETO. CERTAIN WORDS AND PHRASES THAT APPEAR IN BOLD PRINT HAVE SPECIAL MEANINGS. DEFINITIONS APPEAR IN PART I: DEFINITIONS OF THIS POLICY. PLEASE READ THIS POLICY CAREFULLY. PART I: DEFINITIONS Accident or Accidental means a sudden, unforeseen and unexpected event happening by chance that results in the Insured Person suffering death, disablement or bodily injury. Accidental Bodily Injury means bodily injury sustained by the Insured Person during the Policy period caused solely and directly by Accidental means (excluding any sickness or medical disorder) and within twelve calendar months from the date of the Accident results in the Insured Person’s death or disablement. Benefit / Benefits means the respective capital sum insured, as stated in the Policy Declarations, payable by the Company under the terms and conditions of this Policy in respect of each event or loss covered by this Policy. Benefit Amount means the Benefit Amount shown in the Declarations against the relevant category of loss. Company means ABC Insurance Company (Singapore) Limited. Commencement of Insurance means the Policy period stated in the declarations from which the insurance plan under this Policy is operative. Declarations mean the declarations which is incorporated in and forms part of this Policy. Effective Date means the date from which the insurance coverage under this Policy in respect of any Insured Person becomes effective. Insured Person means person(s) named or described in the Declarations, or any amendment or endorsement to this Policy, to be the Insured Person and who is aged between sixteen (16) and seventy (70) years on the first day of the Policy period. Permanent as used in respect to disablement means disability that lasts more than three hundred and sixty-five (365) days following which there is no hope of improvement. Permanent Total Disablement means disablement lasting at least three hundred and sixty-five (365) consecutive days and which will, in all probability, entirely prevent the Insured Person from engaging in any occupation for which he is capable by reason of education, training or experience (or if he has no occupation from engaging in gainful employment) for the remainder of his life, and which is beyond hope of improvement. Policyholder means the employer identified as the Named Insured on the Policy Declarations. Policy means this agreement, any endorsements, attachments or amendments hereto signed by the Company, which together constitute the entire Policy between the parties. Reference Benefit Amount means the Benefits stated in the Declarations, (in the event that the Benefits are at any time reduced below the Benefits stated in the Declarations) the Benefits stated on the Policy schedule less the amount of such reduction in Benefits or (in the event that the Benefits are increased above the Benefits stated in Declarations prior to the commencement of the immediately preceding Policy period) the sum of the Benefits stated on the Declarations and the amount of such increase in Benefits. For the avoidance of doubt, in the event that the Benefits are increased above the Benefits stated in the Declarations during the immediately preceding Policy period or at the time of renewal, the Reference Benefit Amount shall mean the Benefits stated in Declarations. PART II: COMMENCEMENT AND TERMINATION OF COVERAGE 1. Termination of Coverage The insurance coverage in respect of an Insured Person will terminate on the occurrence of the following event, whichever is the earliest of: a. When this Policy is terminated in accordance with Part VI, Clause 5 of this Policy. b. End of the Policy period during which the Insured Person attains age 70. c. Date on which the Insured Person ceases to be an employee of the Insured. SPECIMEN 2. Automatic Inclusion and Deletion This Policy shall automatically cover all present and new Insured Persons upon their date of employment subject to notification by the Insured to the Company at year end adjustment. Coverage ceases upon the date of termination of employment. The Insured shall provide written proof of employment to the Company in the event of a claim. PART III: BENEFITS 1. Coverage If whilst this Policy is in force, an Insured Person sustains Accidental Bodily Injury and suffers from any of the loss events described in the table of benefits, the Company shall, upon receipt and approval of proof, pay up to Benefit Amount stated in the Declarations. 2. Extended Benefits Assault, Hijack and Murder If whilst this Policy is in force, in the event the Insured Person suffers death or Permanent Total Disablement as a result of being a victim of assault, hijack or murder, the relevant Benefit Amount stated in the table of benefits shall be payable, provided that such injury does not arise out of the Insured Person’s own participation or provocation of any such act. Disappearance If whilst this Policy is in force, in the event the Insured Person disappears and after 365 days, it is reasonable to believe that such Insured Person has died as a result of Accidental Bodily Injury, the death Benefit Amount stated in the table of benefits shall be payable subject to a signed undertaking by the beneficiary that if the Insured Person is subsequently found alive, such death Benefit Amount shall be refunded to the Company. Drowning and Suffocation by Gas, Poisonous Fumes or Smoke If whilst this Policy is in force, in the event the Insured Person suffers death or Permanent Total Disablement as a result of drowning or suffocation by gas, poisonous fumes or smoke, the relevant Benefit Amount stated in the table of benefits shall be payable, provided that such injury does not arise out of the Insured Person’s wilful and intentional act. Exposure If whilst this Policy is in force, in the event the Insured Person is unavoidably exposed to the elements due to an Accident and, as a result of such exposure, suffers death, the death Benefit Amount stated in the table of benefits shall be payable. Strike, Riot, Civil Commotion and Terrorism If whilst this Policy is in force, in the event the Insured Person suffers death or Permanent Total Disablement as a result of strike, riot, civil commotion or terrorism, the relevant Benefit Amount stated in the table of benefits shall be payable, provided that such injury does not arise out of the Insured Person’s own participation or provocation of any such act or if such act could reasonably have been avoided by the Insured Person. 3. Automatic Increase in Benefit The Benefits (A) and (B) in the table of benefits shall increase by five percent (5%) of the Reference Benefit Amount upon each annual renewal of this Policy provided that: a. no claim had been made under this Policy in the previous Policy period; b. this Policy was not terminated or cancelled at any time in the previous Policy period; c. this Policy was renewed by the Policyholder before the end of the previous Policy period or as provided in the premium warranty clause; d. the total increase in the Benefit shall not exceed twenty-five percent (25%) of the Reference Benefit Amount; and e. the total automatic increase in Benefit in respect of any one Insured Person shall not exceed S$500,000. 4. Mobility Extension If whilst this Policy is in force, an Insured Person sustains Accidental Bodily Injury which directly and independently of all other causes results in Permanent Total Disablement of such a nature that such Insured Person need and can operate: a. a self-powered, climbing wheelchair; and/or b. his/her motor vehicle with the controls suitably adjusted; and/or c. a lift, necessary ramps, railings and holds to usual place of residence the Company agrees to reimburse up to one thousand dollars (S$1,000) or 95% of the costs of such equipment and the installation thereof whichever is lesser. 5. Ambulance Costs If whilst this Policy is in force, an Insured Person sustains Accidental Bodily Injury which necessitates the use of an ambulance, the Company agrees to reimburse the actual ground ambulance costs incurred by the Insured Person up to five hundred dollars (S$500), for transportation to the hospital. 6. Funeral Expenses If whilst this Policy is in force, the Company agrees to reimburse up to two thousand dollars (S$2,000) for costs of funeral expenses following Accidental death due to Accidental Bodily Injury. PART IV: EXCLUSIONS The Company shall not be liable to pay for any loss directly or indirectly consequent upon the following causes: 1. War, declared or undeclared, revolution or warlike operations; 2. Insured Person engaging in naval, military or airforce service or operations except peacetime reservist training under Section 14 of the Enlistment Act, Cap.93 of the Republic of Singapore; 3. Self-inflicted injury, suicide or any attempts thereat, suicide pacts or agreement while sane or insane; 4. Insured Person engaging in racing on wheels. SPECIMEN PART V: CLAIMS PROCEDURE 1. Notice of Claim Any occurrence or loss, which may give rise to a claim under this Policy, should be reported to the Company in writing within thirty (30) days after the occurrence or loss. Failure to furnish notice within the time provided in the Policy shall not invalidate any claim if it shall be shown not to have been reasonably possible to furnish such notice and that such notice was furnished as soon as was reasonably possible. Written Proof of Loss must be given to the Company as soon as possible and, in any event, within 30 days of Notice of Claim together with original copies of all relevant documentation. The Insured Person or the Policyholder shall, at their own expense, furnish the Company such certificates, information and evidence as the Company may from time to time require in the form prescribed by the Company. The Company shall have the right but not the obligation, at its own expense, to require the Insured Person to submit to medical examinations as often as may be necessary during the pendency of a claim hereunder and to have an autopsy performed in case of death, unless prohibited by law. 2. Payment of Claim The Company shall treat the Insured, the employer, as the absolute legal and beneficial owner of this Policy and shall not be bound to recognise any equitable or other claim to or interest in this Policy. Any amount payable under this Policy shall be paid by cheque to the order of the Insured and such payment shall effectively discharge the Company from any further liability in respect thereof. 3. Fraudulent Claims If any claim under this Policy shall be fraudulent in any respect, or if any fraudulent means or devices shall be used by the Insured, the Insured Person, or anyone acting on their behalf to obtain benefits under this Policy, the Company shall be under no liability in respect of such claim. PART VI: OTHER POLICY PROVISIONS 1. Geographical Limits The coverage under this Policy is 24 hours a day, worldwide unless otherwise endorsed or amended. 2. Compliance with Policy Provisions Failure to comply with any of the provisions contained in this Policy shall invalidate all claims hereunder. 3. Conditions Precedent to the Validity of Policy The validity of this Policy is subject to the condition precedent that: a. for the risk insured, the named insured has never had any insurance terminated in the last twelve (12) months due solely or in part to a breach of any premium payment condition; or b. if the named insured has declared that it has breached any premium payment condition in respect of a previous policy taken up with another insurer in the last twelve (12) months: i. the named insured has fully paid all outstanding premium for time on risk calculated by the previous insurer based on the customary short period rate in respect of the previous policy; and ii. a copy of the written confirmation from the previous insurer to this effect is first provided by the named insured to the Company before cover incepts. 4. Premium Payment Warranty a. Notwithstanding anything herein contained but subject to clause b hereof, it is hereby agreed and declared that if the period of insurance is 60 days or more, any premium due must be paid and actually received in full by the Company (or the intermediary through whom this Policy was effected) within 60 days of the inception date of the coverage under the Policy, Renewal Certificate or Cover Note. b. In the event that any premium due is not paid and actually received in full by the Company (or the intermediary through whom this Policy was effected) within the 60-day period referred to above, then:- i. the cover under the Policy, Renewal Certificate or Cover Note is automatically terminated immediately after the expiry of the said 60-day period; ii. the automatic termination of the cover shall be without prejudice to any liability incurred within the said 60-day period; and iii. the Company shall be entitled to a pro-rata time on risk premium subject to a minimum of S$25.00. c. If the period of insurance is less than 60 days, any premium due must be paid and actually received in full by the Company (or the intermediary through whom this Policy was effected) within the period of insurance. 5. Termination of Policy The Insured or the Company may terminate this Policy as of any Policy anniversary by giving written notice of at least 30 days prior to such Policy anniversary. The Policy shall be deemed as cancelled upon expiry of 30 days from the date of receipt of such written notice. Upon earlier termination of the Policy by the Insured prior to the renewal date, the Company will charge premiums based on the following short period rates: SPECIMEN Period Covered Short Period Rates Not exceeding: 1 week 1 month 1 month 3 months 2 months 4 months 3 months 6 months 4 months 7 months 6 months 9 months 8 months 10 months Above 8 months Full Annual Premium 6. Legal Action No legal action may be brought to recover on the Policy until sixty (60) days after the Company has been given written proof of loss. No such action may be brought after two (2) years from the date of loss. 7. Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 2001 A person who is not a party of this policy contract shall have no right under the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 2001 to enforce any of its terms. 8. Jurisdiction The Company will in all competent judicial proceedings in connection with matters arising out of this insurance acknowledge the jurisdiction of the Courts in Singapore only. 9. Operation of Law This Policy shall be construed according to and governed by the laws of the Republic of Singapore. ACCIDENTAL DEATH & PERMANENT DISABLEMENT If whilst this Policy is in force, the Insured Person sustains Accidental Bodily Injury which directly and independently of all other causes results in death or Permanent Disablement within 365 days of the date of loss, the Company will pay the Insured Person the compensation stated in the table of benefits. toes (a) All-one foot 25% (b) Great toe-two phalanges 10% (c) Great toe-one phalanx 10% (d) Other than great toe, each toe 2% 17 Fractured leg or patella with established non-union 20% 18 Shortening CONDITIONS:- a. The total compensation payable in respect of any disabilities due to the same injury is arrived at by adding together the various percentages but shall not exceed 150% of the capital sum insured and there shall be no further liability under the Policy in respect of the same Insured Person for Accidental Bodily Injury sustained thereafter; b. The Company shall in its absolute discretion determine the percentage payable for any Permanent Disablement not otherwise provided for under Items 1 to 18 inclusive; c. The Company shall in its absolute discretion determine the percentage payable not otherwise provided for under Item 19; d. In case where the Insured Person is left-handed, the compensation percentage in items 12 to 15 shall be reversed whereby the greater compensation percentage shall apply to the left hand and parts thereof. SPECIMEN Appendix 9B PERSONAL ACCIDENT & MEDICAL EXPENSES CLAIM FORM The Insured is requested to state as fully and as accurately as possible the information asked for hereunder in order to expedite claim processing and to return the form immediately to the Company.
